## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Personal Protective Equipment (PPE). List four categories of PPE with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) refers to the specialized clothing, accessories, or gear worn by workers to protect against specific workplace hazards. It is the **last line of defense** in the hierarchy of controls (after elimination, substitution, engineering, and administrative controls).

**Four Categories of PPE:**

1. **Head Protection** – e.g., Hard Hat (Type I, Class E)
2. **Eye Protection** – e.g., Safety Goggles (ANSI Z87.1)
3. **Respiratory Protection** – e.g., N95 Particulate Respirator
4. **Foot Protection** – e.g., Steel-toe Safety Boots

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**What is the Assigned Protection Factor (APF) of a respirator? Mention the APF value for a full-facepiece air-purifying respirator.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Assigned Protection Factor (APF)** is the ratio of the concentration of a hazardous substance in the ambient atmosphere to the concentration inside the respirator facepiece. It indicates the level of protection the respirator provides when used correctly.

$$
\text{APF} = \frac{C_{outside}}{C_{inside}}
$$

**APF for Full-Facepiece Air-Purifying Respirator (APR) = 50** (per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134).

This means the respirator can be used in atmospheres where the contaminant concentration is up to 50 times the Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3 (a).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(7 Marks)** Explain in detail the different types of respirators used in industries. State the conditions under which each type is selected.

**Model Answer:**

Respirators are classified into two main categories based on the source of breathing air:

### **1. Air-Purifying Respirators (APR)**
These respirators clean contaminated air using filters or cartridges. They are used **only in atmospheres with adequate oxygen (≥ 19.5%)** and known contaminants.

**Types:**
- **Particulate Respirators (Filtering Facepieces):**
  - N95 (filters 95% of 0.3 µm particles)
  - N99 (99%), N100 (99.97%)
  - P100 (oil-resistant, 99.97%)
  - Use: Dust, mist, fumes (silica, lead, asbestos)
- **Chemical Cartridge Respirators:**
  - Organic vapor cartridges (for solvents, VOCs)
  - Acid gas cartridges (for SO₂, HCl)
  - Combination cartridges (for mixed atmospheres)
  - Use: Paint spraying, chemical handling

**Half-Life Formula for cartridge change-out:**

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{k} \text{ (for first-order breakthrough kinetics)}
$$

### **2. Supplied-Air Respirators (SAR)**
Air is supplied from a clean source (compressor or cylinder) through a hose.

- **Type CE (Constant Flow):** Continuous flow of grade D air.
- **Pressure-Demand:** Air supplied on demand; maintains positive pressure inside facepiece.
- **Use:** Confined spaces, spray painting, abrasive blasting.

**Grade D Air Requirements (per ANSI/CGA G-7.1):**
- Oxygen: 19.5–23.5%
- Hydrocarbons (condensed): ≤ 5 mg/m³
- CO: ≤ 10 ppm
- CO₂: ≤ 1,000 ppm

### **3. Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA)**
The worker carries their own air supply in a high-pressure cylinder.

- **Open-Circuit SCBA:** Exhaled air released to atmosphere. Duration 30–60 min.
- **Closed-Circuit SCBA (rebreather):** Exhaled CO₂ absorbed, O₂ replenished. Duration 2–4 hours.
- **Use:** Firefighting, IDLH atmospheres, hazardous materials response.

**Selection Criteria (Selection Logic):**

| Condition | Respirator Type |
|---|---|
| Oxygen ≥ 19.5%, known gas/vapor | Air-purifying with cartridge |
| Oxygen ≥ 19.5%, unknown contaminant | SCBA |
| Oxygen < 19.5% | SCBA (mandatory) |
| IDLH atmosphere | Pressure-demand SCBA |
| Skin absorption of toxin | SCBA + chemical suit |

> **Conclusion:** Respirator selection follows a strict decision logic based on **oxygen concentration**, **contaminant type**, **concentration**, and **IDLH status**.

---

**Q3 (b).** `(7 Marks)` A worker is exposed to noise levels in three different work areas during an 8-hour shift:
- Area A: 95 dB(A) for 2 hours
- Area B: 90 dB(A) for 3 hours
- Area C: 88 dB(A) for 3 hours

The worker uses earplugs with NRR = 27 dB. Determine:
(i) The unprotected TWA exposure
(ii) The protected TWA exposure using OSHA method
(iii) Whether hearing protection is adequate (PEL = 85 dB(A))

**Model Answer:**

### **(i) Unprotected TWA Calculation**

TWA in dB(A) cannot be averaged linearly. We convert dB to sound intensity and average:

$$
L_{TWA} = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{8} \sum (10^{L_i/10} \times T_i) \right]
$$

**Step 1:** Convert each dB value to relative sound power:

$$
10^{95/10} = 10^{9.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
10^{90/10} = 10^{9.0} = 1.0 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
10^{88/10} = 10^{8.8} = 6.31 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by time and sum:

$$
\text{Sum} = (3.162 \times 10^{9})(2) + (1.0 \times 10^{9})(3) + (6.31 \times 10^{8})(3)
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 6.324 \times 10^{9} + 3.0 \times 10^{9} + 1.893 \times 10^{9} = 1.122 \times 10^{10}
$$

**Step 3:** Average over 8 hours:

$$
\text{Average} = \frac{1.122 \times 10^{10}}{8} = 1.4025 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert back to dB:

$$
L_{TWA} = 10 \log_{10}(1.4025 \times 10^{9}) = 10 \times 9.147 = 91.47 \text{ dB(A)}
$$

> **Unprotected TWA = 91.5 dB(A)**

### **(ii) Protected TWA (OSHA Method)**

**Step 1:** Derate NRR by 7 dB:

$$
\text{NRR}_{\text{derated}} = 27 - 7 = 20 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply 50% OSHA correction:

$$
\text{NRR}_{\text{effective}} = 20 \times 0.5 = 10 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract from unprotected TWA:

$$
L_{\text{protected}} = 91.5 - 10 = 81.5 \text{ dB(A)}
$$

> **Protected TWA = 81.5 dB(A)**

### **(iii) Adequacy Check**

$$
81.5 \text{ dB(A)} < 85 \text{ dB(A)} \text{ (PEL)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Hearing protection is **adequate**. The protected exposure is within OSHA's permissible limit. However, audiometric testing and training are still required per 29 CFR 1910.95.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4 (a).** `(7 Marks)` Classify the various types of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) used in industries. Explain in detail the standards governing head, eye, and foot protection.

**Model Answer:**

### **Classification of PPE**

PPE is classified based on the body part protected:

1. **Head Protection** – Helmets, hard hats, bump caps
2. **Eye and Face Protection** – Goggles, face shields, welding helmets
3. **Hearing Protection** – Earplugs, earmuffs
4. **Respiratory Protection** – APR, SAR, SCBA
5. **Hand and Arm Protection** – Gloves (chemical, cut, heat)
6. **Foot Protection** – Safety shoes, boots, gumboots
7. **Body Protection** – Coveralls, FR clothing, high-visibility vests
8. **Fall Protection** – Harnesses, lanyards, lifelines

### **Standards Governing PPE**

#### **A. Head Protection Standards**

**ANSI Z89.1-2014 (American)** and **IS 2925:1984 (Indian)** classify hard hats as:

- **Type I:** Vertical impact (objects falling from above)
- **Type II:** Vertical + lateral impact (sideways blows)
- **Class G (General):** Electrical insulation up to 2,200 V
- **Class E (Electrical):** Electrical insulation up to 20,000 V
- **Class C (Conductive):** No electrical protection; used where static charge must be avoided

**Marking:** Each helmet must bear manufacturer's mark, ANSI/IS class designation, and date of manufacture (replace within 5 years).

#### **B. Eye and Face Protection Standards**

**ANSI Z87.1-2020** and **IS 5983:1980** classify protective eyewear by:

- **Impact Rating:** "Z87+" (high impact), "Z87" (basic)
- **H (Hazard) marking:** Specific hazards
  - H1–H4: Optical radiation
  - H5–H6: Glare
  - H7–H9: Heat
  - H10: Splash
  - H11–H14: Chemical, dust, gas
- **Welding Shade Numbers:**
  - Gas welding: Shade 4–6
  - Light cutting: Shade 3–4
  - SMAW (stick): Shade 9–13

#### **C. Foot Protection Standards**

**ANSI Z41 / ASTM F2413** and **IS 15298** classify safety footwear:

- **Toe Impact Rating:** I-50 (50 ft-lbs impact), I-75 (75 ft-lbs)
- **Compression Rating:** C-50, C-75
- **Electrical Hazard (EH):** Dielectric to 18,000 V
- **Static Dissipative (SD):** Resistance 1×10⁶ to 1×10⁹ Ω
- **Conductive (CD):** Resistance < 1×10⁵ Ω
- **Puncture Resistance:** Steel midsole (PR) or composite (CPR)

> **Conclusion:** A complete PPE program must align with national (IS) and international (ANSI/EN) standards to ensure worker safety, regulatory compliance, and insurance validity.

---

**Q4 (b).** `(7 Marks)` A construction worker is using a 1.5 m lanyard with shock absorber. The harness stretch is 0.3 m, deceleration distance is 1.07 m, and the worker's height is 1.8 m. Calculate the total fall clearance required. If the anchor is only 4 m above the lower level, suggest three engineering alternatives.

**Model Answer:**

### **Fall Clearance Calculation**

Using the OSHA 1926.502 formula:

$$
\text{FC} = L_l + L_d + L_h + L_w + S
$$

Where:
- $L_l$ (lanyard) = 1.5 m
- $L_d$ (deceleration) = 1.07 m
- $L_h$ (harness stretch) = 0.3 m
- $L_w$ (worker height) = 1.8 m
- $S$ (safety margin) = 0.91 m

**Substituting:**

$$
\text{FC} = 1.5 + 1.07 + 0.3 + 1.8 + 0.91 = 5.58 \text{ m}
$$

> **Required Fall Clearance = 5.58 m**

### **Adequacy Check**

Anchor height available = **4 m**
Required clearance = **5.58 m**

$$
4 \text{ m} < 5.58 \text{ m} \Rightarrow \text{INADEQUATE}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The current setup is **unsafe**. The worker would strike the ground before the fall is arrested.

### **Three Engineering Alternatives**

1. **Use a Self-Retracting Lifeline (SRL):** SRL limits free fall to 0.6 m. New clearance:
$$
\text{FC}_{SRL} = 0.6 + 0.3 + 1.8 + 0.91 = 3.61 \text{ m}
$$
3.61 m < 4 m → **Adequate**

2. **Install a Higher Anchor Point:** Use a temporary horizontal lifeline (HLL) or a properly rated beam clamp/anchor strap at a height ≥ 5.6 m.

3. **Use Shorter Lanyard with Restraint System:** A 1.0 m fixed-length restraint lanyard prevents the worker from reaching the fall edge (restraint, not arrest). This eliminates fall clearance concerns entirely.

> **Recommendation:** **Adopt SRL (Alternative 1)** as the most practical and cost-effective solution while maintaining fall arrest capability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **dB Averaging:** Many students **linearly average dB values**. This is **WRONG**. dB is logarithmic; always convert to sound power (10^(L/10)), average, then convert back to dB.
> 2. **Forgetting the 7 dB NRR correction:** OSHA method requires subtracting 7 dB before the 50% factor. Skipping this yields inflated protection and wrong answers.
> 3. **Misidentifying HRC categories:** HRC 1 = 4 cal/cm², NOT 8. HRC 2 = 8 cal/cm².
> 4. **Forgetting safety margin in fall clearance:** The 0.91 m (3 ft) safety margin is mandatory per OSHA. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing APF with MUC (Maximum Use Concentration):** APF is the protection factor; MUC = APF × PEL is the maximum contaminant concentration.
> 6. **Respirator without oxygen check:** Students often suggest APR for low-oxygen environments. APR is **PROHIBITED** when O₂ < 19.5%.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **PPE = Last Line of Defense** in the hierarchy: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ✅ **Head Protection:** Type I (vertical), Type II (lateral). Class E (20 kV) for electrical work.
- ✅ **Eye Protection:** Always ANSI Z87+ marked. Use face shield **WITH** safety glasses, never alone.
- ✅ **Hearing:** TWA PEL = **85 dB(A) / 8 hrs**. OSHA formula: TWA − [(NRR − 7) × 0.5].
- ✅ **dB Math:** Never linear-average. Convert → average power → convert back.
- ✅ **Respiratory:** APR needs O₂ ≥ 19.5%; SCBA mandatory for IDLH.
- ✅ **APF values:** Half-mask APR = 10; Full-face APR = 50; SCBA = 10,000.
- ✅ **SCBA Duration:** Use Boyle's Law. Standard 30 MPa / 6.8 L cylinder ≈ 40–45 min at heavy work.
- ✅ **Hand Protection:** Match glove to chemical (use compatibility charts); nitrile for solvents, butyl for ketones.
- ✅ **Foot Protection:** Steel toe (I-75/C-75), EH-rated for electrical work (18 kV), SD for static-sensitive areas.
- ✅ **FR Clothing:** ATPV in cal/cm²; HRC 1 (4) → HRC 2 (8) → HRC 3 (25) → HRC 4 (40).
- ✅ **Fall Protection:** Anchorage ≥ **22.2 kN**; free fall ≤ 1.8 m; arrest force ≤ **4 kN**.
- ✅ **Fall Clearance:** L_lanyard + 1.07 + 0.3 + 1.8 + 0.91 m. **Never forget the 3 ft safety margin.**
- ✅ **Heat Stress (WBGT):** Outdoor = 0.7 Tnw + 0.2 Tg + 0.1 Tdb. Indoor = 0.7 Tnw + 0.3 Tg.
- ✅ **SRL advantage:** Limits free fall to 0.6 m, reducing required clearance.
- ✅ **Dual Hearing Protection:** NRR_combined = NRR_highest + 5 dB.
- ✅ **TWA Formula:** Σ(Cᵢ × Tᵢ) / 8 — used for chemical and dust exposures.
- ✅ **Half-life of cartridges:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693 / k$ — service-life estimation.
- ✅ **Grade D Air:** For SAR; CO ≤ 10 ppm, O₂ 19.5–23.5%, oil-free.
- ✅ **Recordkeeping:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1020 requires 30-year retention of medical/exposure records.
- ✅ **Inspection of PPE:** Pre-use inspection mandatory (harness, lanyards, SCBA).

---

> **End of Module 3 — Topic: Equipment Used | KTU PEIET754 | 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ropes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET754 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ropes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ROPE (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> A **rope** is a flexible, tensile load-bearing element manufactured by twisting, braiding, or weaving natural or synthetic fibers (or metallic wires) together to form a continuous, unified strand of significant axial strength. In the context of **Industrial Safety and Material Handling**, ropes serve as one of the most fundamental and versatile lifting mediums used in cranes, hoists, slings, rigging operations, fall protection systems, and rescue equipment.

In KTU terminology, ropes are classified as **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** accessories when used for life-safety applications, and as **Material Handling Equipment (MHE)** when used for load lifting and rigging.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Muscle of the Industrial World" Analogy:**
> Imagine a human muscle — it is not a single fiber, but rather thousands of tiny fibers bundled together. Each fiber alone is weak and snaps easily, but when bundled and twisted together, they form a strong, flexible cord capable of lifting heavy weights. A rope works exactly the same way.

Think of it this way:
- A **single cotton thread** breaks under a small weight (maybe a few grams).
- But **twist 100 cotton threads together** and the resulting rope can lift kilograms!
- The twist makes the fibers grip each other through **friction**, so when you pull, ALL the fibers share the load together.

In industries, this same principle is scaled up:
- **Natural fiber ropes** (Manila, Sisal, Coir) → for light-duty, low-risk tasks.
- **Synthetic fiber ropes** (Nylon, Polyester, Polypropylene) → for chemical-resistant, marine, and high-strength applications.
- **Wire ropes** (Steel) → for heavy lifting, cranes, elevators, mining, and construction.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants and Standards:**
> - **Standard Safety Factor (SF) for Lifting Ropes: 5 to 12** (varies by application)
> - **Standard Safety Factor (SF) for Hoisting Ropes: 3.5 to 5**
> - **Standard Density of Manila Rope: 1,440 kg/m³**
> - **Standard Density of Steel Wire Rope: 7,850 kg/m³**
> - **Standard Minimum Breaking Load (MBL)** is the load at which a new, unused rope is guaranteed to fail under laboratory conditions.
> - **Working Load Limit (WLL)** = MBL ÷ Safety Factor

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The formula **WLL = MBL / SF** is one of the highest-weightage formulas in industrial safety exams. A rope's WLL is the **maximum safe working load** — never exceed this value under any operational circumstance.

---

## 1.4 Why Ropes are Critical in Industrial Safety

Ropes are considered the **"first line of defense"** in many industrial lifting and safety operations because:

1. They are the **direct load-bearing connection** between a load and a lifting device (crane, hoist, winch).
2. **Failure of a rope = catastrophic accident** — dropped loads, fallen workers, structural collapse.
3. They are governed by strict standards: **IS 2266 (Wire Ropes)**, **IS 4571 (Fiber Ropes)**, **IS 3521**, **OSHA 1926.1400 series**, and **BIS guidelines**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Many students confuse **"Breaking Strength"** with **"Working Load Limit."** The breaking strength is when the rope SNAPS. You should NEVER lift anywhere near the breaking strength. Always operate at the WLL, which is 5x to 12x LOWER than the breaking strength.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Ropes

Ropes are broadly classified into **three major categories**:

### A. Natural Fiber Ropes
| Fiber Type | Source | Key Properties | Typical Use |
|------------|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **Manila** | Abacá plant (Philippines) | Strong, flexible, good grip, **resists saltwater** | Marine, general rigging |
| **Sisal** | Agave plant (Mexico/Africa) | Stiffer than Manila, lower cost | Agriculture, light lifting |
| **Coir** | Coconut husk | Poor strength, water-resistant | Decorative, packing |
| **Cotton** | Cotton plant | Soft, low strength | Decorative, light tying |
| **Hemp** | Cannabis plant | Moderate strength | Historical, low-duty use |

### B. Synthetic Fiber Ropes
| Fiber Type | Key Properties | Typical Use |
|------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Nylon (Polyamide)** | High elasticity (~20-30%), high strength, **absorbs shock** | Towing, mooring, fall arrest |
| **Polyester (Dacron/Terylene)** | Low stretch (~5-10%), UV-resistant, abrasion-resistant | Sailing, lifting slings |
| **Polypropylene** | Floats on water, cheap, low melting point (~165°C) | Marine, light-duty rescue |
| **Polyethylene** | Very light, floats, low strength | Mooring, light utility |
| **Kevlar (Aramid)** | Extreme strength, **5x stronger than steel by weight**, heat-resistant | High-performance rigging, military |
| **Dyneema (HMPE)** | Ultra-high strength, floats, low stretch | Heavy-duty offshore, winching |

### C. Wire Ropes (Steel)
Wire ropes consist of multiple **steel wires** twisted into **strands**, which are then twisted around a **core** to form the complete rope.

---

## 2.2 Construction of Wire Ropes (Detailed)

> [!NOTE]
> **Wire Rope Anatomy:**
> A wire rope is built in three nested helical levels:
> 1. **Wire** → the smallest unit (single steel filament).
> 2. **Strand** → multiple wires twisted together.
> 3. **Rope** → multiple strands laid helically around a central core.

### Standard Construction Designations

A wire rope is described by the formula: **N × (n × d) + C**

Where:
- **N** = number of strands in the rope
- **n** = number of wires per strand
- **d** = diameter of each wire (mm)
- **C** = type of core

**Common Examples:**
- **6 × 19** → 6 strands, each with 19 wires (general purpose, good flexibility)
- **6 × 37** → 6 strands, each with 37 wires (high flexibility, smoother operation)
- **6 × 7** → 6 strands, each with 7 wires (stiff, abrasion-resistant)
- **8 × 19** → 8 strands, each with 19 wires (extraordinary flexibility, used in elevators)

### Types of Lay
| Lay Type | Description | Use Case |
|----------|------------|----------|
| **Regular Lay (RL)** | Wires in strand twisted opposite to strands in rope | Most common, resists rotation |
| **Lang's Lay (LL)** | Wires in strand twisted SAME direction as strands in rope | Better abrasion resistance, larger bearing surface |
| **Right Lay (Z-lay)** | Strands twist clockwise (Z pattern) | Most common in industry |
| **Left Lay (S-lay)** | Strands twist counterclockwise (S pattern) | Special applications |

### Types of Core
| Core Type | Description | Use Case |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **Fiber Core (FC)** | Made of sisal, hemp, or polypropylene | General use, lubricated core reduces internal friction |
| **Independent Wire Rope Core (IWRC)** | A separate wire rope as the core | Higher strength, heat-resistant |
| **Wire Strand Core (WSC)** | A single strand as core | Medium-duty applications |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE THESE:**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $WLL = \dfrac{MBL}{SF}$ | Working Load Limit = Min. Breaking Load ÷ Safety Factor | kN or kg |
| $MBL = WLL \times SF$ | Minimum Breaking Load = WLL × Safety Factor | kN or kg |
| $SF = \dfrac{MBL}{WLL}$ | Safety Factor = MBL ÷ WLL | Dimensionless |
| $D_{effective} = d \times n_{legs}$ | Effective diameter in multi-leg sling | mm |
| $T = W \times \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta}$ | Tension in sling leg at angle θ from vertical | N |
| $L_{actual} = \dfrac{L_{horizontal}}{\sin\theta}$ | Actual sling length vs horizontal span | m |
| $\eta_{rope} = \dfrac{WLL_{practical}}{MBL_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$ | Rope efficiency (typically **80-90%**) | % |

### Standard Safety Factors (Memorize for KTU)

| Application | Safety Factor |
|-------------|---------------|
| **Standing Ropes (guys, supports)** | **3.5** |
| **Hoisting Ropes (general)** | **5.0** |
| **Hoisting Ropes (personnel lift)** | **10–12** |
| **Slings (general)** | **5.0** |
| **Life Safety / Fall Arrest** | **10–15** |
| **Towing Lines** | **3.0** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical:** When a rope is used to lift **human beings** (e.g., personnel baskets on cranes), the safety factor MUST be **at least 10**, per OSHA and most international standards. This is a frequent KTU exam question.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wire ropes are the **backbone of vertical material movement** in:
- **Tower cranes** in skyscraper construction
- **Mining cage hoists** in deep shafts
- **Aerial tramways** and cable cars
- **Ship mooring and offshore drilling** platforms
- **Aircraft carrier arresting gear**
- **Bridges suspension cables**

Synthetic fiber ropes are critical in:
- **Fire rescue** (Kernmantle ropes for rappelling)
- **Confined space entry** (life lines)
- **Marine** (mooring, towing)
- **Arborist work** (tree climbing)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sling Tension Under Inclined Load

> **Problem Context:** A load of W = 1000 kg is lifted using a two-leg sling where each leg makes an angle θ with the vertical. Find the tension in each leg.

### Given:
- Load W = 1000 kg
- Mass → Force: F = W × g = 1000 × 9.81 N (assume g = 9.81 m/s²)
- Angle with vertical: θ = 30°

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Identify the forces.**
The two sling legs form a symmetric V-shape. Each leg carries an equal share of the vertical load due to symmetry.

**Step 2: Apply equilibrium at the load attachment point.**

Sum of vertical forces must equal the load:

$$2 \cdot T \cdot \cos\theta = W$$

Where T is the tension in each leg, and cos θ is the vertical component of each leg's tension.

**Step 3: Solve for T.**

$$T = \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot \cos\theta}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the values.**

$$T = \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81}{2 \cdot \cos 30°}$$

$$\cos 30° = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

**Step 5: Final evaluation.**

$$T = \dfrac{9810}{2 \times 0.866} = \dfrac{9810}{1.732}$$

$$T \approx 5664.6 \text{ N} \approx 5.66 \text{ kN}$$

### Interpretation:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter?** Notice that as the sling angle becomes more horizontal (θ → 90°), cos θ → 0, so tension T → ∞. This is why **slings should always be as vertical as possible**. A 60° sling leg carries **double** the load of a vertical sling. A nearly horizontal sling can exert **tens of times** the load weight — a common cause of sling failure!

### Worked Numerical Example — Vertical Component Loading Table:

| Angle from Vertical (θ) | cos θ | T per leg (× W/2) | Multiplier on load |
|:----------------------:|:-----:|:-----------------:|:------------------:|
| 0° (vertical) | 1.000 | 1.00 W/2 | 1.0× |
| 15° | 0.966 | 1.04 W/2 | 1.04× |
| 30° | 0.866 | 1.15 W/2 | 1.15× |
| 45° | 0.707 | 1.41 W/2 | 1.41× |
| 60° | 0.500 | 2.00 W/2 | 2.00× |
| 75° | 0.259 | 3.86 W/2 | 3.86× |
| 89° (nearly flat) | 0.017 | 58.6 W/2 | 58.6× |

> [!WARNING]
> **Practical Rule (BIS 3930 & OSHA):** Never use sling legs at an angle less than 30° from horizontal (i.e., > 60° from vertical). This is a critical KTU pitfall.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Rope Efficiency Calculation

### Given:
- A wire rope has 6 strands of 19 wires each.
- Wire diameter d = 1.0 mm.
- Steel tensile strength σ = 1,800 MPa.
- Wire material factor k = 0.85 (accounts for non-uniform loading).

### Step 1: Cross-sectional area of one wire.

$$A_{wire} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (1.0)^2}{4} = 0.785 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 2: Total number of wires.

$$N_{wires} = 6 \times 19 = 114 \text{ wires}$$

### Step 3: Total metallic cross-sectional area.

$$A_{total} = N_{wires} \times A_{wire} = 114 \times 0.785 = 89.49 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 4: Theoretical breaking load (if all wires shared equally).

$$F_{theoretical} = \sigma \times A_{total} = 1800 \times 89.49 = 161{,}082 \text{ N} \approx 161.1 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 5: Apply wire material factor for actual breaking load.

$$MBL = k \times F_{theoretical} = 0.85 \times 161.1 = 136.9 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 6: Calculate rope efficiency.

$$\eta = \dfrac{MBL_{actual}}{F_{theoretical}} \times 100\% = 0.85 \times 100\% = 85\%$$

### Interpretation:

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is efficiency less than 100%?** When wires are twisted into strands and strands into ropes, they don't all share the load equally. The outer wires carry more load than the inner wires. Additionally, the helical geometry introduces torsional stresses. Hence, a rope is always **weaker** than the sum of its individual wires' straight-pull strength.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Rope

| Criteria | Natural Fiber | Synthetic Fiber | Wire Rope |
|----------|:-------------:|:---------------:|:---------:|
| **Strength** | Low-Medium | Medium-High | **Very High** |
| **Flexibility** | High | High | Medium |
| **Weight** | Medium | Light | Heavy |
| **Cost** | Medium | Low-Medium | High |
| **Heat Resistance** | Low (burns) | Low (melts) | **High (up to 400°C)** |
| **Chemical Resistance** | Low | High | Medium |
| **UV Resistance** | Medium | Variable | High |
| **Shock Absorption** | Medium | **High (Nylon)** | Low |
| **Lifespan** | 1-3 years | 3-5 years | **5-15 years** |
| **Inspection Ease** | Visual only | Visual | **Magnetic, Eddy Current, Visual** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics


---

## 4.3 Rope Failure Modes — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    F["ROPE FAILURE MODES"] --> F1["MECHANICAL FAILURE"]
    F --> F2["CHEMICAL FAILURE"]
    F --> F3["THERMAL FAILURE"]

    F1 --> F1a["Abrasion - Surface Wear"]
    F1 --> F1b["Cut or Nick - Sharp Edge Contact"]
    F1 --> F1c["Kinking - Improper Handling"]

    F1 --> F1e["Core Failure - Loss of Support"]
    
    F2 --> F2a["Corrosion - Rust on Steel"]
    F2 --> F2b["Acid Attack - H2SO4 HCL"]
    F2 --> F3a["Alkali Attack - NaOH"]

    F3 --> F3c["Cold Embrittlement - Below -40C"]
    
    F4 --> F4a["Bending Fatigue - Repeated Pulleys"]
    F4 --> F4b["Tension Fatigue - Cyclic Loading"]
    F4 --> F4c["Rotation Fatigue - Twist Cycles"]
    
    style F fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff

    style F2 fill:#84cc16,color:#000000

```

---

## 4.4 Inspection Methodology — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    I["DAILY ROPE INSPECTION"] --> I1["Visual Check"]
    I --> I2["Tactile Check"]
    I --> I3["Dimensional Check"]
    
    I1 --> I1a["Broken Wires Count"]
    I1 --> I1b["Corrosion Patches"]
    I1 --> I1c["Kinks or Birdcages"]
    I1 --> I1d["Heat Discoloration Marks"]
    
    I2 --> I2a["Stiff Sections Detection"]
    I2 --> I2b["Soft Spots Identifying Core Damage"]
    
    I3 --> I3a["Diameter Reduction more than 10 percent"]
    I3 --> I3b["Elongation Beyond Specification"]
    
    I1a --> R["REJECT if more than 10 broken wires per lay length for 6x19 rope"]
    I1b --> R
    I1c --> R
    I3a --> R
    I3b --> R
    
    R --> ACT["IMMEDIATE REMOVAL FROM SERVICE"]
    
    style I fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style ACT fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.5 Knot Strength Comparison — Decision Matrix (ASCII Schematic)

```
KNOT STRENGTH vs STRAIGHT ROPE STRENGTH (PERCENTAGE RETAINED)

Straight Rope        100 percent  |============================
Bowline Knot          75 percent   |===================
Figure-8 Knot         80 percent   |====================
Double Fisherman       78 percent   |===================
Clove Hitch           65 percent   |===============
Sheet Bend            65 percent   |===============
Timber Hitch          60 percent   |==============
Square Knot           50 percent   |==========
Half Hitch            45 percent   |=========
OVERHAND KNOT         50 percent   |==========   AVOID FOR LOAD BEARING
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule:** Always avoid knots in the load-bearing section of any rope used for lifting. Knots reduce strength by **20–55%** and are NOT acceptable in industrial lifting operations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Minimum Breaking Load (MBL) and Working Load Limit (WLL). State the relationship between them with one industrial example.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Minimum Breaking Load (MBL)** is the minimum tensile load at which a new, unused rope is guaranteed to fail under standardized laboratory test conditions (usually under gradual, static loading). It is expressed in **kN** or **tonnes**.

> **Working Load Limit (WLL)** is the maximum load that a rope is **permitted to carry during normal service** without any risk of failure. It is also called the **Safe Working Load (SWL)** or **Rated Capacity**.

> **Relationship:**
>
> $$WLL = \dfrac{MBL}{SF}$$
>
> where SF is the **Safety Factor**.

> **Example:** A wire rope has MBL = 200 kN. For a hoisting application with SF = 5, the WLL = 200/5 = **40 kN**. This 40 kN is the maximum load the rope can safely lift in operation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Regular Lay and Lang's Lay wire ropes. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Regular Lay (RL) | Lang's Lay (LL) |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| Lay Direction | Wires in strand twisted **opposite** to strands in rope | Wires in strand twisted **same** direction as strands in rope |
| Rotation Tendency | **Less** prone to rotation under load | **More** prone to rotation/unwinding |
| Abrasion Resistance | Lower (wires exposed on surface) | **Higher** (wires laid flat on bearing surface) |
| Flexibility | **More** flexible | Stiffer |
| Wear Surface | Distributed on strand crowns | Spread along entire wire length |
| Best For | Crane hoist lines, balance | Drum-wound ropes, mining |

> **Advantage of Regular Lay:** Resists rotation — safer for free-hanging loads.
> **Advantage of Lang's Lay:** Better abrasion resistance and longer drum-rolling life.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Module 3 Choice A]`

**Question A (a) [7 marks]:**
Explain the **construction of a wire rope** with neat sketches of various constructions. Describe the different **types of cores** used in wire ropes and their functions.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of a Wire Rope:**
A wire rope is a complex mechanical assembly consisting of three hierarchical helical levels:

1. **Wires:** The fundamental building block. Cold-drawn high-carbon steel wires of diameter 0.3 mm to 3.0 mm with tensile strength ranging from **1,500 MPa to 2,400 MPa**.

2. **Strands:** Multiple wires (typically 7, 19, 37, or 61) twisted helically in one direction (the "wire lay"). The number of wires per strand determines the flexibility.

3. **Rope:** Multiple strands (usually 6 or 8) laid helically around a central core in the opposite or same direction to the strand lay.

**Standard Construction Notations:**
- **6 × 7** — 6 strands × 7 wires (stiff, abrasion-resistant)
- **6 × 19** — 6 strands × 19 wires (general purpose)
- **6 × 37** — 6 strands × 37 wires (high flexibility, smoother)
- **8 × 19** — 8 strands × 19 wires (extra-flexible, used in elevators)

**Types of Cores:**

| Core Type | Material | Function | Application |
|-----------|----------|----------|-------------|
| **Fiber Core (FC)** | Sisal, hemp, or polypropylene | Holds lubrication, cushions strands, reduces friction | General-purpose lifting, indoor cranes |
| **Independent Wire Rope Core (IWRC)** | A separate small wire rope | Provides additional strength, resists heat, supports strands under crushing | Heavy-duty cranes, mining, marine |
| **Wire Strand Core (WSC)** | A single strand | Medium strength support | Moderate-duty applications |

**Functions of the Core:**
- Maintains the **circular cross-section** of the rope under load.
- Provides a **reservoir for lubrication** that reduces internal friction.
- **Supports the strands** to prevent them from collapsing inward under radial pressure on drums.
- Adds approximately **15% additional breaking strength** (in IWRC).

**Question A (b) [7 marks]:**
A **6 × 19 wire rope** has a wire diameter of 1.2 mm and steel tensile strength of 1,750 MPa. Calculate:
(i) Total metallic cross-sectional area.
(ii) Theoretical breaking load.
(iii) Actual minimum breaking load assuming wire factor = 0.87.
(iv) WLL for a crane application with safety factor 6.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Total Metallic Cross-Sectional Area:**

Single wire area:

$$A_{wire} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (1.2)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 1.44}{4} = 1.131 \text{ mm}^2$$

Total wires in rope:

$$N = 6 \times 19 = 114$$

Total area:

$$A_{total} = N \times A_{wire} = 114 \times 1.131 = 128.93 \text{ mm}^2$$

**(ii) Theoretical Breaking Load:**

$$F_{theory} = \sigma \times A_{total} = 1750 \times 128.93 = 225{,}628 \text{ N} \approx 225.6 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Actual Minimum Breaking Load:**

$$MBL = k \times F_{theory} = 0.87 \times 225.6 = 196.3 \text{ kN}$$

**(iv) Working Load Limit:**

$$WLL = \dfrac{MBL}{SF} = \dfrac{196.3}{6} = 32.7 \text{ kN} \approx 3.34 \text{ tonnes}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A-b):**
> - Students often forget to convert **mm² to m²** or **N to kN** correctly.
> - The wire factor **k = 0.87** MUST be applied — students who skip this lose 2 marks.
> - The final answer must specify **units (kN or tonnes)** — never leave a bare number.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Module 3 Choice B]`

**Question B (a) [7 marks]:**
Discuss the **causes of deterioration** of wire ropes used in industrial cranes. List the **criteria for removal from service** as per IS 2266 / OSHA standards.

**Model Solution:**

**Causes of Deterioration:**

1. **Mechanical Wear (Abrasion):** Wires rub against sheaves, drums, or each other, progressively thinning them.

2. **Fatigue Failure:** Repeated bending over sheaves causes micro-cracks in wires, leading to eventual breakage. More severe on smaller sheaves.

3. **Corrosion:** Moisture, salt spray, and chemicals attack steel, reducing cross-section and causing pitting.

4. **Overloading:** Static or dynamic overloading beyond the WLL causes permanent deformation (stretching, necking).

5. **Heat Damage:** Friction on poorly-lubricated drums generates heat above 400°C, which anneals the steel and reduces tensile strength.

6. **Birdcaging:** Sudden release of tension causes strands to splay outward and the rope loses structural integrity.

7. **Kinking:** Improper handling causes permanent deformation in the strand geometry.

8. **Chemical Attack:** Acid or alkali exposure causes surface pitting and embrittlement.

**Criteria for Removal from Service (IS 2266 / OSHA 1926.1413):**

| Criterion | Threshold for 6×19 Rope |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| Broken wires in one rope lay length | More than **12 randomly distributed** OR **5 in one strand** |
| Reduction in rope diameter | More than **1/32 inch (0.8 mm) reduction** from nominal |
| Corrosion / pitting | Visible pitting on outer surface |
| Birdcaging | Any visible splaying of strands |
| Heat damage | Discoloration (blue/brown) of wires |
| Core failure | Soft spots, fiber core protrusion, IWRC damage |
| Kinking | Any permanent deformation |
| Loss of elasticity | Visible permanent stretch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Once ANY ONE of these criteria is met, the rope MUST be removed from service immediately.** Continued use is a violation of safety standards and may lead to catastrophic failure.

**Question B (b) [7 marks]:**
A 2-tonne load is to be lifted using a **two-leg wire rope sling**. Both legs make an angle of **45° with the vertical**. Calculate:
(i) Tension in each leg.
(ii) Whether a rope of WLL = 15 kN is safe for this application.
(iii) Maximum safe load for this sling if SF = 5.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Load W = 2 tonnes = 2000 kg
- Force W = 2000 × 9.81 = 19,620 N ≈ 19.62 kN
- Angle from vertical: θ = 45°

**(i) Tension in Each Leg:**

$$T = \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot \cos\theta} = \dfrac{19.62}{2 \cdot \cos 45°}$$

$$\cos 45° = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$$

$$T = \dfrac{19.62}{2 \times 0.707} = \dfrac{19.62}{1.414} = 13.88 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Safety Check:**

Tension per leg = 13.88 kN
Rope WLL = 15 kN

Since 13.88 kN < 15 kN, the rope **IS SAFE** for this application.

However, the **utilization ratio** is:

$$\text{Utilization} = \dfrac{13.88}{15} \times 100\% = 92.5\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Warning:** Although technically safe, a 92.5% utilization is **dangerously high**. Best practice limits utilization to **75% of WLL**. The examiner will deduct marks if the student blindly accepts "safe" without this engineering judgment.

**(iii) Maximum Safe Load for This Sling Configuration:**

Maximum tension per leg = 15 kN (the WLL limit).

Total vertical lift capacity:

$$W_{max} = 2 \cdot T_{max} \cdot \cos 45° = 2 \times 15 \times 0.707 = 21.21 \text{ kN}$$

Convert to tonnes:

$$W_{max} = \dfrac{21.21}{9.81} = 2.16 \text{ tonnes}$$

So the maximum safe load is approximately **2.16 tonnes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Even though each rope is rated for 15 kN, the actual maximum lift is only **2.16 tonnes** — NOT double the rope rating. This is the **"angle penalty"** that engineers must account for.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON PITFALLS — WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MBL and WLL** — Examiners specifically test this. Remember: WLL = MBL/SF.
> 2. **Forgetting the wire factor (k = 0.85)** in wire rope strength calculations — 2 marks lost.
> 3. **Not specifying safety factors for personnel lifting** — it MUST be 10 to 12, not 5.
> 4. **Skipping the unit conversions** in numerical problems (N to kN, mm² to m²).
> 5. **Ignoring sling angle effects** — the load multiplied by 1/cosθ is a 7-mark question if missed.
> 6. **Drawing wire rope constructions without labels** — sketch must show strands, wires, and core clearly.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY:**

- ⭐ **Rope = bundled fibers/wires** for tensile load-bearing.
- ⭐ **WLL = MBL / Safety Factor** — most important formula.
- ⭐ **Safety Factors**: Standing (3.5), Hoisting (5), Personnel (10–12).
- ⭐ **Wire rope construction**: N × (n × d) + C — e.g., 6×19, 6×37, 8×19.
- ⭐ **Three types of cores**: FC (fiber), IWRC (wire), WSC (strand).
- ⭐ **Regular Lay** = opposite twists (rotation-resistant); **Lang's Lay** = same twists (abrasion-resistant).
- ⭐ **Synthetic rope strengths**: Kevlar > Polyester > Nylon > Polypropylene.
- ⭐ **Sling angle tension**: T = W / (2 cos θ) — NEVER exceed 60° from vertical.
- ⭐ **Wire factor k ≈ 0.85** for breaking load calculations.
- ⭐ **Removal criteria**: > 12 broken wires per lay length (for 6×19), > 0.8 mm diameter reduction.
- ⭐ **Knots reduce rope strength** by 20–55%; never use knots in load-bearing sections.
- ⭐ **Inspection**: Visual + tactile + dimensional; daily before each shift.
- ⭐ **Lubrication** is essential — reduces internal friction between strands.
- ⭐ **Standards**: IS 2266 (wire rope), IS 4571 (fiber rope), OSHA 1926.1400 series.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET754 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **chain** is a flexible mechanical linkage composed of a series of interconnected rigid links, manufactured from metal, designed to transmit tensile force, lift loads, secure cargo, or transmit power in industrial applications. In the context of **Industrial Safety and Health (PEIET754)**, chains are classified as critical **lifting tackles** and **material-handling accessories** governed by standards such as **IS 2429**, **IS 6216**, **IS 2740**, and the **Factories Act 1948 (India)**, along with **ASME B30.9** internationally.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Chains form a sub-topic under "Material Handling Equipment – Hoists, Cranes, Slings, and Lifting Tackle." A clear understanding of chain types, grades, working load limits (WLL), safety factors, and inspection protocols is mandatory for both **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **paper-clip chain** you might construct as a child — you bend one link through another, and so on. Now scale that idea to industrial strength: each link is forged from heat-treated alloy steel, precisely dimensioned, and tested to hold several tonnes without breaking.

Think of a chain as a **"row of soldiers holding hands"**: each link must be as strong as the load itself, because the chain's overall strength is limited by its **weakest link**. If one link fails, the entire chain fails — and the suspended load falls catastrophically.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Safety Factor for Lifting Chains:** **4:1 to 5:1** (varies by application)
- **Standard Proof Load:** Typically **2× the Working Load Limit (WLL)**
- **Elongation Discard Limit:** **5%** of original pitch length (for Grade 80/100 alloy chains)
- **Operating Temperature Limit for Carbon Steel Chains:** **-20°C to 200°C**
- **Modulus of Elasticity of Steel Chain:** approximately **200 GPa**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition Recap:** A chain in industrial safety is a tensile load-bearing assembly whose **rated capacity** is determined by its **grade (material)**, **link diameter**, and **manufacturer's WLL chart**, *not* by its thickness alone.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Industrial Chains

Industrial chains fall into two primary families:

#### A. **By Manufacturing Method**
- **Welded Link Chain (WLC):** Made by resistance welding; common for general-purpose use.
- **Studded Chain:** Each link has a stud (crossbar) to prevent link deformation and kinking; used for high-stress lifting.
- **Forged Chain:** Each link is individually forged and heat-treated; highest strength, used in **overhead lifting slings**.

#### B. **By Function**
- **Lifting Chains (Slings):** Single, double, triple, or quadruple-leg configurations.
- **Tie-Down / Tow Chains:** Lower-grade, for cargo securing only.
- **Power Transmission Chains:** Roller chains, leaf chains, silent chains (used in machinery, not lifting).
- **Anchor / Mooring Chains:** Heavy marine-grade chains.

### 2.2 Chain Grades (KTU High-Yield Topic)

| Grade | Common Name | Material | Typical Use | Marking Color |
|:-----:|:-----------:|:--------:|:-----------:|:-------------:|
| **Grade 30** | Proof Coil | Low-carbon steel | Towing, barrier, NOT for overhead lifting | None |
| **Grade 43** | High Test | Medium-carbon steel | Tow, tie-down, logging | None |
| **Grade 70** | Transport | Carbon steel | Truck/trailer tie-down | **Yellow** |
| **Grade 80** | Alloy Lifting | Heat-treated alloy steel | **Overhead lifting slings** | **Black** |
| **Grade 100** | Premium Alloy | Alloy steel | Heavy lifting, rigging | **Green** |
| **Grade 120** | High-Performance Alloy | Alloy steel | Extreme-duty lifting | **White** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Trick Question Alert:** "Which chain grade is approved for overhead lifting of personnel or critical loads?" The answer is **Grade 80 or higher** (Black/Green/White marked). Grade 30, 43, and 70 are **NEVER** to be used for overhead lifting.

### 2.3 The Core 'Why' Behind Chain Design

1. **Why is link geometry critical?** A round, smooth, symmetrical link distributes stress evenly. Sharp bends, oblong links, or twisted links create **stress concentration points** that drastically reduce the breaking load.
2. **Why are alloy chains preferred for lifting?** Heat-treated alloy steel offers a superior **strength-to-weight ratio**, smaller diameter chains can lift heavier loads, making rigging more efficient and safer.
3. **Why does elongation matter?** Chains stretch permanently when overloaded. **Pitch elongation beyond 5%** indicates internal plastic deformation — the chain must be **withdrawn from service immediately**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Units | Purpose |
|:--------|:---------|:-----:|:--------|
| **Working Load Limit** | $WLL = \dfrac{MBL}{SF}$ | Tonnes / kg | Maximum safe working load |
| **Minimum Breaking Load** | $MBL = WLL \times SF$ | Tonnes / kg | Failure threshold load |
| **Sling Capacity (Single Leg, Vertical)** | $V = WLL_{chain} \times 1.0$ | Tonnes | Single vertical lift |
| **Sling Capacity (Two-Leg, 60° angle)** | $V = WLL_{chain} \times 1.0 \times 0.866$ | Tonnes | Two-leg choker at 60° |
| **Sling Capacity (Basket Hitch)** | $V = 2 \times WLL_{chain} \times \text{angle factor}$ | Tonnes | Basket configuration |
| **Elongation %** | $\Delta L \% = \dfrac{P_{measured} - P_{original}}{P_{original}} \times 100$ | % | Wear/stretch measurement |
| **Approximate Chain Mass** | $M = k \times d^2$ | kg/m | $d$ = link diameter (mm), $k$ ≈ 0.0222 for steel |

Where:
- $MBL$ = Minimum Breaking Load
- $SF$ = Safety Factor (typically **4** for overhead lifting)
- $P$ = Pitch (internal length) of one link

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the basis for **lifting plan calculations** on construction sites, shipyards, and heavy engineering plants. A KTU question may ask you to compute WLL given MBL, or check if a chain is safe for a given load and sling angle.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Application

Chains are essential in:
- **Construction:** Hoisting steel beams, concrete buckets, rebar cages.
- **Shipyards:** Launching vessel sections, securing propellers.
- **Mining:** Hoisting ore buckets, anchoring heavy machinery.
- **Heavy Manufacturing:** Moving dies, forgings, turbine rotors.
- **Power Plants:** Lifting turbine/generator components during maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sling Load Capacity at an Inclined Angle

When a chain sling is used at an angle $\theta$ from the vertical, the **effective vertical lift component** is reduced because some of the tension is "wasted" as horizontal pull.

**Given:**
- Single chain leg WLL = 1.5 tonnes
- Sling angle = $\theta$ = 60° from vertical

**To Find:** Total vertical lift capacity of a two-leg sling.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The tension $T$ in each leg produces a vertical lifting component equal to $T \cos\theta$. The total vertical capacity of a two-leg sling is therefore the sum of the vertical components of both legs.

$$T_{total} = 2 \times T_{leg} \times \cos\theta$$

For a 60° sling angle from vertical:

$$\cos(60°) = 0.5$$

Substituting:

$$T_{total} = 2 \times 1.5 \times 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ tonnes}$$

So, even though each chain can lift 1.5 tonnes vertically, when the sling is at 60° from vertical (a common rigging angle), the combined capacity drops because of the **angle inefficiency**.

**Note:** In rigging practice, the **sling angle from horizontal** is more commonly referenced. The formula in industry charts is often stated as:

$$V = WLL_{chain} \times \sin(\alpha)$$

where $\alpha$ is the **sling angle measured from horizontal**. For a 60° horizontal angle:

$$V = 1.5 \times \sin(60°) = 1.5 \times 0.866 = 1.299 \text{ tonnes per leg}$$

For a two-leg sling:

$$V_{total} = 2 \times 1.299 = 2.598 \text{ tonnes}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Working Load Limit from Minimum Breaking Load

**Given:**
- Chain Grade 80, link diameter $d = 16$ mm
- Manufacturer-stated MBL = 8 tonnes
- Application: Overhead lifting
- Required Safety Factor (SF) = 4

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

The relationship between WLL, MBL, and SF is:

$$WLL = \dfrac{MBL}{SF}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$WLL = \dfrac{8}{4} = 2 \text{ tonnes}$$

Therefore, the chain can safely lift up to **2 tonnes** under normal service conditions. Beyond 2 tonnes, the chain enters the **yield zone** and may permanently deform.

**Interpretation:** The safety factor of 4 means the chain will not fail until the load reaches 4× the working limit (8 tonnes). This margin accounts for **dynamic loads, shock loading, wear, corrosion, and human error**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Elongation Check for Chain Discard Decision

**Given:**
- Original chain pitch (5 links) $P_{original}$ = 250 mm
- After 2 years of service, measured pitch (5 links) $P_{measured}$ = 258 mm

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

The elongation percentage is calculated as:

$$\Delta L \% = \dfrac{P_{measured} - P_{original}}{P_{original}} \times 100$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta L \% = \dfrac{258 - 250}{250} \times 100$$

$$\Delta L \% = \dfrac{8}{250} \times 100 = 3.2\%$$

**Decision:** Since 3.2% < 5% (the discard threshold for Grade 80 chains), the chain is **still serviceable**, but it should be **closely monitored** and rechecked at the next inspection cycle. If the elongation had exceeded **5%**, the chain would have to be **withdrawn from service immediately**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

| Scenario | Variables Given | Formula to Use | Final Output |
|:---------|:----------------|:---------------|:-------------|
| Find WLL | MBL = 10 t, SF = 4 | $WLL = MBL/SF$ | 2.5 t |
| Find MBL | WLL = 1 t, SF = 5 | $MBL = WLL \times SF$ | 5 t |
| Check safety | Load = 2 t, chain WLL = 1.5 t | $Load \leq WLL$ | **Unsafe — DO NOT LIFT** |
| Sling angle | WLL = 1 t, angle 30° horiz. | $V = 1 \times \sin(30°)$ | 0.5 t per leg |
| Elongation | 5-link pitch grew from 200 mm to 211 mm | $\Delta L\% = (11/200)\times 100$ | 5.5% — **DISCARD** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strictly No Step-Skipping:** Every numerical KTU question requires you to (1) state the given data, (2) write the formula, (3) substitute, (4) compute, and (5) state the engineering decision. Examiners award **partial marks** for each correct step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between Grade 30 (Proof Coil) and Grade 80 chain. Which one is permitted for overhead lifting applications?

**Model Answer:**
**Grade 30 (Proof Coil)** is made of low-carbon steel, has lower tensile strength, and is intended for general-purpose use such as towing, log chaining, and barrier applications. It is **NOT** approved for overhead lifting.

**Grade 80 chain** is manufactured from quenched-and-tempered alloy steel, has significantly higher strength-to-weight ratio, and is specifically **approved for overhead lifting operations** as sling chains, hoisting chains, and rigging assemblies.

**Identification markings:** Grade 30 has no color code; Grade 80 chains are marked in **black**. Only **Grade 80 and above** (Black, Green, White) may be used for overhead lifting in compliance with **ASME B30.9** and **IS 6216**.

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** Define Working Load Limit (WLL) and Safety Factor for industrial chains. State the standard safety factor for overhead lifting.

**Model Answer:**
**Working Load Limit (WLL)** is the maximum load that a chain or sling is authorized to support under ideal service conditions. It is also known as the **Rated Capacity** or **Safe Working Load (SWL)**.

**Safety Factor (SF)** is the ratio of the chain's minimum breaking load to its working load limit, representing the margin of safety against unexpected overload, dynamic forces, wear, and shock.

$$WLL = \dfrac{MBL}{SF}$$

**Standard Safety Factor for Overhead Lifting:** **4:1** (i.e., the chain will not fail until four times the rated working load is applied).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**a)** Classify industrial chains based on manufacturing and function. Describe the **material, grade markings, and typical applications** of Grade 30, Grade 70, and Grade 80 chains. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A Grade 80 alloy chain sling has a **minimum breaking load of 12 tonnes** and is used in a **two-leg bridle configuration at 60° from horizontal**. Compute the **WLL per leg** and the **total safe lifting capacity** of the sling. Take **SF = 4**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Chain Classification and Grade Details:**

**By Manufacturing Method:**
- Welded link chains, Studded chains, Forged chains.

**By Function:**
- Lifting chains, Tow/tie-down chains, Power transmission chains, Mooring chains.

**Detailed Grade Table:**

| Grade | Material | Marking | Application |
|:-----:|:--------|:-------:|:-----------|
| **30** | Low-carbon steel | No color | Towing, barriers, general utility (NOT for lifting) |
| **70** | Carbon steel | Yellow | Truck and trailer tie-down, cargo securement |
| **80** | Quenched & tempered alloy steel | Black | Overhead lifting slings, rigging, hoist chains |

Grade 80 chains have a **higher carbon and alloy content** (typically Cr-Ni-Mo), undergo **heat treatment** for superior strength, and provide approximately **50% higher strength** than Grade 30 of the same diameter.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Minimum Breaking Load (MBL) = 12 tonnes
- Two-leg bridle sling at 60° from horizontal
- Safety Factor (SF) = 4

**Step 1:** Calculate WLL per chain leg (vertical capacity).

$$WLL = \dfrac{MBL}{SF} = \dfrac{12}{4} = 3 \text{ tonnes per leg}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the effective capacity per leg at 60° from horizontal.

$$V_{leg} = WLL \times \sin(60°) = 3 \times 0.866 = 2.598 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate total sling capacity (two legs).

$$V_{total} = 2 \times V_{leg} = 2 \times 2.598 = 5.196 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Engineering Decision:** The two-leg bridle sling can safely lift up to **5.196 tonnes** when rigged at 60° from horizontal. Any load exceeding this value must NOT be lifted with this configuration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**a)** Explain the **inspection procedures** for industrial chains used in lifting operations. List **five discard criteria** that mandate immediate withdrawal from service. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A chain originally measuring **200 mm across 5 links** is found to measure **212 mm after two years** of service. Determine the **elongation percentage** and recommend whether the chain should be **retained or discarded**. Justify your answer with reference to standard thresholds. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Chain Inspection & Discard Criteria:**

**Inspection Procedure:**
1. **Visual Inspection (Daily / Pre-shift):** Check for nicks, gouges, cuts, corrosion, discoloration, and bent or twisted links.
2. **Dimensional Check (Periodic / Monthly):** Measure pitch across 5 consecutive links; record in the lifting tackle register.
3. **Diameter Check:** Use calipers to verify wire diameter has not reduced by more than **10%** of original.
4. **Load Testing (Annual):** Proof load test to 2× WLL where required.
5. **NDT (Non-Destructive Testing):** Magnetic particle or dye-penetrant testing for chains in critical service or after suspected overload.

**Five Mandatory Discard Criteria:**
1. **Elongation exceeding 5%** of original pitch length.
2. **Wire diameter reduction exceeding 10%** of original.
3. **Visible cracks, nicks, or gouges** deeper than 10% of wire diameter.
4. **Heat damage** (indicated by blue-black discoloration, loss of temper).
5. **Severe corrosion, pitting, or chain links that are twisted, bent, or stretched out of shape.**

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Original pitch (5 links): $P_{original} = 200$ mm
- Measured pitch (5 links): $P_{measured} = 212$ mm

**Step 1:** Apply the elongation formula.

$$\Delta L \% = \dfrac{P_{measured} - P_{original}}{P_{original}} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$\Delta L \% = \dfrac{212 - 200}{200} \times 100$$

$$\Delta L \% = \dfrac{12}{200} \times 100 = 6\%$$

**Step 3:** Compare with the standard threshold.

Standard discard threshold for alloy lifting chains = **5%**

Since 6% > 5%, the chain has **exceeded the allowable elongation limit**.

**Engineering Decision:** The chain must be **immediately withdrawn from service and discarded**. Continued use would risk sudden failure under load, as the elongation indicates **permanent plastic deformation** in the link material, drastically reducing the remaining breaking strength.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse sling angle from vertical with angle from horizontal.** Examiners commonly test this: angle 30° from vertical = 60° from horizontal. The trigonometric function differs (sin vs. cos).
> 2. **Never forget to multiply by the number of legs** in multi-leg sling calculations.
> 3. **Do not apply Grade 30 or 70 chains for overhead lifting** in numerical examples — this is an automatic mark-loss error.
> 4. **Always state the safety factor** (typically 4 for lifting) in your solution, even if not explicitly asked.
> 5. **For elongation problems, always specify which grade of chain** the 5% threshold applies to (alloy lifting chains). Proof coil chains have a different (lower) threshold of about 3%.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Chain = a series of interlinked metal rings** used for lifting, towing, securing, or transmitting power.
- **Six major grades** exist: 30, 43, 70, 80, 100, 120 — only **Grade 80 and above** for overhead lifting.
- **Color codes are mandatory:** Yellow = Grade 70, **Black = Grade 80**, **Green = Grade 100**, **White = Grade 120**.
- **WLL = MBL / Safety Factor**; standard SF for lifting = **4:1**.
- **Sling capacity depends on angle:** $V = WLL \times \sin(\alpha)$ where $\alpha$ is the angle from horizontal.
- **Elongation discard limit = 5%** of original pitch for alloy chains.
- **Wire diameter reduction limit = 10%** of original.
- **Heat damage, cracks, severe corrosion** = mandatory discard regardless of dimensions.
- **Always record inspection data** in the **Lifting Tackle Register** as required by the **Factories Act 1948**.
- **Studded chains** resist deformation and kinking; **forged chains** offer the highest strength.
- **Power transmission chains** (roller, leaf) are **NOT** used for lifting — different application entirely.
- **Sling configurations:** Single-leg, two-leg bridle, three-leg, four-leg, basket hitch, choker hitch — each has a different capacity multiplier.
- **Side-loading and shock loading** drastically reduce effective capacity and must be avoided.
- **NDT methods:** Magnetic particle testing (MPT) and dye-penetrant testing (DPT) for critical chains.
- **Relevant Indian Standards:** IS 2429 (round steel chains), IS 6216 (slings), IS 2740 (chain slings), IS 8759 (use of steel chains).
- **International standards:** ASME B30.9 (Slings), OSHA 1910.184 (Slings).
- **Chain failures originate from:** wear, overloading, heat exposure, corrosion, side-loading, and improper rigging.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET754 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Slings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SLING (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **sling** is an assembly composed of one or more lengths of rope, chain, wire rope, or synthetic webbing, terminated at each end with fittings (hooks, rings, links, or shackles), used in conjunction with a crane, hoist, or other lifting device to support and transport a load during material-handling operations.

In the framework of **Industrial Safety and Health (PEIET754)**, slings form a critical subset of **lifting accessories (loose gear)** governed by international codes such as **ASME B30.9 (Slings)**, **IS 3832 / IS 6606 (Indian Standards for slings)**, and the **Factories Act 1948 (India)**.

## 1.2 Classification of Slings (By Material)

| Type | Construction Material | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Wire Rope Slings** | Stranded steel wire | Heavy loads, marine, construction |
| **Chain Slings** | Alloy or mild steel links | High-temperature environments, heavy forging |
| **Synthetic Web Slings** | Nylon or polyester webbing | Smooth/finished surfaces, general industry |
| **Fiber / Manila Rope Slings** | Natural or synthetic fibers | Light-duty, low-risk lifting |
| **Metal Mesh Slings** | Coiled wire mesh | Hot or abrasive loads (e.g., steel coils) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tow Rope of Industry"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of a sling as the *industrial equivalent of a luggage strap*. When you lift a suitcase by its single top handle, all the weight pulls straight down on that single point. But if you sling a strap under the suitcase and grip it with two hands forming a "V," the load is shared — yet you can *feel* the strap pulling harder on your hands as the V becomes narrower. That "pull" is the **sling tension**, and the angle of the V is the **sling angle (θ)**. In industry, when this angle gets too small, the sling can fail even if the load itself is well below the rope's breaking strength. This is the single most important safety concept in slings.

## 1.4 Primary Functions of a Sling

1. **Load Distribution** — Spreading the load across multiple legs or contact points.
2. **Reach Adaptation** — Allowing a crane hook to engage loads that cannot be hooked directly.
3. **Load Protection** — Providing a flexible interface that cradles fragile, polished, or oddly shaped objects.
4. **Force Redirection** — Converting vertical lifting force into a controlled multi-directional pull.

## 1.5 The Five Cardinal Sling Hazards

> [!WARNING]
> The KTU board examiner expects students to identify failure modes:
> - **Overloading** — Exceeding the rated capacity of the sling.
> - **Acute Sling Angles** — Tension amplifies dramatically below 60° from horizontal.
> - **Shock Loading** — Dynamic forces can multiply the static load by 1.5× to 2×.
> - **Abrasion / Cutting** — Contact with sharp edges without padding.
> - **Improper Hitch Selection** — Using a vertical hitch where a basket hitch is required.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Sling Hitches (Configurations)

A **hitch** is the geometric manner in which a sling engages the load. The rated capacity of the *same* sling changes dramatically based on the hitch.

### 2.1.1 Vertical Hitch
The simplest configuration — one end attached to the hook, the other end attached directly to the load.

$$T_{vertical} = W$$

where $W$ = Load weight, and the entire weight is borne by a single vertical leg.

### 2.1.2 Basket Hitch
The sling passes **under** the load with both ends held by the hook, forming a U-shape. Theoretically, the load is shared by two vertical legs.

$$T_{basket} = \frac{W}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basket Hitch Rule:** Always ensure the **D/d ratio** (curvature diameter of load ÷ sling diameter) is **≥ 2 for chain** and **≥ 20 for wire rope**, otherwise the sling suffers crippling bend stress.

### 2.1.3 Choker Hitch
The sling passes **around** the load and through its own end (self-tightening). Friction loss reduces effective capacity.

$$T_{choker} \approx \frac{W}{\mu \cdot n}$$

where $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at the contact point, and $n$ = number of choker wraps. Typically, choker capacity is rated at **75%** of single vertical-leg capacity in manufacturer tables.

### 2.1.4 Bridle Hitch (Multi-Leg Sling)
Two, three, or four legs joined at a master link. This is the most common industrial configuration.

$$T_{leg} = \frac{W}{n \cdot \cos\theta} \quad \text{(when not vertical)}$$

where:
- $n$ = number of supporting legs
- $\theta$ = angle of the leg from the **vertical**

## 2.2 The Sling Angle Tension Multiplier

> [!NOTE]
> **Why angle matters:** As the sling angle (measured from horizontal) decreases, each leg must carry a *greater* portion of the load because the vertical component of the leg tension shrinks.

### Tension Multiplier Table (Mode of Loading)

| Sling Angle from Horizontal (β) | Mode Factor (k) | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| **90° (Vertical)** | **1.000** | Full vertical lift — safe |
| **60°** | **1.155** | Mild horizontal component |
| **45°** | **1.414** | Textbook critical angle |
| **30°** | **2.000** | Tension = 2× load per vertical leg |
| **15°** | **3.864** | **UNSAFE — avoid in practice** |

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following equations — they appear in **every KTU board exam** under Module 3.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\beta}$ | Tension in one leg (β from horizontal) | N or kgf |
| 2 | $T_{basket} = \dfrac{W}{2}$ | Tension per leg in basket hitch | N or kgf |
| 3 | $T_{choker} = 0.75 \times T_{vertical}$ | Choker capacity (rule of thumb) | N or kgf |
| 4 | $SWL = \dfrac{Breaking\ Strength}{FOS}$ | Safe Working Load | — |
| 5 | $F_{dynamic} = F_{static} \times (1 + v \cdot k)$ | Shock loading amplification (k ≈ 0.5–1.0) | N |
| 6 | $D/d \geq 2\ (chain),\ \geq 20\ (wire)$ | Minimum bend ratio to prevent crippling | mm/mm |
| 7 | $Angle\ Factor = \dfrac{1}{\sin\beta}$ | Multiplier on load per leg | dimensionless |
| 8 | $L = 2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{\tan\beta}\right) + S$ | Sling length for basket hitch | mm |

## 2.4 Safe Working Load (SWL) & Factor of Safety (FOS)

The **Factor of Safety (FOS)** is the ratio between the *Minimum Breaking Strength* of the sling material and its *rated working load*.

$$SWL = \frac{Minimum\ Breaking\ Strength}{FOS}$$

### Standard FOS Values (Per IS / ASME Practice)

| Sling Type | Minimum FOS |
|---|---|
| **Wire Rope** | **5.0** |
| **Alloy Chain** | **4.0** |
| **Synthetic Web** | **5.0 (Nylon) / 7.0 (Polyester)** |
| **Manila Rope** | **5.0** |

## 2.5 Shock Loading (Dynamic Amplification)

When a load is **suddenly picked up**, **jerked**, or **snatched**, the dynamic force can be 1.5× to 2× the static weight.

$$F_{dynamic} = W \cdot (1 + \text{shock factor})$$

| Lifting Condition | Shock Factor |
|---|---|
| Smooth, slow hoist | **0.0 – 0.1** |
| Normal speed, careful operation | **0.1 – 0.3** |
| Sudden pickup, jerky motion | **0.5 – 1.0** |
| Snatch / free fall pickup | **≥ 1.0 (Danger)** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Sling Leg Tension in a Bridle Hitch

**Problem Setup:** A load of weight $W$ is supported by a sling with two legs forming an angle $\beta$ with the **horizontal**. Find the tension in each leg.

### Step 1: Free Body Diagram Setup

Consider a single leg of the bridle. The tension $T$ acts along the leg, at angle $\beta$ above horizontal. The vertical component of tension must support its share of the load.

### Step 2: Vertical Equilibrium

For a symmetric two-leg sling, each leg carries half the weight vertically:

$$T \cdot \sin\beta = \frac{W}{2}$$

### Step 3: Solve for Tension

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \sin\beta}$$

### Step 4: Generalization for n Legs

$$T = \frac{W}{n \cdot \sin\beta}$$

where $n$ = number of legs, $\beta$ = angle from horizontal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When $\beta = 90°$ (vertical), $\sin\beta = 1$, and $T = W/n$ — the minimum possible tension. As $\beta \to 0°$, $\sin\beta \to 0$, and $T \to \infty$ — the sling fails. **This is why KTU mandates $\beta \geq 30°$ at minimum, with $60°$ recommended.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

### Problem (Part B – 14 Marks Standard):

> A steel block weighing **5,000 kgf** is to be lifted using a **3-leg wire rope sling**. Each leg makes an angle of **45°** with the horizontal. Determine:
> (a) The tension in each leg.
> (b) Whether a sling rated at SWL = **2,500 kgf per leg** is safe.
> (c) The minimum angle at which the rated sling can be used safely.

### Part (a) — Tension in Each Leg

**Given:**
- Load $W = 5{,}000$ kgf
- Number of legs $n = 3$
- Angle $\beta = 45°$

**Formula:**

$$T = \frac{W}{n \cdot \sin\beta}$$

**Substitution:**

$$T = \frac{5{,}000}{3 \cdot \sin(45°)}$$

$$T = \frac{5{,}000}{3 \cdot 0.7071}$$

$$T = \frac{5{,}000}{2.1213}$$

$$\boxed{T = 2{,}357.02\ \text{kgf per leg}}$$

### Part (b) — Safety Check

Comparing actual tension with rated SWL:

$$T_{actual} = 2{,}357.02\ \text{kgf}$$

$$SWL_{rated} = 2{,}500\ \text{kgf per leg}$$

Since $T_{actual} < SWL_{rated}$ (i.e., $2{,}357.02 < 2{,}500$):

$$\boxed{\text{The sling IS SAFE for use at } 45° \text{ angle.}}$$

### Part (c) — Minimum Safe Angle

For the sling to remain safe, the tension per leg must NOT exceed 2,500 kgf:

$$T \leq SWL$$

$$\frac{W}{n \cdot \sin\beta_{min}} \leq 2{,}500$$

$$\frac{5{,}000}{3 \cdot \sin\beta_{min}} \leq 2{,}500$$

$$\sin\beta_{min} \geq \frac{5{,}000}{3 \cdot 2{,}500}$$

$$\sin\beta_{min} \geq \frac{5{,}000}{7{,}500}$$

$$\sin\beta_{min} \geq 0.6667$$

$$\beta_{min} = \arcsin(0.6667)$$

$$\boxed{\beta_{min} = 41.81°\ \text{(approximately } 42°)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** If the sling angle falls below **41.81°** (i.e., the legs spread wider apart horizontally), the tension per leg will exceed the rated SWL and the sling is unsafe.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Sling Length for a Basket Hitch

**Problem:** A load of diameter $D$ is to be lifted in a basket hitch. The two sling legs meet at the hook at an angle $\beta$ with horizontal. Find the required sling length $L$.

### Step 1: Geometric Setup

Each leg forms a right triangle with:
- Horizontal run $= D/2$ (half the load diameter)
- Vertical height $= h$
- Hypotenuse $=$ leg length $= \ell$

### Step 2: Trigonometric Relation

$$\tan\beta = \frac{h}{D/2} = \frac{2h}{D}$$

Solving for height:

$$h = \frac{D \cdot \tan\beta}{2}$$

### Step 3: Leg Length

$$\ell = \frac{h}{\sin\beta} = \frac{D \cdot \tan\beta}{2\sin\beta} = \frac{D}{2\cos\beta}$$

### Step 4: Total Sling Length

The total length must accommodate both legs plus a sling-to-link allowance $S$:

$$L = 2\ell + S$$

$$L = \frac{D}{\cos\beta} + S$$

$$\boxed{L = \frac{D}{\cos\beta} + S}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** The $S$ term accounts for the master link, thimbles, or end fittings — typically **300 mm to 500 mm** for industrial applications.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Angle on Capacity

The following matrix quantifies the *capacity loss* of a 10-tonne SWL single-leg sling when used in a 2-leg bridle configuration.

| Sling Angle (β) | $\sin\beta$ | Tension Multiplier $\left(\frac{1}{\sin\beta}\right)$ | Effective 2-Leg SWL | Capacity Loss vs Vertical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **90°** | **1.000** | **1.000** | **5.0 tonne per leg** | **0%** |
| **75°** | **0.966** | **1.035** | **4.83 tonne per leg** | **3.4%** |
| **60°** | **0.866** | **1.155** | **4.33 tonne per leg** | **13.4%** |
| **45°** | **0.707** | **1.414** | **3.54 tonne per leg** | **29.3%** |
| **30°** | **0.500** | **2.000** | **2.50 tonne per leg** | **50.0%** |
| **15°** | **0.259** | **3.864** | **1.29 tonne per leg** | **74.1%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students frequently forget that the rated SWL on a sling tag is for **vertical hitch**. When used at an angle, the actual capacity must be **de-rated** by the multiplier.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sling Hitch Classification & Force Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sling Hitch Selection] --> B{Load Geometry?}
    B -- Vertical pull point --> C[Vertical Hitch]
    B -- Cylindrical horizontal load --> D[Basket Hitch]
    B -- Tight bundle or pipe --> E[Choker Hitch]
    B -- Multi-point balanced load --> F[Bridle Hitch]

    C --> C1[Load W = Sling Tension T]
    D --> D1[Each leg carries W divided by 2]
    E --> E1[Capacity = 0.75 x Vertical SWL]
    F --> F1[Each leg: T = W divided by n sin beta]

    F1 --> G{Sling Angle beta}
    G -- beta = 90 deg --> G1[Safe: minimum tension]
    G -- beta = 60 deg --> G2[Acceptable: 13 percent loss]
    G -- beta = 45 deg --> G3[Critical: 29 percent loss]
    G -- beta = 30 deg --> G4[Caution: 50 percent loss]
    G -- beta less than 30 deg --> G5[UNSAFE: replace sling]

    D1 --> H{D divided by d ratio check}
    H -- Chain --> H1[Minimum 2]
    H -- Wire Rope --> H2[Minimum 20]
    H -- Below minimum --> H3[Add padding or use larger sling]

    C1 --> I[Inspect sling before each lift]
    E1 --> I
    G1 --> I
    G2 --> I
    G3 --> I
    G4 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I

    I --> J[Lift permitted]
    G5 --> K[Lift REJECTED]
    H3 --> K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Bridle Hitch Force Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LoadSystem [Load and Sling System]
        direction TB
        L[Load W at center] -->|Weight acting down| RES[Resultant equilibrium point]
        L1[Left Leg Tension T1] -->|Vector at angle beta from horizontal| RES
        L2[Right Leg Tension T2] -->|Vector at angle beta from horizontal| RES
        RES -->|Vertical components balance| VEQ[2 T sin beta equals W]
    end

    VEQ --> FORM[T equals W divided by 2 sin beta]
    FORM --> NOTE[Angle beta measured from HORIZONTAL]

    style L fill:#fff4cc
    style L1 fill:#cce5ff
    style L2 fill:#cce5ff
    style RES fill:#ffcccc
    style VEQ fill:#ccffcc
    style FORM fill:#ffccff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Sling Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Lifting Operation] --> Q1{What is the load?}
    Q1 -- Smooth, polished --> SYN[Use Synthetic Web Sling]
    Q1 -- Hot above 200 deg C --> CHN[Use Alloy Chain Sling]
    Q1 -- Heavy, abrasive --> WRP[Use Wire Rope Sling]
    Q1 -- Light below 500 kg --> FBR[Use Fiber Rope Sling]

    SYN --> Q2{Edge protection present?}
    CHN --> Q3{Direct heat contact?}
    WRP --> Q4{Diameter ratio OK?}
    FBR --> Q5{Light duty only?}

    Q2 -- No --> SYN2[Add corner protectors or pads]
    Q2 -- Yes --> SYN3[Proceed]
    Q3 -- Yes --> CHN2[Use heat-resistant alloy grade]
    Q3 -- No --> CHN3[Proceed]
    Q4 -- No --> WRP2[Use larger sling diameter]
    Q4 -- Yes --> WRP3[Proceed]
    Q5 -- No --> FBR2[Reject: select heavier sling]
    Q5 -- Yes --> FBR3[Proceed]

    SYN2 --> INSPECT[Daily Inspection Checklist]
    SYN3 --> INSPECT
    CHN2 --> INSPECT
    CHN3 --> INSPECT
    WRP2 --> INSPECT
    WRP3 --> INSPECT
    FBR2 --> INSPECT
    FBR3 --> INSPECT

    INSPECT --> CHK1{Visible damage?}
    CHK1 -- Yes --> REJ[Tag Out and Remove from Service]
    CHK1 -- No --> CHK2{SWL tag legible?}
    CHK2 -- No --> REJ
    CHK2 -- Yes --> LIFT[Authorized Lift]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Each blue node (load type) routes to a material-specific check, and every path — except those marked REJ — converges at the **Daily Inspection Checklist** before a lift is authorized. This is the *Block-Level Functional Architecture* used in KTU Module 3 viva questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023])
**Define a sling. List any FOUR types of slings used in industrial material handling.**

**Model Answer:**

> A sling is a flexible load-bearing assembly of rope, chain, wire rope, or synthetic webbing, fitted with end attachments (hooks, rings, shackles), used to engage and lift a load by means of a crane or hoist.
>
> Four types of slings:
> 1. **Wire Rope Sling** — Made of stranded steel wires; used for heavy loads.
> 2. **Chain Sling** — Made of welded or forged alloy-steel links; suitable for high temperatures.
> 3. **Synthetic Web Sling** — Manufactured from nylon or polyester webbing; protects polished surfaces.
> 4. **Fiber / Manila Rope Sling** — Natural or synthetic fibers; used only for light-duty lifts.

---

### Question 2 (Conceptual — [KTU University Exam – May 2024])
**What is meant by the term "Sling Angle"? Why is an angle of less than 30° from horizontal considered unsafe?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Sling Angle (β)** is the angle that a sling leg makes with the **horizontal plane** when supporting a load.
>
> The tension in a single leg is given by $T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\beta}$. As $\beta$ decreases toward 0°, $\sin\beta$ approaches 0, causing tension $T$ to rise toward infinity. Specifically, at $\beta = 30°$, the tension per leg is already **twice** the vertical component of the load. Below 30°, the amplification factor exceeds **2.0×**, which approaches or exceeds the rated capacity of most industrial slings. Furthermore, the horizontal component of force tries to **crush** or **squeeze** the load. For these reasons, KTU practice (and ASME B30.9) mandates that the sling angle be **≥ 30°** from horizontal, with **60° or higher strongly preferred**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ❓ QUESTION A (Module 3 Choice Option 1) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **different types of slings** with their **advantages, limitations, and typical industrial applications**. (7 Marks)
