$$

For simplicity in KTU exams, we use $P_{\text{test}} = 1.30 \times \text{MAWP}$.

**Step 2 — Test Duration (ASME Sec VIII UG-99)**

$$
t_{\text{hold}} = 30 \,\text{minutes} \; \text{minimum after stabilization}
$$

**Step 3 — Acceptance Criterion**

$$
\Delta P_{\text{observed}} \leq 0 \quad \text{(no pressure drop, no visible leak, no permanent deformation)}
$$

If any of these conditions fail → vessel is **rejected**, repair redone, retest.

**Step 4 — Test Medium**

$$
\text{Medium} = \text{Water at ambient temperature} \; (\text{preferred — pneumatic is dangerous and only used if hydro impossible})
$$

Water temperature must remain **above the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** to avoid brittle fracture.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    nodeD[Step 4: Visual & Dimensional Check<br/>Defect Scoring]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Read Before Attempting):**
> 1. Always **name the standard** (e.g., IS 3177, IS 2825, Factories Act Section 28) — vague answers lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. Always mention **test load multiplier** (1.25 × SWL, 2.0 × SWL, 1.3 × MAWP) — examiners award specific marks for numbers.
> 3. Always include **frequency** (12 months / 24 months / 5 years) — partial answers without time intervals are incomplete.
> 4. **Never confuse Lifting Machine proof load (1.25 × SWL) with Lifting Tackle proof load (2.0 × SWL)** — this is the #1 mistake costing full marks.
> 5. Always state the **role of the Competent Person** and the **legal section** under Factories Act.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a "Competent Person" as per the Indian Factories Act, 1948. List any four statutory tests that require a Competent Person's certificate.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Competent Person** is one who possesses **adequate training, experience, and skill** to carry out the specific testing/examination of an item of equipment, and is **certified in writing** by the Chief Inspector of Factories (Factories Act Section 2(ca)).

**Four statutory tests requiring Competent Person certification:**
1. **Lifting Machines** (Cranes, hoists, winches) — Sec 28, every **12 months**.
2. **Lifting Tackles** (Slings, shackles, hooks) — Sec 28, every **12 months**.
3. **Pressure Vessels** — Sec 31, every **24 months**.
4. **Boilers** — Indian Boilers Act, every **12 months**.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark each × 4 = 2 marks → 3 marks total.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Distinguish between a "Pre-use Check" and a "Thorough Examination" with two points of difference.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Pre-use Check | Thorough Examination |
|---|---|---|
| **By Whom** | Operator / User | Competent Person |
| **Frequency** | Every shift / Daily | Every 12 months (or as per statute) |
| **Scope** | Visual + basic functional | Visual + dimensional + functional + load test + NDT |
| **Documentation** | Operator checklist log | Statutory Test Certificate |

*(1.5 marks for table; 1.5 marks for one-line distinction.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer **ONE** of the following (A or B). Each has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks). `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the **procedure for testing a 20-tonne EOT crane** as per IS 3177. Include pre-test preparation, load test values, and acceptance criteria. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory uses **wire rope slings of SWL 5 tonnes**. Describe the testing procedure including the proof load, inspection criteria for discard, and the documentation required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Pre-Test Preparation (2 marks)**
- Lock-Out Tag-Out (LOTO) of power supply.
- Confirm crane is on rails / supports properly; check runway.
- Calibrate dynamometer, load cell, deflection gauge.
- Issue Permit-to-Work.
- Verify operator competency.

**Step 2 — Visual & Functional Inspection (2 marks)**
- Check all welds, hooks (throat opening), wire ropes (broken wires count).
- Operate crane without load through full duty cycle — hoist, lower, traverse, slew.
- Verify brakes, limit switches, overload relay, horn, indicator lamps.

**Step 3 — Static Load Test (2 marks)**
- Apply **1.25 × SWL = 1.25 × 20 = 25 tonnes** as static load.
- Hold for **10 minutes minimum**.
- Measure deflection, observe for cracks, listen for unusual noises.

**Step 4 — Dynamic Load Test (1 mark)**
- Apply **1.5 × SWL = 30 tonnes** and operate through full cycle.
- Monitor brake holding capacity, motor temperature, vibration.

| Criterion | Acceptable Limit |
|---|---|
| Hook throat opening increase | $\leq 5\%$ of original |
| Wire rope broken wires | $\leq 10\%$ in $8d$ length |
| Deflection recovery | $\geq 95\%$ after load removal |
| Brake slip | Zero |

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Visual Inspection (2 marks)**
- Check for broken wires, corrosion, kinking, birdcaging, diameter reduction.
- Check end fittings (eye, thimble, splice).
- Check sling tag / SWL label is intact and legible.

**Step 2 — Proof Load Test (2 marks)**
$$
L_{\text{test}} = 2.0 \times \text{SWL} = 2.0 \times 5 = 10 \text{ tonnes}
$$
- Apply slowly without shock.
- Hold for **5 minutes**.
- Check elongation, damage, broken wires.

**Step 3 — Discard Criteria (2 marks)**
- More than **10% wires broken** in any strand length of $8 \times$ rope diameter.
- Diameter reduction **> 10%** from nominal.
- **Kinking, birdcaging, popped core, severe corrosion** → immediate discard.
- Heat damage / melting → discard.
- Sling tag missing or unreadable → discard.

**Step 4 — Documentation (1 mark)**
- Test certificate from Competent Person.
- Entry in **Sling Register** with: ID, SWL, length, manufacturer, date of test, next due date.
- Sling tag physically attached to sling with SWL, ID, test date.

---

### Question B (14 Marks). `[KTU University Exam - June 2023 — Adapted]`

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure for hydrostatic testing of a pressure vessel** as per ASME Sec VIII / IS 2825. Include calculations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **procedure for testing and checking an industrial earthing system and lifting scaffolding** as per applicable Indian Standards. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Vessel Preparation (1 mark)**
- Drain, clean, remove scale, oil, sludge.
- Close all openings except for the test water inlet.
- Mount calibrated pressure gauge on top.

**Step 2 — Fill with Test Medium (1 mark)**
- Use **clean water at ambient temperature** (preferred; pneumatic test only when hydro impossible).
- Vent all air pockets via top vent valves.

**Step 3 — Pressurize in Stages (2 marks)**
- Raise pressure gradually: 25%, 50%, 75% of $P_{\text{test}}$ — hold 10 min each.
- Final stage: hold at $P_{\text{test}} = 1.30 \times \text{MAWP}$.

**Step 4 — Calculation (2 marks)**
For MAWP = 8 bar:
$$
P_{\text{test}} = 1.30 \times 8 = 10.4 \text{ bar}
$$
Hold time: **30 minutes minimum** after stabilization.

**Step 5 — Acceptance Criteria (1 mark)**
- No pressure drop.
- No visible leak (sweep welds with soapy water).
- No permanent deformation after depressurization.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Earthing System Testing (3.5 marks)** — per IS 3043:
- Measure **earth electrode resistance** using **fall-of-potential method** or **clamp-on earth tester**.
- Acceptance: $R_{\text{earth}} \leq 5\,\Omega$ for industrial; $\leq 1\,\Omega$ for sub-station.
- Test frequency: **annually**, plus after any excavation or lightning strike.
- Inspect all joints, conductor size (typically $25 \times 3 \,\text{mm}$ GI strip or $50 \,\text{mm}^2$ copper).
- Maintain **Earth Resistance Test Register**.

**Scaffolding Testing (3.5 marks)** — per IS 4014 & IS 2750:
- **Daily pre-use check** by user (planks, ties, base jacks, guard rails, toe boards).
- **Weekly thorough check** by competent person.
- After any alteration, before commissioning, after bad weather, or after 7 days idle.
- Load test on planks: apply $1.25 \times$ design load, deflection $\leq L/100$.
- Document in **Scaffold Inspection Register** with green/yellow/red tagging system.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute KTU exam revision.**

- ✅ Testing = verification by standard procedure; Checking = routine inspection.
- ✅ 5 test categories: Pre-use, Periodic, Thorough Examination, Post-Repair, Statutory.
- ✅ Lifting Machine proof load = **1.25 × SWL (static)**, **1.5 × SWL (dynamic)**.
- ✅ Lifting Tackle proof load = **2.0 × SWL**.
- ✅ Pressure vessel hydro test = **1.3 × MAWP**, hold **30 min**.
- ✅ Pneumatic test = **1.1 × MAWP**, only if hydro not feasible.
- ✅ Hook opening increase limit = **5%** of original.
- ✅ Wire rope discard = **> 10% wires broken** in $8d$ length.
- ✅ Earth resistance: industrial $\leq 5\,\Omega$, sub-station $\leq 1\,\Omega$.
- ✅ Insulation resistance: $\geq 1 \,\text{M}\Omega$ per $1000\,\text{V}$.
- ✅ Lifting machine test frequency = **12 months** (Factories Act Sec 28).
- ✅ Pressure vessel test frequency = **24 months** (Factories Act Sec 31).
- ✅ Fire extinguisher hydro test = **5 years** (CO₂ = 10 years).
- ✅ Gas cylinder test = **5 years**.
- ✅ Competent Person = certified by Chief Inspector, qualified, independent.
- ✅ Test certificate must include: ID, date, method, load, observation, result, validity, signature, registration number.
- ✅ Records retained $\geq 3$ years (or equipment life for incident records).
- ✅ Standards to remember: **IS 3177** (crane), **IS 2825** (vessel), **IS 4014** (scaffold), **IS 3043** (earthing), **IS 2190** (fire extinguisher), **ASME Sec VIII** (boiler/vessel), **BS 7121** (crane safe use), **OISD-145** (oil & gas).
- ✅ Permits required before test: **LOTO + Permit-to-Work + Calibration Certificate + Competent Person Letter**.
- ✅ Risk-Based Inspection (RBI) is the modern industry 4.0 approach.
- ✅ Three decision outcomes: **PASS / FAIL / CONDITIONAL (with derated SWL)**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conveyor Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET754 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conveyor Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conveyor System (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> A *conveyor system* is a **mechanical handling equipment** that transports materials, packages, or items from one location to another through the controlled movement of a **continuous carrying medium** (belt, chain, rollers, screw, air, etc.) driven by a **power transmission arrangement** (motor, gearbox, drive pulley), forming an integral part of bulk material handling and assembly-line operations in industry.

According to **IS 8598:1991** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.219**, a conveyor is classified as a *power-transmission apparatus* whose moving parts pose **mechanical, electrical, ergonomic, and fire-related hazards** that must be controlled through engineering safeguards, administrative controls, and PPE.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Escalator at a Shopping Mall**
> 
> Imagine an **escalator** in a shopping mall. It carries people continuously from the ground floor to the first floor without them having to climb stairs. A conveyor works on the **exact same principle**, except instead of people, it carries **coal, ore, sand, grain, cement, packages, or even cars**. The continuous loop (belt) and the motor (drive) are the "muscles" of the system. The safety guard rails on the escalator are analogous to the **emergency stop cords, pull-wires, and side guards** on industrial conveyors. When the escalator's safety comb triggers, the whole system shuts down — this is the same as the **belt-sway switch, rip-detection sensor, and zero-speed switch** on a conveyor.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (KTU Module 3 Reference)

| S.No | Conveyor Type | Carrying Medium | Typical Use |
|------|---------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Belt Conveyor | Continuous fabric/steel-cord belt | Coal, ore, cement, aggregate |
| 2 | Roller/Gravity Conveyor | Free-spinning rollers | Cartons, boxes (logistics) |
| 3 | Chain Conveyor | Sprocket-engaged chain | Pallets, automotive bodies |
| 4 | Screw Conveyor | Helical screw in trough | Sludge, grain, ash, lime |
| 5 | Bucket Elevator | Belt+attached buckets | Vertical lift of bulk solids |
| 6 | Pneumatic Conveyor | Air stream in pipe | Flour, cement, plastic pellets |
| 7 | Overhead/Hoist Conveyor | Trolley on overhead rail | Assembly lines, paint shops |

## 1.4 Key Industrial Standards & Constants

- **Maximum safe belt speed** (per **IS 11592:2000**): **5.0 m/s** for general bulk solids
- **Standard belt widths** (per **ISO 5293**): **300, 400, 500, 600, 800, 1000, 1200, 1400, 1600, 1800, 2000 mm**
- **Standard troughing angle**: **30°** (2-roll) or **45°** (3-roll) troughing idlers
- **Idler rotation speed**: typically **200–600 rpm**
- **Noise exposure limit** near conveyors: **85 dB(A)** (per **Factories Act 1947, Schedule XV**)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of a Belt Conveyor

A belt conveyor is not a single device; it is a **mechatronic system** of interacting subsystems:

- **Drive Subsystem** — Electric motor, fluid coupling, gearbox, drive pulley
- **Carrying Subsystem** — Endless belt, idlers (carrying and return), pulleys
- **Loading Subsystem** — Hopper, feeder, chute, skirt board
- **Discharge Subsystem** — Head pulley scraper, plough, tripper
- **Tensioning Subsystem** — Gravity take-up, screw take-up, winch take-up
- **Safety Subsystem** — Pull-cord, belt-sway switch, rip detector, zero-speed switch, metal detector, over-speed trip

## 2.2 Operating Logic – Step-by-Step

1. **Motor energizes** → torque is transmitted through a fluid coupling (soft-start) to the gearbox.
2. **Gearbox reduces RPM** to the design pulley speed (e.g., 60–180 rpm for head pulley).
3. **Drive pulley rotates** → friction between pulley and belt generates **driving tension**.
4. **Belt circulates continuously** carrying material on the carrying strand, returning empty.
5. **Idlers support the belt** — troughing idlers (top) and flat return idlers (bottom).
6. **Material loads** at the tail end through a feeder, ensuring **centred loading** to prevent spillage.
7. **Discharge occurs** at the head pulley due to centrifugal throw and gravity.
8. **Safety interlocks** continuously monitor — any fault trips the contactor and stops the drive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Take-Up is Critical:**
> Without a take-up device, the belt stretches over time, causing **slippage on the drive pulley** → loss of traction → reduced capacity and possible **fire hazard** from frictional heat.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is KTU-board favourite. Memorize every equation and unit.**

| S.No | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Application |
|------|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Volumetric capacity of belt | $Q_v = A \cdot v$ | m³/s | $A$ = cross-section of load |
| 2 | Mass capacity of belt | $Q_m = 3.6 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho$ | t/h | The constant **3.6** converts m³/s to t/h |
| 3 | Peripheral force on drive pulley | $F_u = \frac{P}{v}$ | N | P in W, v in m/s |
| 4 | Power at drive pulley | $P = \frac{F_u \cdot v}{1000}$ | kW | Standard output power |
| 5 | Effective belt tension | $F_e = F_1 + F_2$ (or $F_1 - F_2$) | N | $F_1$ tight side, $F_2$ slack side |
| 6 | Euler's belt-equation (no slip) | $\frac{F_1}{F_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | — | $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $\theta$ = wrap angle (rad) |
| 7 | Belt length (open belt) | $L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}$ | m | C = centre distance, D = pulley diameter |
| 8 | Belt length (cross belt) | $L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \frac{(D_1 + D_2)^2}{4C}$ | m | Crossed configuration |
| 9 | Idler spacing (carrying) | $I_{top} = 0.4 \cdot B$ to $1.5$ | m | B = belt width, depends on material density |
| 10 | Idler spacing (return) | $I_{btm} = 2.0 \cdot B$ to $3.0$ | m | Lower load → wider spacing allowed |
| 11 | Belt speed (typical) | $v = \frac{\pi D_1 N}{60}$ | m/s | D₁ = head pulley dia (m), N = rpm |
| 12 | Troughing angle | $\alpha = 30°, 35°, 45°$ | deg | Three-roll idler standard angles |
| 13 | Total conveyor power | $P_{total} = \frac{P_{eff}}{\eta_{total}}$ | kW | $\eta_{total} \approx 0.85 - 0.92$ |
| 14 | Maximum tension in belt | $T_{max} = k \cdot F_e$ | N | k = safety factor (**6–10** for fabric belts) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Conveyor systems are the **arteries of modern industry**. Without them, **mining, cement, power, steel, food, automotive, e-commerce logistics, and ports** cannot function at scale. The safety analysis of conveyors is heavily tested in KTU because it integrates **mechanics, electrical safety, ergonomics, fire safety, and statutory compliance** — making it a *signature topic* in the Industrial Safety & Health syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass Capacity of a Troughed Belt Conveyor

**Statement to prove:** $Q_m = 3.6 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho$ where $A$ is the cross-sectional area of the load on the belt, $v$ is the belt speed, and $\rho$ is the bulk density of the material.

### Step 1 — Start from the fundamental definition of mass flow rate

By definition, mass flow rate is the mass of material crossing a vertical plane per unit time.

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = \rho \cdot (A \cdot v)
$$

where $\dot{V}$ is the volumetric flow rate in m³/s, equal to the cross-section area $A$ (m²) multiplied by the velocity $v$ (m/s).

### Step 2 — Convert to standard t/h used in industry

Industry standard (IS 11592) expresses capacity in **tonnes per hour (t/h)**. Therefore, the conversion is required:

$$
1\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 3600\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

Since **1 tonne = 1000 kg = 1 m³ × 1000 kg/m³** (for water), for any density $\rho$ (in kg/m³ or t/m³):

$$
\dot{m}\ (\text{t/h}) = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot 3600}{1000}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the numerical constant

$$
\dot{m}\ (\text{t/h}) = 3.6 \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v
$$

### Step 4 — Final boxed result

$$
\boxed{Q_m = 3.6 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho\ \text{tonnes/hour}}
$$

> **Units check:** If $A$ = 0.05 m², $v$ = 2 m/s, $\rho$ = 1500 kg/m³, then $Q_m = 3.6 \times 0.05 \times 2 \times 1.5 = 0.54$ t/h. ✓ (dimensions consistent).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effective Tension using Euler's Capstan Equation

**Statement to prove:** $\dfrac{F_1}{F_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$

### Step 1 — Consider an elemental arc of the belt on the pulley

Let a small element of belt subtend an angle $d\theta$ at the pulley centre. Let $F$ be the tension on the entering side and $F + dF$ on the leaving side.

### Step 2 — Force balance in tangential direction

The net tangential force $(F + dF) - F = dF$ is balanced by the **maximum static friction** from the pulley:

$$
dF = \mu \cdot dN
$$

where $dN$ is the normal reaction on the element.

### Step 3 — Force balance in radial direction

The normal reaction supports the centrifugal + tension components. For a small element, the radial equilibrium gives:

$$
dN \approx F \cdot d\theta
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and integrate

$$
dF = \mu \cdot F \cdot d\theta
$$

Separating variables:

$$
\frac{dF}{F} = \mu \cdot d\theta
$$

Integrating from $F_2$ (slack side) to $F_1$ (tight side) over the wrap angle $\theta$:

$$
\int_{F_2}^{F_1} \frac{dF}{F} = \mu \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{F_1}{F_2}\right) = \mu \theta
$$

### Step 5 — Exponentiate to obtain the Euler-Capstan equation

$$
\boxed{\frac{F_1}{F_2} = e^{\mu \theta}}
$$

> **Numerical illustration:** If $\mu = 0.30$ and $\theta = 180° = \pi$ rad, then $F_1/F_2 = e^{0.30 \times 3.1416} = e^{0.9425} = 2.567$. So the tight-side tension must be at least **2.567 times** the slack-side tension to prevent slippage.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Open Belt Length (Pulley Centre Distance)

**Statement:** $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}$

### Step 1 — Identify the geometry

Open belt has a tight side (top) and a slack side (bottom). Both sides are straight lines of length $L_1$ each, connecting the two pulleys.

$$
L_1 = C + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)}{2} \cdot \tan^{-1}\text{(small angle)}
$$

For practical values, the straight length approximates to:

$$
L_1 \approx C + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}
$$

### Step 2 — Add the two straight segments

$$
2 L_1 = 2C + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{2C}
$$

### Step 3 — Add the two wrap arcs

Each pulley wraps by $\pi$ rad (open belt) so total wrap length:

$$
L_2 = \frac{\pi D_1}{2} + \frac{\pi D_2}{2} = \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Total belt length

$$
\boxed{L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \frac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}}
$$

> **Note:** The factor $\dfrac{(D_1 - D_2)^2}{4C}$ arises from the Pythagorean adjustment because the straight-line tangent does not directly span $C$ when the pulley diameters differ.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Belt Conveyor with Safety Overlay

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Power Source 11kV 415V AC Supply] --> B[Motor Starter VFD Soft Starter]
    B --> C[Coupling Fluid Coupling or Gearbox]
    C --> D[Drive Pulley Head Pulley Motorized]
    D --> E[Conveyor Belt Endless Fabric or Steel Cord]
    E --> F[Tail Pulley Tensioning Take Up]
    F --> G[Loading Point Hopper Chute Feeder]
    G --> E
    E --> H[Discharge Point Head Pulley Scraper]
    E --> I[Carrying Idlers Troughing 30 45 deg]
    E --> J[Return Idlers Flat 0 deg]
    H --> K[Discharge Chute Spillage Control]
    D --> L[Safety Sensors Block]
    L --> M[Pull Cord Switch Both Sides]
    L --> N[Belt Sway Switch]
    L --> O[Rip Detection Switch]
    L --> P[Zero Speed Switch]
    L --> Q[Metal Detector for Tramp Metal]
    L --> R[Overload Trip Current Relay]
    L --> S[Emergency Stop Push Button]
    M --> B
    N --> B
    O --> B
    P --> B
    Q --> B
    R --> B
    S --> B
    T[Dust Extraction Unit Wet Dry Scrubber] --> G
    U[Belt Cleaning System Plough Scraper] --> H
    V[Fire Suppression CO2 or Water Spray] --> E
    W[Guard Rails Side Covers Fixed and Interlocked] --> I
    X[Operator Control Desk PLC HMI] --> B
    Y[Audible Visual Alarm Hooter Beacon] --> B
```

## 4.2 Component-Level Block Diagram — Safety Interlock Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Operator Start Command] --> G1[AND Gate All OK]
    B1[Pull Cord Reset] --> G1
    C1[Belt Sway OK] --> G1
    D1[Zero Speed OK] --> G1
    E1[Rip Detector OK] --> G1
    F1[Emergency Stop Released] --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Contactor Coil Energized]
    H1 --> I1[Motor ON Belt Runs]
    I1 --> J1[Feedback to PLC]
    J1 --> K1[Alarm or Trip Logic]
    K1 --> L1[Display on HMI Fault Log]
```

## 4.3 Diagram Interpretation for KTU Board

- **Power flow:** Source → Starter → Coupling → Drive pulley → Belt (through friction)
- **Material flow:** Tail end loading → along carrying strand → Head end discharge
- **Safety logic:** All sensor outputs feed an **AND gate** (all-clear condition); a single fault **trips the contactor** and de-energizes the motor.
- **Per IS 1890 & OSHA 1910.219:** Pull-cord switches must be **within 30 m reach** along the conveyor length; emergency stops every **30 m**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Direct Recall)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set B]`
**Q:** *Define a conveyor system. List any four types of conveyors commonly used in industry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A conveyor system is a **mechanical handling device** that transports materials continuously between two or more points using a **powered carrying medium**.

**Four types of conveyors:**

1. **Belt Conveyor** — uses an endless belt; suitable for bulk solids (coal, ore, grain).
2. **Screw Conveyor** — uses a helical screw inside a trough; for sludge, ash, cement.
3. **Roller Conveyor** — uses free-spinning rollers; for cartons, baggage, pallet boxes.
4. **Bucket Elevator** — uses belt-attached buckets for vertical lifting of granular material.

> *(Each type: 0.5 mark = 2 marks; definition: 1 mark = total 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Set A]`
**Q:** *Mention any three safety devices used in belt conveyors.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Pull-cord switch** — Installed along both sides of the conveyor; manual pull stops the belt instantly. *(1 mark)*
2. **Belt-sway switch** — Detects lateral misalignment of the belt and trips the system. *(1 mark)*
3. **Zero-speed switch** — Confirms the belt has stopped; prevents restart under loaded conditions. *(1 mark)*

*(Other acceptable answers: rip-detection switch, metal detector, over-speed trip, emergency stop push-button, belt slippage detector.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU ESE - July 2024 Model Paper]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**
**Q:** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a belt conveyor system. List the main components.*

**Model Solution:**

A belt conveyor system is a **continuous material handling equipment** that uses a flexible belt stretched between two pulleys. Material is loaded at the tail end and discharged at the head end.

**Main Components (4 marks):**

1. **Drive pulley (head pulley)** — Motor-driven; provides traction to the belt.
2. **Tail pulley** — At the loading end; can be used as take-up.
3. **Belt** — Endless fabric or steel-cord; carries the material.
4. **Idlers (carrying and return)** — Support the belt and the load.
5. **Take-up device** — Maintains correct belt tension.
6. **Loading and discharge chutes** — Control material entry and exit.
7. **Drive assembly** — Motor, fluid coupling, gearbox, brake.

**Working (3 marks):**

- The motor rotates the drive pulley via the gearbox.
- Friction between the drive pulley and the belt causes the belt to circulate.
- Material loaded on the belt at the tail end is carried to the head end.
- At the head end, the material is discharged by gravity and centrifugal action.
- The empty belt returns on the underside, supported by return idlers.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**
**Q:** *Discuss the major hazards associated with conveyor systems and the engineering control measures to mitigate them.*

**Model Solution:**

**Major Hazards (3.5 marks):**

| Hazard | Source | Consequence |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| **Entanglement** | Rotating shafts, pulleys, nip points | Amputation, fatality |
| **Nip-point crushing** | Belt-pulley interface | Hand/finger crushing |
| **Slip and fall** | Spillage, oil leaks | Musculoskeletal injury |
| **Falling material** | Misalignment, overloading | Head/foot injuries |
| **Fire** | Belt slippage, friction heat | Belt fire, plant fire |
| **Electrical** | Cable damage, earthing failure | Electrocution |
| **Dust explosion** | Combustible dust (grain, coal) | Explosion, mass casualty |
| **Noise** | Idlers, drive assembly | Hearing loss > **85 dB(A)** |

**Control Measures (3.5 marks):**

1. **Guarding** — All rotating shafts, couplings, and pulleys must have fixed or interlocked guards (per **OSHA 1910.219**).
2. **Pull-cord switches** — Every 30 m on both sides, with **latching** to prevent auto-restart.
3. **Emergency stops** — Mushroom-head push buttons at head, tail, and at intermediate positions.
4. **Belt-sway and rip-detection** — Automatic trip on mis-tracking or longitudinal cuts.
5. **Dust suppression** — Enclosed hood, water spray, or dry cyclone; spark detection in coal conveyors.
6. **Fire protection** — CO₂ or water-spray system, infra-red flame detectors, fire-resistant belts (per **IS 3181**).
7. **PPE** — Safety shoes, hearing protection, dust masks, gloves, helmets.
8. **Lock-Out Tag-Out (LOTO)** — Mandatory before any maintenance.
9. **Training** — Authorized personnel only; permit-to-work for confined space work.
10. **Housekeeping** — Daily spillage removal; clean walkways.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Marks are deducted** if students write only hazards **without linking them to control measures** (and vice-versa) — KTU's CO3 expects *engineering control linkage*.
> 2. **Avoid generic answers** like "use PPE" without specifying *what* PPE and *against what* hazard.
> 3. **Do not forget statutory references** — mentioning *OSHA / IS / Factories Act* earns extra marks in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Belt slippage causing fire** is the *most expected* KTU question — know it cold.
> 5. **Numericals without units** lose 1–2 marks; **units must accompany every numerical answer**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU ESE - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 marks**
**Q:** *Derive an expression for the mass handling capacity of a troughed belt conveyor in tonnes per hour.*

**Model Solution:**

Let:
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the load on the belt (m²)
- $v$ = belt speed (m/s)
- $\rho$ = bulk density of material (t/m³)
- $Q_m$ = mass capacity (t/h)

**Step 1:** Volumetric capacity (m³/s):

$$
Q_v = A \cdot v
$$

**Step 2:** Mass capacity (kg/s):

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_v = \rho \cdot A \cdot v\ \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to t/h:

$$
Q_m = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot 3600}{1000} = 3.6 \rho A v
$$

**Step 4:** Final boxed equation:

$$
\boxed{Q_m = 3.6 \cdot A \cdot v \cdot \rho\ \text{tonnes/hour}}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical Example (bonus credit):** For a belt of width 1000 mm, troughing angle 30°, $v = 1.5$ m/s, $\rho = 1.2$ t/m³ (coal): $A \approx 0.07$ m². Then $Q_m = 3.6 \times 0.07 \times 1.5 \times 1.2 = 0.454$ t/h.

*(Full marks: 5 for derivation + 2 for numerical example or for stating the assumption that A is constant.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 marks**
**Q:** *Explain with applications: (i) Screw conveyor, (ii) Bucket elevator, and (iii) Pneumatic conveyor.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Screw Conveyor (2.5 marks):**
- **Construction:** A helical screw rotates inside a U-shaped trough; bearing-mounted at both ends.
- **Principle:** Material is pushed forward by the rotating flight (Archimedes principle).
- **Applications:** Handling of *cement, ash, sand, grain, lime, sludge, food waste* — in power plants, cement plants, wastewater treatment.
- **Advantages:** Enclosed, dust-free; simple, low cost.
- **Limitations:** Limited length (≤ 30 m); high power for inclined operation; causes particle degradation.

**(ii) Bucket Elevator (2 marks):**
- **Construction:** Endless belt or chain with buckets bolted at regular intervals, running between a head and boot pulley.
- **Principle:** Buckets scoop material at the boot, carry it vertically, and discharge at the head by centrifugal throw.
- **Applications:** Vertical lifting of *grain in silos, clinker in cement plants, raw coal in power plants*.
- **Discharge types:** *Centrifugal, continuous, positive* — based on belt speed.

**(iii) Pneumatic Conveyor (2.5 marks):**
- **Construction:** A blower, pipeline, cyclone separator, and dust collector.
- **Principle:** Material is suspended and transported in a high-velocity air stream.
- **Types:** *Dilute-phase* (high velocity, low pressure) and *dense-phase* (low velocity, high pressure, plug flow).
- **Applications:** *Cement, fly ash, plastic pellets, flour, sugar, pharmaceutical powders* — in food, chemical, and pharmaceutical industries.
- **Advantage:** Can transport around bends, vertical lifts, and over long distances with no spillage.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 5-minute revision — read this just before the exam.**

- ✅ **Conveyor** = continuous mechanical material-handling device with a powered carrying medium.
- ✅ **Belt conveyor is the most common** type in mining, cement, and power plants.
- ✅ **Capacity formula** (high-yield!): $Q_m = 3.6 A v \rho$ t/h.
- ✅ **Power formula**: $P = F_u v / 1000$ kW, where $F_u$ is the peripheral force in Newtons.
- ✅ **Euler's belt equation** (must memorize!): $F_1 / F_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ — tight side is **2.57× slack side** for $\mu = 0.3$, $\theta = 180°$.
- ✅ **Open belt length**: $L = 2C + \pi(D_1 + D_2)/2 + (D_1 - D_2)^2 / 4C$.
- ✅ **Standard troughing angles**: 30° (2-roll) and 45° (3-roll).
- ✅ **Idler spacing**: Top ≈ (0.4 to 1.5) × belt width; Bottom ≈ (2.0 to 3.0) × belt width.
- ✅ **Standard belt speed** limit: 5 m/s (per IS 11592).
- ✅ **Noise limit**: 85 dB(A) (Factories Act, Schedule XV).
- ✅ **Pull-cord switch** every **30 m**; Emergency stops every **30 m**.
- ✅ **Critical hazards**: Entanglement at nip points, slip-fall from spillage, fire from belt slippage, dust explosion in grain/coal.
- ✅ **Mandatory statutory references** to write in 14-mark answers: **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.219**, **IS 11592:2000**, **IS 1890**, **IS 8598:1991**, **Factories Act 1947**.
- ✅ **Most-tested KTU sub-topics**: (a) Capacity derivation, (b) Euler's belt equation, (c) Hazards & controls, (d) Types of conveyors with applications, (e) Safety devices list.
- ✅ **Avoid losing marks**: Always include **units**, **statutory references**, and a **labelled diagram/block diagram** wherever the question allows.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Belt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET754 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Belt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Industrial Safety and Health**, a **Belt** refers to a flexible, continuous loop of reinforced rubber, leather, fabric, or synthetic polymer used in industrial machinery for two primary purposes:
1. **Power Transmission Belts** – Used to transmit mechanical power between rotating shafts (e.g., Flat Belts, V-Belts, Toothed/Timing Belts, Round Belts).
2. **Material Handling Belts (Conveyor Belts)** – Used to transport bulk materials, components, or finished goods across production lines.

From a safety engineering perspective, belts constitute one of the most common sources of **mechanical entanglement hazards**, **nip-point injuries**, **friction burns**, and **secondary projectile hazards** in industries governed by **IS 1893 (Industrial Guarding)** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.219** standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** "A belt is a continuous flexible medium used for transmitting power or conveying material, which presents hazards including entanglement at nip points, shearing between pulley and belt, abrasion burns from friction, and secondary hazards from belt failure or slippage."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bicycle chain system** but made of flexible rubber instead of metal links. The belt wraps around two pulleys (like wheels), and when one pulley spins (driven by a motor), the friction between the belt and pulley surface causes the belt to move, which in turn spins the second pulley. This simple principle powers everything from your car's alternator to giant factory conveyor systems.

**However**, think about what happens when your shirt sleeve, loose hair, or a tool accidentally touches a spinning belt or gets caught between the belt and the pulley:
- The belt **grabs** the material and pulls it in with enormous force (entanglement)
- The **nip point** (where belt meets pulley) acts like a giant spinning wringer
- Friction from the moving belt can **burn skin** in seconds
- A broken belt can whip violently and cause **impact injuries**

This is why every belt system in industry **must** have proper guards and safety devices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Safety Constants/Metrics (per OSHA/IS Standards):**
> - **Minimum Belt Guard Strength:** Must withstand **≥ 250 lbs (113 kg)** of impact force
> - **Maximum Safe Belt Speed:** Typically **≤ 30 m/s** for textile belts without special guards
> - **Minimum Distance from Belt Edge to Guard Opening:** **≤ 6.5 mm** for shafts, **≤ 12.5 mm** for belts
> - **Standard Nip-Point Definition:** Point where belt first contacts pulley at ≤ **45°** wrap angle

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Industrial Belts

Industrial belts are classified based on their cross-sectional geometry, material, and application:

#### A. Power Transmission Belts

| Belt Type | Cross-Section | Application | Typical Speed |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Flat Belt** | Rectangular | Long-distance power transmission, textile mills | 10–25 m/s |
| **V-Belt** | Trapezoidal | Short-center drives, high torque, automotive | 5–30 m/s |
| **Toothed/Timing Belt** | Notched | Synchronous drives, no slip required | Up to 50 m/s |
| **Round Belt** | Circular | Light-duty, fractional HP drives | 5–15 m/s |
| **Poly-V / Multi-Rib Belt** | Multiple ribs | Compact drives, serpentine routing | 20–40 m/s |

#### B. Material Handling (Conveyor) Belts

| Belt Type | Construction | Application |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Solid Woven Cotton** | Multi-ply fabric | Light-duty package handling |
| **Steel Cord** | Steel cables in rubber | Long-distance bulk material (mining) |
| **PVC/PVG** | Polymer coated | Food processing, pharmaceuticals |
| **Heat-Resistant** | Special rubber compounds | Cement plants, foundries |
| **Cleated/Profiled** | With vertical ridges | Inclined transport |

### 2.2 Hazard Analysis: Why Belts Are Dangerous

Belt-driven systems present a unique combination of hazards due to their continuous motion and high contact force:

**Step 1 — Entanglement Hazard**
- The belt surface has high friction → grabs loose clothing, hair, jewelry
- Once caught, the body part is pulled toward the nip point

**Step 2 — Nip Point Shear/Crush**
- Nip point = convergence of belt and pulley
- Acts like a rotating wringer
- Can amputate fingers in milliseconds

**Step 3 — Friction Burns and Abrasion**
- Continuous sliding contact generates heat (up to **80–120°C**)
- Skin contact causes 2nd/3rd degree burns

**Step 4 — Secondary Projectile Hazards**
- Belt breakage releases stored elastic energy
- Broken belt can strike workers at velocities exceeding **50 m/s**

**Step 5 — Static Electricity Generation**
- High-speed non-conductive belts generate static charge
- Can ignite flammable atmospheres (grain silos, paint shops)

**Step 6 — Noise Hazards**
- Belt slippage generates high-frequency noise (often **> 90 dB(A)**)
- Requires hearing protection zone

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters:** Belts are involved in approximately **30% of all machinery-related entanglement fatalities** in manufacturing, making them the single most guarded component in industrial safety standards worldwide.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Equation | Units | Description |
|-----------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| **Belt Velocity** | $v = \pi \cdot d \cdot n$ | m/s | $d$ = pulley diameter (m), $n$ = RPM |
| **Centrifugal Tension** | $T_c = m \cdot v^2$ | N | $m$ = belt mass per unit length (kg/m) |
| **Effective Tension** | $T_e = T_1 - T_2$ | N | $T_1$ = tight side, $T_2$ = slack side |
| **Power Transmitted** | $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ | W | Net effective tension × belt velocity |
| **Belt Wrap Angle** | $\theta = \pi - 2 \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{R-r}{C}\right)$ | rad | Open belt drive, $R,r$ = radii, $C$ = center distance |
| **Maximum Belt Stress** | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{T_1 + T_c}{A}$ | N/m² | $A$ = cross-sectional area of belt |
| **Slip Ratio** | $s = \dfrac{v_1 - v_2}{v_1} \times 100$ | % | Performance indicator |
| **Nip-Point Force** | $F_{nip} = \mu \cdot F_{clamp}$ | N | Frictional grip force at pulley |
| **Belt Guard Strength** | $F_{guard} \geq 113 \text{ kg}$ load | kg | OSHA minimum impact resistance |
| **Safe Guard Opening** | $\leq 12.5 \text{ mm}$ for belts | mm | Maximum aperture to prevent finger entry |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **wrap angle formula** for open vs. crossed belt drives. For **crossed belts**, the formula inverts the sign: $\theta = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{R+r}{C}\right)$. Examiner deductions are common here.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Belt safety analysis is critical in:
- **Automotive manufacturing:** Serpentine belt drives in engine assembly
- **Textile industry:** Flat belt drives for looms (historically the most dangerous belts)
- **Mining & Cement:** Multi-kilometer conveyor belts with high-tension hazards
- **Food processing:** Hygienic belt drives requiring wash-down guards
- **Power plants:** Coal conveyor belt systems (combustion hazard from slip-induced heat)
- **Printing presses:** Synchronous timing belts requiring exact guarding

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Tension in a Moving Belt

Consider a small element of belt of mass $dm$ moving along a curved path of radius $r$ with velocity $v$. The centripetal force required to keep this element on the curved path must be supplied by the additional tension in the belt.

**Step 1 — Define the mass element**

For a belt of mass per unit length $m$ (kg/m) and arc length $ds$, the mass element is:
$$dm = m \cdot ds$$

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law for circular motion**

The centripetal force acting on this element is:
$$dF_c = dm \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{r}$$

Substituting the mass element:
$$dF_c = m \cdot ds \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{r}$$

**Step 3 — Relate arc length to subtended angle**

For an element subtending angle $d\theta$ at the center:
$$ds = r \cdot d\theta$$

Substituting back:
$$dF_c = m \cdot r \cdot d\theta \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{r} = m \cdot v^2 \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Recognize uniform tension contribution**

Since belt velocity is constant throughout (assuming inextensible belt and no slip), the tension due to centrifugal effects is uniformly distributed along the belt:
$$T_c = \dfrac{dF_c}{d\theta} = m \cdot v^2$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{T_c = m \cdot v^2}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** The centrifugal tension is **independent of pulley radius** and depends only on belt mass per unit length and the square of belt velocity. This is why high-speed belt drives require stronger, lighter belts (e.g., polyester vs. leather).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Transmission Capacity

**Step 1 — Power definition**

Mechanical power transmitted by a belt is force times velocity:
$$P = F_{net} \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Identify net force**

The net tangential force is the difference between tight-side and slack-side tensions (effective tension):
$$F_{net} = T_1 - T_2 = T_e$$

**Step 3 — Express velocity in terms of pulley RPM**

For a pulley of diameter $d$ rotating at $n$ revolutions per minute:
$$v = \pi \cdot d \cdot n$$

Converting to SI units:
$$v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{60} \quad \text{(m/s, with d in m, n in rpm)}$$

**Step 4 — Combine results**

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{60}$$

**Numerical Worked Example:**

Given: V-belt drive with $d = 0.3$ m, $n = 1440$ rpm, $T_1 = 1200$ N, $T_2 = 400$ N.

**Step 5 — Calculate belt velocity**
$$v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.3 \cdot 1440}{60} = \dfrac{1357.17}{60} = 22.62 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate effective tension**
$$T_e = 1200 - 400 = 800 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate power transmitted**
$$P = 800 \times 22.62 = 18{,}096 \text{ W} \approx 18.1 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8 — Centrifugal tension check**

If belt mass per unit length $m = 0.4$ kg/m:
$$T_c = 0.4 \times (22.62)^2 = 0.4 \times 511.66 = 204.66 \text{ N}$$

This reduces the effective tension by $T_c$ on both sides, so the actual transmitted power is slightly less than calculated.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Belt Selection for Safety

| Hazard Factor | Low Risk | Medium Risk | High Risk | Mitigation |
|---------------|----------|-------------|-----------|------------|
| **Belt Speed** | < 10 m/s | 10–25 m/s | > 25 m/s | High-strength guard; speed sensors |
| **Nip Point Access** | Fully enclosed | Partial enclosure | Open access | Interlocked guard; light curtain |
| **Ambient Temperature** | < 40°C | 40–80°C | > 80°C | Heat-resistant belt; ventilation |
| **Flammable Atmosphere** | None | Possible | Continuous | Anti-static belt; grounding |
| **Operator Proximity** | > 2 m | 0.5–2 m | < 0.5 m | Physical barrier; emergency stop |
| **Belt Tension** | Low | Medium | High | Tension monitoring; breakaway coupling |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Guarded Belt Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    shaft1A --> pulley1A[Driving Pulley]
    pulley1A -->|friction contact| beltA[Industrial Belt]
    beltA -->|friction contact| pulley2A[Driven Pulley]
    pulley2A --> shaft2A[Driven Shaft]
    shaft2A --> loadA[Machine Load]
    
    guardA[Belt Guard Enclosure] -.protects.-> beltA
    guardA -.protects.-> pulley1A
    guardA -.protects.-> pulley2A
    
    nip1A[Nip Point Sensor] -.monitors.-> pulley1A
    nip2A[Nip Point Sensor] -.monitors.-> pulley2A
    
    interlockA[Safety Interlock Switch] -.controls access.-> guardA
    eStopA[Emergency Stop System] -.cuts power.-> motorA
    tMonA[Tension Monitor] -.measures.-> beltA
    
    interlockA --> eStopA
    nip1A --> eStopA
    nip2A --> eStopA
    tMonA --> eStopA
    
    classDef safety fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef power fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

```

### 4.2 Belt Hazard Zone Identification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    zone1[Hazard Zone 1: Belt Run] -->|Entanglement| injuryA[Limb Entanglement]
    zone2[Hazard Zone 2: Nip Points] -->|Shear and Crush| injuryB[Finger Amputation]
    zone3[Hazard Zone 3: Belt Surface] -->|Friction and Heat| injuryC[2nd and 3rd Degree Burns]
    zone4[Hazard Zone 4: Belt Breakage] -->|Whip Impact| injuryD[Trauma from Broken Belt]
    zone5[Hazard Zone 5: Static Charge] -->|Ignition| injuryE[Fire or Explosion]
    zone6[Hazard Zone 6: Slippage Noise] -->|Acoustic| injuryF[Hearing Loss]

    classDef injury fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class injuryA,injuryB,injuryC,injuryD,injuryE,injuryF injury
```

### 4.3 Belt Guard Selection Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA{Is belt accessible to operators during operation?}
    startA -->|Yes, frequent| typeA[Fixed Perforated Guard]

    typeA --> specA[Material: 2 mm Mild Steel or 1.5 mm Stainless]
    typeA --> specA1[Opening Size: Less than 12.5 mm]
    typeA --> specA2[Color: Yellow or Orange for Visibility]
    
    typeB --> specB[Safety Interlock: IEC 60947-5-1 Compliant]
    typeB --> specB1[Guard Lock: Category 3 PLd Minimum]
    typeB --> specB2[Tool Required for Removal]

    typeC --> specC1[Ventilation Ports if Heat Dissipation Needed]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Nip Point" with reference to belt drives. Why is it considered the most hazardous zone in a belt drive system?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Nip Point** is defined as the point of convergence between a rotating element (pulley, drum, or roller) and the belt or material being conveyed, where the gap closes to zero and a shearing/crushing action occurs.

**Why most hazardous:**
1. **Inaccessibility:** It is often partially obscured by the belt itself
2. **High force concentration:** All transmitted torque passes through this small contact zone
3. **Rapid injury:** Can cause complete amputation of fingers within **50 milliseconds**
4. **Pull-in effect:** Friction at nip point actively pulls loose materials (clothing, hair, tools) into the gap
5. **No escape:** Once entanglement begins, the rotational momentum prevents manual withdrawal

(3 marks – 1 for definition, 2 for hazard explanation)

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six safety devices used for protection against belt drive hazards.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Fixed Belt Guard** – Perforated metal enclosure over belt and pulleys
2. **Interlocked Guard with Safety Switch** – Cuts power when guard is opened
3. **Belt Tension Monitor** – Detects slack or breakage conditions
4. **Anti-Static Grounding Wire** – Drains static charge from non-conductive belts
5. **Emergency Stop Pull Cord** – Located along entire belt length (for conveyors)
6. **Nip-Point Roller Guard** – Additional cylindrical guard preventing finger entry
7. **Belt Misalignment Switch** – Detects belt drift and triggers shutdown
8. **Speed Sensor with Overspeed Trip** – Stops system on belt speed anomaly

(3 marks – ½ mark per correct device)

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice Provided

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the different types of industrial belts used for power transmission, with neat sketches. Discuss the relative merits and demerits of each type with respect to safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flat belt drives a pulley of diameter 0.6 m at 1200 rpm. The mass of the belt per meter length is 0.5 kg/m. The angle of wrap is 160°, and the coefficient of friction is 0.3. If the maximum tension in the belt is limited to 2400 N, calculate: (i) Belt velocity (ii) Centrifugal tension (iii) Maximum power transmitted. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Industrial power transmission belts are classified into five main types:

**1. Flat Belt:**
- **Construction:** Rectangular cross-section, typically leather, fabric, or rubber
- **Advantages:** Simple design, high efficiency (95–98%), low cost, suitable for long center distances
- **Disadvantages:** High slip risk (3–5%), requires high tension, prone to stretching, dangerous at high speeds due to whip effect
- **Safety concern:** Most prone to entanglement because entire belt surface is exposed

**2. V-Belt:**
- **Construction:** Trapezoidal cross-section that wedges into pulley groove
- **Advantages:** Self-aligning, higher torque capacity than flat belt, compact, slip-resistant due to wedging action
- **Disadvantages:** Limited speed range, generates more heat, shorter life
- **Safety concern:** Heat generation can damage belt and create ignition source

**3. Toothed (Timing/Synchronous) Belt:**
- **Construction:** Reinforced rubber with molded teeth that mesh with pulley
- **Advantages:** No slip, constant velocity ratio, no need for high tension, quieter operation
- **Disadvantages:** Expensive, requires precise alignment, limited shock absorption
- **Safety concern:** Teeth failure can cause sudden breakage with projectile hazard

**4. Poly-V / Multi-Rib Belt:**
- **Construction:** Multiple longitudinal ribs (typically 3–12) on flat belt surface
- **Advantages:** Compact, flexible, high-speed capability, good heat dissipation
- **Disadvantages:** Sensitive to misalignment, special pulleys required
- **Safety concern:** Misalignment causes rib tearing and belt whip

**5. Round Belt:**
- **Construction:** Circular cross-section, often polyurethane
- **Advantages:** Inexpensive, easy to join, suitable for small drives
- **Disadvantages:** Low power capacity, high slip tendency
- **Safety concern:** Easily caught and pulled into machinery

**Safety Comparison Matrix:**

| Belt Type | Entanglement Risk | Heat Risk | Breakage Risk | Static Risk |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| Flat | High | Low | Medium | High |
| V-Belt | Medium | High | Low | Medium |
| Toothed | Low | Low | High | Low |
| Poly-V | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Round | High | Low | Low | High |

(7 marks distributed: 1 for each type description, 2 for safety comparison, 1 for conclusion)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Pulley diameter, $d = 0.6$ m
- Speed, $n = 1200$ rpm
- Belt mass per unit length, $m = 0.5$ kg/m
- Angle of wrap, $\theta = 160° = \dfrac{160\pi}{180} = 2.793$ rad
- Coefficient of friction, $\mu = 0.3$
- Maximum tension, $T_1 = 2400$ N

**Step 1 — Calculate Belt Velocity**

$$v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.6 \cdot 1200}{60} = \dfrac{2261.95}{60} = 37.70 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Centrifugal Tension**

$$T_c = m \cdot v^2 = 0.5 \times (37.70)^2 = 0.5 \times 1421.29 = 710.65 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Belt Friction Equation (Eytelwein/Capstan Equation)**

The tension ratio on the tight and slack sides is:
$$\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

Substituting values:
$$\dfrac{2400 - 710.65}{T_2 - 710.65} = e^{0.3 \times 2.793}$$

Computing the exponent:
$$e^{0.838} = 2.312$$

Therefore:
$$\dfrac{1689.35}{T_2 - 710.65} = 2.312$$

$$T_2 - 710.65 = \dfrac{1689.35}{2.312} = 730.68$$

$$T_2 = 730.68 + 710.65 = 1441.33 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Maximum Power Transmitted**

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v = (2400 - 1441.33) \times 37.70 = 958.67 \times 37.70$$

$$\boxed{P = 36{,}141.86 \text{ W} \approx 36.14 \text{ kW}}$$

(7 marks distributed: 2 for velocity, 2 for centrifugal tension, 3 for power calculation)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` – Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various hazards associated with industrial conveyor belts. Explain the safety measures and guarding requirements as per OSHA and IS standards. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a typical belt guard interlock system. How does it satisfy the requirements of ISO 13849-1 (Category 3, PL d)? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Hazards of Industrial Conveyor Belts:**

**1. Entanglement and Entrainment Hazards**
- Loose clothing, hair, jewelry caught by moving belt
- Risk of limbs being dragged into tail or head pulleys
- **Mitigation:** Self-cleaning tail pulleys, side guards, emergency stop cords within reach

**2. Nip Point and Shear Hazards**
- Convergence zones between belt and pulleys/idlers
- Can cause crushing, shearing, amputation
- **Mitigation:** Nip guards, roller guards, fixed enclosures

**3. Falling Object Hazards (for inclined/overhead conveyors)**
- Material falling from belt onto workers below
- **Mitigation:** Skirt boards, hoods, restricted access zones

**4. Electrical and Fire Hazards**
- Static electricity from dry belt → ignition source
- Belt friction heat → fire risk (especially with combustible materials)
- **Mitigation:** Static grounding brushes, fire-resistant belts, spark detection

**5. Noise Hazards**
- Belt-on-idler noise can exceed 95 dB(A)
- **Mitigation:** Idler lagging, sound enclosures, hearing PPE zone marking

**6. Ergonomic Hazards**
- Manual loading/unloading stations
- Repetitive motion injuries
- **Mitigation:** Ergonomic design, mechanical aids, job rotation

**OSHA Standards (29 CFR 1910.219 & 1910.219):**
- All belts, pulleys, chains must be guarded
- Guard openings ≤ 12.5 mm for belts
- Guards must be secured (requiring tools for removal)
- Pull cord emergency stops every 15–30 m on long conveyors

**IS Standards (IS 1893 Part 1, IS 11952):**
- Guard strength minimum 113 kg impact load
- Yellow/black hazard striping on guard surfaces
- Mandatory maintenance access provisions with interlock

(7 marks: 1 for each hazard with mitigation, 2 for standards)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Construction of Belt Guard Interlock System:**

A belt guard interlock system consists of the following components:

**1. Guard Door Switch (Sensor)**
- Mechanically-actuated safety switch (e.g., Schmersal RSS 36, Euchner CTP)
- Mounted on guard door with actuator on frame
- Provides positive-break contacts per IEC 60947-5-1

**2. Safety Relay or Safety PLC**
- Monitors switch state and controls motor contactors
- Examples: Pilz PNOZ s4, Allen-Bradley GuardLogix
- Performs cross-fault monitoring

**3. Contactor Monitoring**
- Two contactors in series for motor control
- EDM (External Device Monitoring) feedback loop

**4. Diagnostic Interface**
- LED indicators for status
- Connection to plant SCADA/PLC for fault logging

**Working Principle:**

1. When guard door is closed, the safety switch is actuated → safety relay energizes → contactors close → motor starts
2. When guard door is opened during operation:
   - Safety switch mechanically breaks contact within **< 25 ms**
   - Safety relay detects open circuit
   - Safety relay removes power to contactors
   - Motor stops within category 1 or 0 stop time
3. Restart is prevented until guard is re-closed AND a reset command is issued

**Compliance with ISO 13849-1 Category 3, PL d:**

| Requirement | Implementation |
|-------------|----------------|
| **Two-channel architecture** | Dual-channel safety switch with separate contacts |
| **Cross-fault monitoring** | Safety relay checks both channels independently |
| **Single-fault tolerance** | System remains safe if one channel fails |
| **Diagnostic Coverage (DC)** | ≥ 90% (achieved through periodic test pulses) |
| **Mean Time to Dangerous Failure (MTTFd)** | ≥ 30 years (High category) |
| **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** | Score ≥ 65 (separation, diversity, environmental control) |

This architecture ensures that **even with a single component failure**, the system maintains its safety function or transitions to a safe state, which is the defining requirement for Performance Level d (PL d) per ISO 13849-1.

(7 marks: 2 for construction, 2 for working, 3 for ISO 13849-1 compliance)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **$T_1$ and $T_2$** in the friction equation. Remember: $T_1$ is the **tight side** tension (larger), $T_2$ is the **slack side** tension (smaller). Marks are deducted if reversed.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to **subtract $T_c$** before applying the capstan equation. The correct form is $\dfrac{T_1 - T_c}{T_2 - T_c} = e^{\mu\theta}$, NOT $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta}$. This error alone costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Treating wrap angle $\theta$ in degrees instead of radians in the exponential. Always convert: $\theta_{rad} = \theta_{deg} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Not specifying **units** in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Drawing belt diagrams without showing the **nip points** explicitly with labels. Always mark nip points with arrows in sketches.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ✅ A **belt** is a flexible power transmission or conveying medium with multiple hazard categories
- ✅ The **nip point** is the most dangerous zone — always highlight it in diagrams
- ✅ Five main belt types: **Flat, V-Belt, Toothed, Poly-V, Round** — know each for 3-mark questions
- ✅ Three core formulas (must memorize):
  - Velocity: $v = \pi d n / 60$
  - Centrifugal Tension: $T_c = m v^2$
  - Capstan Equation: $(T_1 - T_c)/(T_2 - T_c) = e^{\mu\theta}$ (always subtract $T_c$ first)
- ✅ Belt power formula: $P = (T_1 - T_2) \times v$
- ✅ Guard opening limit: **≤ 12.5 mm** for belts (OSHA standard)
- ✅ Guard strength minimum: **113 kg** (250 lbs) impact resistance
- ✅ Six hazard categories: **Entanglement, Nip-Point, Heat/Friction, Projectile, Static, Noise**
- ✅ Eight safety devices: Fixed guard, Interlocked guard, Tension monitor, Anti-static, E-stop cord, Nip roller guard, Misalignment switch, Speed sensor
- ✅ ISO 13849-1 PL d requires: **dual-channel, fault-tolerant, DC ≥ 90%, MTTFd ≥ 30 years**
- ✅ Convert angles **to radians** before using in exponential — common error
- ✅ Always mention **units** in final numerical answers
- ✅ IS standards for belts: **IS 1893, IS 11952**; OSHA: **29 CFR 1910.219**
- ✅ **Wrap angle**: Open belt $\theta = \pi - 2\sin^{-1}[(R-r)/C]$; Crossed belt $\theta = \pi + 2\sin^{-1}[(R+r)/C]$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roller Chain, Elevator and Lifts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET754 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Roller Chain, Elevator and Lifts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Roller Chain — Formal Definition

A **roller chain** is a power-transmission element composed of a series of cylindrical rollers linked together by means of side plates and pins, transmitting rotary motion between two or more toothed sprockets through positive (non-slipping) engagement. It is the most widely used chain drive in industrial machinery due to its **high efficiency (typically 97–98 %)**, compactness, and ability to transmit high power over short to medium centre distances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Roller chain construction (parts: inner link, outer link, pin, bushing, roller, side plate); selection based on power transmission; service factor; design considerations; safety in operation.

## 1.2 Elevator and Lifts — Formal Definition

**Elevator (US) / Lift (UK):** A vertical transport device that moves people or goods between floors of a building using a hoist mechanism driven by an electric motor or hydraulic actuator. The term **elevator** is used in American English, while **lift** is the British/Indian/European term — both refer to the same equipment in KTU examination context.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference (No fabrication):** The design, installation, and maintenance of lifts in India are regulated by the **Bureau of Indian Standards — IS 14665** (lift code) and the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 8, Section 5 — Installation of Lifts and Escalators)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Roller Chain Analogy — "The Bicycle Chain":**
Imagine riding a bicycle: when you pedal, the chain meshes with the front sprocket (chainring) and the rear sprocket, transferring your muscular effort to the wheel. Now scale that up — a roller chain works identically, but instead of your legs, an industrial motor provides the torque. The **rollers** are like tiny bearings that roll over the sprocket teeth, drastically reducing friction compared to a sliding contact (which is what an older "block chain" did). This is why roller chains are quieter, faster, and longer-lasting.

**Elevator Analogy — "The Vertical Conveyor":**
Think of an elevator as a **conveyor belt turned 90° vertical**. A car (cab) is attached to one end of a rope or piston, and a counterweight balances the load on the other end. The motor, in the **machine room** at the top (for traction lifts) or at the bottom (for hydraulic lifts), provides the lifting force. The counterweight is the genius of elevator design — it **offsets the weight of the car + ~50 % of the rated load**, so the motor only has to lift the difference. This slashes energy consumption and motor size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (in Bold):**
> - Standard roller chain pitch: **12.7 mm (1/2 inch)** for the 40B/08B series; **15.875 mm (5/8 inch)** for the 50B/10B series.
> - Standard elevator rated speeds: **0.5 m/s (low), 1.0 m/s (standard), 1.75–2.5 m/s (high-rise)**.
> - Standard lift machine-room location: **Top of the hoistway (overhead) for traction lifts**; **adjacent to shaft at lowest landing for hydraulic lifts**.
> - Counterweight balancing: typically **car weight + 40–50 % of rated load**.

## 1.4 Parts of a Roller Chain — Visual Inventory

| Sl. No. | Part | Function |
|:-:|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Inner Link** | Inner pair of side plates, pin, and bushing — connects the rollers. |
| 2 | **Outer Link** | Outer pair of side plates fitted to the protruding pin ends (riveted or clip-secured). |
| 3 | **Pin** | Cylindrical steel pin joining inner and outer links; it is the **rotating pivot** of the chain. |
| 4 | **Bushing** | Pressed between the inner link plates and the roller; the **roller rotates on the bushing**, while the **pin rotates inside the bushing**. |
| 5 | **Roller** | Outer cylindrical sleeve that rolls over the sprocket teeth; reduces friction and wear. |
| 6 | **Side Plate (Link Plate)** | Load-carrying plate; **perforation-shaped** (figure-8 hole) to reduce weight without compromising strength. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two bushes and pins at every joint?** This double-bearing arrangement lets the chain articulate smoothly at every pitch. The pin and bushing are typically **case-hardened alloy steel** (e.g., 40Cr, 20CrMnTi) to withstand the high Hertzian contact stresses (often **> 1 GPa** at the pin-bushing interface).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Roller Chain — Construction, Operation & Selection Logic

### 2.1.1 Construction Step-by-Step (How a Roller Chain Articulates)

1. **Two inner plates** are pressed onto a **bushing**.
2. The **roller** is a free-fitted sleeve over the bushing (clearance of ~0.1–0.2 mm).
3. The **pin** is pressed into the inner plates and protrudes on both sides.
4. **Two outer plates** are fitted over the protruding pin ends and either **riveted, cotter-pinned, or clip-locked** to form a complete link.
5. Adjacent links interlock at the pins, allowing **articulation about a single axis** (single-strand), with multi-strand versions (duplex, triplex) used for higher power transmission.

### 2.1.2 Why a Roller Chain? (The "How" and "Why")

- **Positive drive (no slip):** Unlike belts, chains cannot slip — essential for **synchronised drives** (e.g., timing of camshafts, conveyor indexing).
- **High efficiency:** Sliding friction is replaced by **rolling contact at sprocket + rotating contact at pin-bushing**. Total efficiency $\eta \approx 0.97$–$0.98$ per mesh.
- **Compactness for high power:** A small 1-inch (25.4 mm) pitch chain can transmit **up to 50 kW** at low speed.
- **Long centre distance capability:** Up to **~8 m** without intermediate idlers; beyond that, an idler/tensioner is mandatory.

### 2.1.3 Design Procedure for Roller Chain Drives (KTU Exam-Relevant)

The standard KTU-step procedure is:

1. **Determine design power:** $P_d = P \times K_s$ — where $K_s$ is the **service factor** (accounts for shock load, type of driver and driven machine, hours/day, lubrication quality).
2. **Select chain pitch** from standard tables (e.g., P = 12.7, 15.875, 19.05, 25.4, 31.75, 38.1, 44.45, 50.8 mm).
3. **Select number of teeth on small sprocket** $z_1$ (recommended $z_1 \geq 17$ to avoid polygonality and chain jumping).
4. **Calculate number of teeth on large sprocket:** $z_2 = i \times z_1$ (where $i$ is the speed ratio).
5. **Calculate chain length in pitches:** $L_p = \dfrac{2C}{P} + \dfrac{z_1 + z_2}{2} + \left(\dfrac{z_2 - z_1}{2\pi}\right)^2 \cdot \dfrac{P}{C}$ — solved iteratively for centre distance $C$.
6. **Check safety against fatigue failure** (pin-bushing bearing pressure) and **tensile failure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polygonality effect:** When a chain wraps around a small sprocket, the chain pitch forms a polygon — the **effective radius of the small sprocket** varies, causing speed pulsation. This is why $z_1 \geq 17$ is mandated (smaller $z_1$ → more polygonality → vibration, noise, chain-jumping risk).

## 2.2 Elevator / Lift — System Architecture & Safety Logic

### 2.2.1 Classification of Lifts

| Type | Drive Mechanism | Typical Use | Rated Speed |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Traction (Electric) Lift** | Rope-and-sheave, motor + gear box or gearless | High-rise, commercial | 1.0 – 10 m/s |
| **Hydraulic Lift** | Piston in cylinder, oil pump | Low-rise (≤ 4 floors) | ≤ 1.0 m/s |
| **Machine-Room-Less (MRL)** | Permanent-magnet gearless motor in shaft | Modern mid-rise | 1.0 – 3.0 m/s |
| **Goods / Service Lift** | Traction or hydraulic | Industrial freight | 0.5 – 1.0 m/s |
| **Scissor Lift (Platform)** | Hydraulic, articulated arms | Warehouse / construction | 0.1 – 0.3 m/s |

### 2.2.2 Major Components of a Traction Lift

1. **Hoistway (Lift Well):** Vertical shaft from pit to overhead.
2. **Car (Cab):** Carries passengers/goods; supported by steel wire ropes (4–8 strands, typically 10–13 mm dia).
3. **Counterweight:** Travels opposite to car; mass = car mass + (40–50) % of rated load.
4. **Driving Machine:** Motor (AC or PM synchronous) + worm-gear or gearless sheave.
5. **Traction Sheave (Drive Sheave):** Grooved pulley with V-grooves; ropes pass over it. Friction between rope and groove generates the lifting force.
6. **Governor (Speed Governor):** Centrifugal device mounted on car; **triggers the safety gear** if car speed exceeds **115 % of rated speed**.
7. **Safety Gear (Brake on Rails):** Wedge-type brakes grip the guide rails to stop a free-falling car.
8. **Buffers:** Spring or oil buffers in the pit; absorb the energy of car or counterweight at the limit of travel.
9. **Over-Speed Governor Rope:** Connects governor to a tensioning pulley in the pit; trips governor at 115 % speed.
10. **Door Interlocks:** Prevent car movement unless doors are closed and locked.

### 2.2.3 Safety Devices — The KTU "Must-Know" List

| Safety Device | Function | Trigger Condition |
|:--|:--|:--|
| **Over-Speed Governor** | Activates safety gear | Car speed ≥ **1.15 × rated** |
| **Safety Gear on Car** | Wedges against guide rails | Governor trips |
| **Buffers (Car + CWT)** | Energy absorption at pit | Car reaches limit of travel downward |
| **Final Limit Switches** | Stop motor | Car reaches terminal floor |
| **Door Interlock** | Block movement if door open | Door not fully closed & locked |
| **Emergency Stop in Car** | Cut power | Manual press |
| **Rope Brake / Rope Gripper** | Holds car on rails during power failure | Power loss |
| **Earthquake / Fire Sensor** | Recall car to safe floor | Detected event |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the counterweight?** Without a counterweight, the motor would need to lift the full car + load — wasteful. With counterweight at **car weight + 50 % load**, the motor only handles the imbalance (50 % of load). The other 50 % is **regeneratively fed back to the line** during downward travel with full load — modern AC drives capture this via **regenerative braking**, giving elevator drives efficiencies of **~80–90 %** overall.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.3.1 Roller Chain Design Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | $P_d = P \times K_s$ | Design power from rated power × service factor | kW |
| 2 | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{z_2}{z_1}$ | Speed ratio (driver/driven) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $L_p = \dfrac{2C}{P} + \dfrac{z_1 + z_2}{2} + \left(\dfrac{z_2 - z_1}{2\pi}\right)^2 \cdot \dfrac{P}{C}$ | Chain length in pitches (initial) | pitches |
| 4 | $C = \dfrac{P}{4}\left[L_p - \dfrac{z_1 + z_2}{2} + \sqrt{\left(L_p - \dfrac{z_1 + z_2}{2}\right)^2 - 8\left(\dfrac{z_2 - z_1}{2\pi}\right)^2}\right]$ | Centre distance (solved from $L_p$) | mm |
| 5 | $T = \dfrac{P_d \times 60}{2\pi N_1}$ | Torque on driving sprocket | N·m |
| 6 | $F_c = \dfrac{1000 \, P_d}{v}$ | Effective chain pull (tangential) | N |
| 7 | $v = \dfrac{z_1 P N_1}{60{,}000}$ | Chain linear speed | m/s |
| 8 | $A = \dfrac{F_c}{p_b \cdot d_r \cdot n_r}$ | Projected bearing area (pin-bushing) | mm² |
| 9 | $p_b \leq [p_b]$ | Bearing pressure check ($[p_b] = 30$–$45$ MPa for good lubrication) | MPa |
| 10 | $F_{br} = K_s \cdot P \cdot f_c$ | Breaking load safety check vs. chain tensile strength | N |

### 2.3.2 Lift Design / Performance Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | $W_{cwt} = W_{car} + \alpha \cdot Q$ | Counterweight mass (α = 0.4 to 0.5) | kg |
| 2 | $F_{rope} = \dfrac{(W_{car} + Q - W_{cwt}) \cdot g}{n_{ropes} \cdot \eta_{sys}}$ | Rope tension per strand | N |
| 3 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{sheave}$ | Overall system efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{(W_{car} + Q) \cdot v \cdot g}{\eta_{sys}}$ | Motor power (with no CWT idealisation) | W |
| 5 | $a_{max} \leq 1.0$ m/s² | Maximum acceleration limit (passenger comfort) | m/s² |
| 6 | $v_{trip} = 1.15 \times v_{rated}$ | Governor trip speed | m/s |
| 7 | $E_{buffer} = \dfrac{1}{2}(W_{car} + Q) \cdot v_{imp}^2 / g$ | Buffer energy (oil buffer) | J |
| 8 | $T_{rope\,factor} = \dfrac{F_{rope}}{F_{rope\,allowable}}$ | Rope safety factor (must be ≥ 8–12 per IS 14665) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember for KTU:**
> - $z_1 \geq 17$ (small sprocket) for **$v \leq 6$ m/s**; $z_1 \geq 21$ for high speeds.
> - $L_p$ must be an **even integer** (so the chain closes with an **offset link** or **spring clip**).
> - Centre distance $C = (30 \text{ to } 80) \cdot P$ for standard drives.
> - Maximum chain speed $v \leq 8$–$10$ m/s without special lubrication.
