# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Chain Length Equation (in Pitches)

The chain length formula in pitches $L_p$ is derived by **unwrapping the chain into straight segments plus the two arcs around the sprockets**.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry of a wrapped chain:**
A chain on two sprockets with centre distance $C$, pitch $P$, and teeth $z_1$ and $z_2$ has:

- **Two tangent straight runs** of length $\approx C$ each (corrected for the pitch-radius geometry).
- **Two wrap arcs** on the sprockets summing to a half circumference of each pitch circle.

**Step 2 — Pitch-circle diameters:**
$$d_1 = \dfrac{P}{\sin(180°/z_1)} \quad ; \quad d_2 = \dfrac{P}{\sin(180°/z_2)}$$

**Step 3 — Pitch-circle radius sum and difference:**
$$A = \dfrac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \dfrac{P}{2}\left[\csc\left(\dfrac{\pi}{z_1}\right) + \csc\left(\dfrac{\pi}{z_2}\right)\right]$$
$$B = \dfrac{d_2 - d_1}{2} = \dfrac{P}{2}\left[\csc\left(\dfrac{\pi}{z_2}\right) - \csc\left(\dfrac{\pi}{z_1}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Exact chain length (linear, in mm):**
The exact unwrapped length of the chain (when straightened) is:

$$
L = 2C\cos\beta + \pi(A + B) + \dfrac{2B \cdot B}{C}
$$

where $\beta$ is the angle of the line of centres from the tangent point to the chain run, and for long centre distances, the standard simplified form (per ISO 606) is:

$$
L = 2C + \dfrac{(z_1 + z_2)P}{2} + \dfrac{(z_2 - z_1)^2 P^2}{4\pi^2 C}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to pitches (divide by P):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= \dfrac{L}{P} \\
    &= \dfrac{2C}{P} + \dfrac{z_1 + z_2}{2} + \dfrac{(z_2 - z_1)^2 P}{4\pi^2 C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange for centre distance (inverse problem):**
Given $L_p$ (must be an integer), solve for $C$:

$$
C = \dfrac{P}{4}\left[L_p - \dfrac{z_1 + z_2}{2} + \sqrt{\left(L_p - \dfrac{z_1+z_2}{2}\right)^2 - 8\left(\dfrac{z_2 - z_1}{2\pi}\right)^2}\right]
$$

### 3.1.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Given:**
- Sprocket teeth: $z_1 = 21$, $z_2 = 63$
- Pitch: $P = 19.05$ mm (3/4 inch, 12B chain)
- Centre distance: $C = 30 P = 30 \times 19.05 = 571.5$ mm

**Find:** $L_p$ (number of pitches) and the actual chain length in mm.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{2C}{P} &= \dfrac{2 \times 571.5}{19.05} = 60.0 \\
\dfrac{z_1 + z_2}{2} &= \dfrac{21 + 63}{2} = 42 \\
\dfrac{(z_2 - z_1)^2 P}{4\pi^2 C} &= \dfrac{(63-21)^2 \times 19.05}{4 \times \pi^2 \times 571.5} \\
&= \dfrac{42^2 \times 19.05}{39.478 \times 571.5} \\
&= \dfrac{1764 \times 19.05}{22{,}564.3} \\
&= \dfrac{33{,}604.2}{22{,}564.3} \approx 1.489
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= 60.0 + 42 + 1.489 \approx 103.49 \text{ pitches}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $L_p$ must be an integer (and even-numbered preferred), choose $L_p = 104$ pitches.

**Recompute centre distance with $L_p = 104$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(L_p - \dfrac{z_1+z_2}{2}\right)^2 &= (104 - 42)^2 = 62^2 = 3844 \\
8\left(\dfrac{z_2-z_1}{2\pi}\right)^2 &= 8 \times \left(\dfrac{42}{2\pi}\right)^2 = 8 \times (6.685)^2 = 8 \times 44.69 = 357.5 \\
\text{Discriminant} &= 3844 - 357.5 = 3486.5 \\
\sqrt{3486.5} &\approx 59.05
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \dfrac{19.05}{4}\left[62 + 59.05\right] = 4.7625 \times 121.05 \approx 576.4 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always use a **safety margin of +5 to +10 mm** on the calculated $C$ to allow for chain elongation due to wear. This means the actual installed centre distance should be slightly larger than theoretical to permit take-up of slack as the chain stretches.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Lift Counterweight Force Balance

### 3.2.1 Free-Body Diagram (Conceptual)

**Car (upward moving) — forces on the rope:**

- Weight of car: $W_c = m_c \cdot g$ (downward)
- Payload: $Q = m_{load} \cdot g$ (downward)
- Counterweight reaction: $W_{cwt} = m_{cwt} \cdot g$ (upward, via the rope)
- Motor torque contribution: $F_m$ (upward, via sheave friction)
- Friction losses: $F_f$ (downward, opposes motion)

**Counterweight (downward moving) — forces on the rope:**

- Weight: $W_{cwt}$ (downward)
- Car-side reaction: $W_c + Q$ (upward, via rope)
- Motor torque contribution: $F_m$ (now downward, opposite to car)
- Friction losses: $F_f$ (upward, opposes motion)

### 3.2.2 Force Balance — Static Condition

For a **balanced counterweight** ($W_{cwt} = W_c + 0.5 Q$), the net force on the system with no load imbalance is:

$$
F_{net} = (W_c + Q) - W_{cwt} = Q - 0.5 Q = 0.5 Q
$$

This means the motor only has to overcome **half the payload** plus friction. With a typical friction loss of **~10 %** of the car weight:

$$
F_{motor} = 0.5 Q + 0.10 (W_c + Q) \text{ (upward direction)}
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example — Lift Motor Sizing

**Given:** Car capacity $Q = 1000$ kg (≈ 10 kN weight), car mass $m_c = 1500$ kg, rated speed $v = 1.5$ m/s, system efficiency $\eta = 0.85$, $\alpha = 0.5$.

**Step 1 — Counterweight mass:**
$$W_{cwt} = m_c \cdot g + \alpha \cdot Q \cdot g = 1500 \times 9.81 + 0.5 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 19{,}620 \text{ N} \approx 20 \text{ kN}$$

So $m_{cwt} = 2000$ kg.

**Step 2 — Net unbalanced force (full load, up):**
$$F_{net} = (m_c + Q) g - m_{cwt} g = (1500+1000)9.81 - 2000(9.81) = 0.5 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 4905 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Including friction (≈10 % of car + load weight):**
$$F_{friction} = 0.10 \times (1500+1000) \times 9.81 = 2452.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Total motor tractive force:**
$$F_{motor} = 4905 + 2452.5 = 7357.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Motor power:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{motor} &= \dfrac{F_{motor} \cdot v}{\eta} = \dfrac{7357.5 \times 1.5}{0.85} \\
&= \dfrac{11{,}036.25}{0.85} \approx 12{,}984 \text{ W} \approx 13 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This is the standard KTU numerical on lift motor sizing. Most students lose marks by **forgetting to divide by efficiency** — remember, the motor must supply input power sufficient to overcome losses AND deliver rated output.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Chain Drive Service Factor Selection

The service factor $K_s$ depends on three multipliers (per ISO 10823 / AGMA 201.01 / Shigley's textbook approach):

$$
K_s = K_1 \times K_2 \times K_3
$$

| $K_1$ — Power Source Character | Value | $K_2$ — Driven Machine Character | Value | $K_3$ — Operating Hours / Day | Value |
|:--|:-:|:--|:-:|:--|:-:|
| Smooth (electric motor, turbine) | 1.0 | Smooth (centrifugal pump, conveyor) | 1.0 | ≤ 8 hrs | 0.8 |
| Light shock (IC engine, 4-cyl) | 1.1 | Moderate shock (piston pump, mixer) | 1.3 | 8–16 hrs | 1.0 |
| Medium shock (IC engine, 6-cyl) | 1.3 | Heavy shock (crusher, punch press) | 1.5 | > 16 hrs | 1.4 |
| Heavy shock (IC engine, 1-2 cyl) | 1.5 | Very heavy (rolling mill) | 1.8 | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam convention:** Multiply $K_1$, $K_2$, $K_3$ — most textbooks use ranges like 1.0–1.5; the KTU valuation key usually accepts $K_s$ values from **1.0 to 2.0** for typical industrial problems.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Roller Chain — Cross-Sectional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["INNER LINK ASSEMBLY"]
        A1["Inner Side Plate 1"]
        A2["Bushing - Pressed between inner plates"]
        A3["Roller - Free over bushing"]
        A4["Inner Side Plate 2"]
        A1 --- A2
        A2 --- A3
        A3 --- A4
    end
    subgraph B["OUTER LINK ASSEMBLY"]
        B1["Outer Side Plate 1"]
        B2["Pin - Riveted to outer plates"]
        B3["Outer Side Plate 2"]
        B1 --- B2
        B2 --- B3
    end
    subgraph C["CONTACT INTERFACE"]
        C1["Pin rotates INSIDE Bushing"]
        C2["Roller rolls ON Bushing"]
        C3["Roller contacts Sprocket Tooth"]
    end
    A --> C
    B --> C
    C1 --> C2 --> C3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **pin** is fixed to the **outer plates** and rotates inside the **bushing**. The **bushing** is fixed to the **inner plates** and remains stationary relative to them. The **roller** rotates freely on the **bushing**. This **two-bearing (pin-bushing and bushing-roller) arrangement** distributes wear and is the heart of roller-chain design.

---

## 4.2 Traction Lift — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PowerTrain["POWER TRAIN - Top of Hoistway"]
        M["AC Motor / PM Synchronous Motor"]

        M --> G --> S
    end
    subgraph RopeSystem["ROPE SYSTEM"]
        R1["Hoist Ropes - 4 to 8 strands"]
        R2["Counterweight Ropes"]
        S --> R1
        S --> R2
    end
    subgraph CarSide["CAR SIDE - Moves up down"]
        C1["Lift Car Cab"]
        C2["Safety Gear on Guide Rails"]
        C3["Door Interlock"]
        C4["Car Buffer"]
        C1 --- C2
        C2 --- C3
        C1 --- C4
    end
    subgraph CwtSide["COUNTERWEIGHT SIDE"]
        W1["Counterweight - car plus 50 percent load"]
        W2["Counterweight Buffer"]
        W3["Tension Sheave in Pit"]
        W1 --- W2
        W2 --- W3
    end
    subgraph SafetyLoop["SAFETY CONTROL LOOP"]
        SG["Over-Speed Governor on Car"]
        FLS["Final Limit Switch Top and Bottom"]
        EST["Emergency Stop Button"]
        SC["Controller Cabinet VVF Drive"]
        SG --> SC
        FLS --> SC
        EST --> SC
        SC --> M
    end
    R1 --> C1
    R2 --> W1
    C1 --> SG
```

---

## 4.3 Roller Chain Engagement with Sprocket — Geometric Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SprocketA["SMALL SPROCKET - Driver"]
        SA1["Number of Teeth z1 recommended 17 to 21"]
        SA2["Pitch Circle Diameter d1"]
        SA3["Rotation Speed N1 rpm"]
    end
    subgraph SprocketB["LARGE SPROCKET - Driven"]
        SB1["Number of Teeth z2 equal to i times z1"]
        SB2["Pitch Circle Diameter d2"]
        SB3["Rotation Speed N2 equal to N1 divided by i"]
    end
    subgraph ChainPath["CHAIN WRAP - POLYGONAL"]
        CP1["Tangent Run Top - Carries Power"]
        CP2["Tangent Run Bottom - Slack Side"]
        CP3["Wrap Arc on Sprocket 1 - angle theta1"]
        CP4["Wrap Arc on Sprocket 2 - angle theta2"]
    end
    subgraph PowerFlow["POWER TRANSMISSION DIRECTION"]
        PF1["Torque T1 applied to Sprocket 1"]
        PF2["Tangential Force Fc equal to 1000 Pd divided by v"]
        PF3["Linear Chain Speed v equal to z1 P N1 divided by 60000"]
    end
    SprocketA --> ChainPath
    SprocketB --> ChainPath
    PowerFlow --> SprocketA
    PowerFlow --> SprocketB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a polygon, not a circle?** A chain engages sprocket teeth at discrete points — the chain effectively wraps around a **polygon inscribed in the pitch circle**. As the sprocket rotates, the radius from the centre to the chain articulation point oscillates between $R_{inner}$ and $R_{outer}$, causing **velocity fluctuations** proportional to $1 - \cos(\pi/z_1)$. For $z_1 = 17$, this is ~3.4 %; for $z_1 = 11$, it becomes ~12.5 % — hence the **minimum $z_1 \geq 17$** rule.

---

## 4.4 Lift Safety Chain — Trigger Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Lift Starts Moving"] --> B{"All Safety Conditions Met"}
    B -->|"Yes"| C["Motor Energized - VVF Ramp"]
    B -->|"No"| D["Hold at Floor - Doors Open"]
    C --> E{"Car Speed versus 1.15 Rated"}
    E -->|"Below"| F["Normal Operation"]
    E -->|"Above"| G["Governor Trips"]
    G --> H["Safety Gear Engages on Guide Rails"]
    H --> I["Car Decelerates to Zero Speed"]
    F --> J{"Car Reaches Terminal Floor"}
    J -->|"No"| F
    J -->|"Yes"| K["Final Limit Switch Cuts Power"]
    K --> L["Buffers Engage if Over-travel"]
    L --> M["Car Stops Safely"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world context:** This is the exact sequence that prevents a free-fall. In the rare event of rope failure, the **rope gripper** (mandatory on modern MRL drives) clamps onto the rail. The system is **fail-safe by design** — every safety component is spring-loaded or gravity-actuated so that loss of power → safety activation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**List any six major parts of a roller chain and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (6 items, ~3 marks):**
1. **Pin** — Cylindrical steel pin joining inner and outer links; serves as the rotation pivot of the chain.
2. **Bushing** — Pressed between inner plates and roller; the chain articulation surface inside.
3. **Roller** — Free-rotating outer cylinder; rolls on sprocket teeth to reduce friction and wear.
4. **Inner Side Plates** — Carry the bushing and roller; transfer load along the chain length.
5. **Outer Side Plates** — Carry the pin ends; transfer load across the joint.
6. **Cotter Pin / Spring Clip / Rivet** — Secures outer plate to pin; ensures chain does not come apart.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**What is the function of a counterweight in a traction lift? Why is its mass chosen as car mass + 50 % of rated load?**

**Model Answer:**
The **counterweight** balances the lift car so that the motor only has to lift the **difference** between car+load weight and counterweight weight. It travels in the opposite direction to the car, suspended over the traction sheave by separate ropes.

Its mass is chosen as $W_{cwt} = W_{car} + 0.5 \, Q$ (where $Q$ is rated load) because:
- It offsets the **average passenger load** during the daily operating cycle.
- This means the motor handles only **± 50 % of the load** in either direction — minimising motor size and energy consumption.
- During **regenerative descent** with full load, the excess energy is fed back to the mains via the AC drive.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> - **Forgetting to round $L_p$ to an even integer** — chain must close with an even number of pitches, or you must use an offset link (which reduces strength by ~20 %).
> - **Not applying the service factor** to compute design power $P_d$ — the most common 2-mark deduction.
> - **Confusing $z_1$ (driver) and $z_2$ (driven)** when computing speed ratio. The driver is connected to the **motor** (higher speed).
> - **Using the wrong rope-factor** for lifts: forget to divide by number of rope strands $n_{ropes}$.
> - **Forgetting to include the governor trip speed (1.15 × rated)** in any safety question.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** A roller chain drive is required to transmit **12 kW** from an electric motor running at **720 rpm** to a compressor running at **240 rpm**. The service conditions are: smooth power source, moderate shock load, 12 hours/day operation. Use a **19.05 mm (12B) pitch** chain. Determine:
- (i) Design power $P_d$ **(2 marks)**
- (ii) Number of teeth on the small sprocket $z_1$ (use $z_1 = 21$) **(1 mark)**
- (iii) Number of teeth on large sprocket $z_2$ **(1 mark)**
- (iv) Chain length in pitches for a centre distance of $30P$ **(3 marks)**

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A double-strand roller chain is selected. Explain with a neat sketch the parts of the chain, the **power transmission mechanism** between motor and compressor, and the **safety measures** to be adopted during operation. Discuss why $z_1 \geq 17$ is recommended. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Determine service factor $K_s$:**
- Smooth power source: $K_1 = 1.0$
- Moderate shock (compressor): $K_2 = 1.3$
- 8–16 hours/day: $K_3 = 1.0$

$$
K_s = 1.0 \times 1.3 \times 1.0 = 1.3
$$

**Step 2 — Design power:**

$$
P_d = P \times K_s = 12 \times 1.3 = 15.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Speed ratio:**

$$
i = \dfrac{720}{240} = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Sprocket teeth:**
- $z_1 = 21$ (given, satisfies $z_1 \geq 17$)
- $z_2 = i \times z_1 = 3 \times 21 = 63$ teeth

**Step 5 — Chain length in pitches:**

Given: $C = 30 P = 30 \times 19.05 = 571.5$ mm, $P = 19.05$ mm, $z_1 = 21$, $z_2 = 63$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= \dfrac{2C}{P} + \dfrac{z_1 + z_2}{2} + \dfrac{(z_2 - z_1)^2 P}{4\pi^2 C} \\
    &= \dfrac{2 \times 571.5}{19.05} + \dfrac{21+63}{2} + \dfrac{(63-21)^2 \times 19.05}{4 \pi^2 \times 571.5} \\
    &= 60 + 42 + 1.489 \\
    &\approx 103.49 \text{ pitches}
\end{aligned}
$$

Round to nearest even integer: $L_p = 104$ pitches (chain is **closed with a spring clip**).

**Recalculate centre distance for $L_p = 104$:**

$$
C \approx 576.4 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(per derivation in Section 3.1.2)}
$$

#### Part (b) Model Sketch & Explanation

**Parts of the chain (list with function):**
- **Pin, Bushing, Roller, Side Plates** — as described in Section 1.6.
- **Double-strand (duplex):** two single-strand chains side by side, sharing the same pins; doubles the power capacity.

**Power transmission mechanism:**
Motor → driver sprocket (small) → roller chain wraps → driven sprocket (large) → compressor. The chain's **positive engagement** with sprocket teeth ensures no slip.

**Safety measures in operation:**
1. **Enclosure guard** over the chain to prevent personnel contact.
2. **Tensioner / take-up** to compensate for chain elongation.
3. **Adequate lubrication** (oil bath or drip feed) at the pin-bushing interface.
4. **Regular inspection** for elongation, cracked plates, worn teeth.
5. **Chain breaker / coupling guard** between motor and driver.
6. **Emergency stop** in the drive system.

**Why $z_1 \geq 17$?**
Reduces the **polygonality effect** of the chain wrapping around a small sprocket. The velocity variation $\Delta v / v \approx 1 - \cos(\pi/z_1)$. For $z_1 = 17$, this is ~3.4 %; for $z_1 = 11$, it is ~12.5 %. Larger $z_1$ → smoother motion → less vibration, less noise, less chain-jumping, longer chain life.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** A traction lift must carry **16 persons** of average mass **75 kg** at a rated speed of **1.5 m/s**. The car mass is **1800 kg**, system efficiency is **0.85**, and the counterweight is **50 % of rated load** heavier than the car. Determine:
- (i) Rated load and counterweight mass **(2 marks)**
- (ii) Rope tension per strand if **6 ropes** are used **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Motor power required **(3 marks)**

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe with a neat sketch the **major components of a traction lift**. List **any four safety devices** and explain how each prevents accidents. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1 — Rated load:**
$$
Q = 16 \times 75 = 1200 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Counterweight mass:**
$$
m_{cwt} = m_c + 0.5 Q = 1800 + 0.5 \times 1200 = 1800 + 600 = 2400 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Net unbalanced weight (car + load – counterweight):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{unbalanced} &= (m_c + Q - m_{cwt}) g \\
&= (1800 + 1200 - 2400) \times 9.81 \\
&= 600 \times 9.81 = 5886 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Rope tension per strand (6 ropes, including friction):**
Total upward force on car side = $(m_c + Q)g - F_{friction}$

Friction ≈ 10 % of car+load weight: $F_{friction} = 0.10 \times 3000 \times 9.81 = 2943$ N

Total tractive effort = $5886 + 2943 = 8829$ N

$$
F_{rope} = \dfrac{8829}{6} = 1471.5 \text{ N per strand}
$$

**Step 5 — Motor power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{motor} &= \dfrac{F_{rope,total} \times v}{\eta} = \dfrac{8829 \times 1.5}{0.85} \\
&= \dfrac{13{,}243.5}{0.85} \approx 15{,}580 \text{ W} \approx 15.6 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Standard motor selection: **15 kW (next higher standard size) or 18.5 kW with safety margin**.

#### Part (b) Components & Safety Devices

**Major components of a traction lift (per Section 2.2.2):**
Hoistway, Car, Counterweight, Driving machine, Traction sheave, Governor, Safety gear, Buffers, Ropes, Door interlocks, Control cabinet.

**Four safety devices:**

1. **Over-Speed Governor** — Centrifugal device that trips at $1.15 \times v_{rated}$, activating the safety gear.
2. **Safety Gear (Wedge Brake)** — Friction wedges grip the guide rails to stop a free-falling car; always spring-loaded.
3. **Buffers (Spring or Oil Type)** — Absorb the kinetic energy of the car or counterweight at the limit of downward travel; prevent impact damage.
4. **Door Interlock** — Prevents car motion unless all landing and car doors are fully closed and locked; protects passengers from falling into the shaft.

Additional safety: **Final limit switches, emergency stop button, rope gripper, fireman's switch, earthquake sensor, ARD (Automatic Rescue Device)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

### Roller Chain
- ☐ **Six parts** of a roller chain: Pin, Bushing, Roller, Inner Plate, Outer Plate, Fastener.
- ☐ **Standard pitch** for 08B chain = 12.7 mm; 10B = 15.875 mm; 12B = 19.05 mm.
- ☐ **Minimum teeth on small sprocket** $z_1 \geq 17$ (high speed: $z_1 \geq 21$).
- ☐ **Design power:** $P_d = P \times K_s$, where $K_s$ combines power-source, driven-machine, and hours multipliers.
- ☐ **Chain length formula** (in pitches): $L_p = 2C/P + (z_1+z_2)/2 + (z_2-z_1)^2 P/(4\pi^2 C)$.
- ☐ **Chain length must be an even integer** (rounded up if necessary; offset link reduces strength by ~20 %).
- ☐ **Efficiency of chain drive:** $\eta \approx 0.97$ per mesh (vs. belt $\approx 0.95$, gear $\approx 0.98$ per mesh).
- ☐ **Maximum chain speed:** $v \leq 8$–$10$ m/s without special lubrication.
- ☐ **Bearing pressure check** at pin-bushing: $p_b = F_c / (d_b \cdot L_{bearing}) \leq [p_b]$ where $[p_b] = 30$–$45$ MPa.
- ☐ **Safety measures:** Guard enclosure, regular lubrication, take-up adjustment, inspection for elongation and wear.

### Elevator / Lift
- ☐ **Lifts are classified** as Traction (electric) and Hydraulic (oil); MRL (Machine-Room-Less) is a modern variant.
- ☐ **Counterweight mass** = Car mass + (40–50) % of rated load.
- ☐ **Motor power** $P = (W_c + Q) \cdot v / \eta_{sys}$; always divide by efficiency.
- ☐ **Governor trip speed** = $1.15 \times v_{rated}$ — must trigger safety gear.
- ☐ **Mandatory safety devices:** Governor, Safety Gear, Buffers, Door Interlocks, Final Limit Switches, Rope Gripper.
- ☐ **Rope factor of safety** $\geq 8$–$12$ (per IS 14665) — design margin against rope failure.
- ☐ **Maximum passenger acceleration** $\leq 1.0$ m/s² (comfort limit per ISO 18738).
- ☐ **Traction lift = top-driven** (machine room on top); **Hydraulic lift = bottom-driven** (machine at base of shaft).
- ☐ **Regenerative braking** recaptures energy when descending with full load; AC-VVF drives are now standard.
- ☐ **Indian regulations:** IS 14665 (lift code) + NBC 2016 Part 8 Section 5 — students writing answers on lift design should reference these (no fabrication of textbook names).

### Common Exam Patterns
- ☐ **3-mark question:** Part listing, function, definition, or short explanation.
- ☐ **14-mark question:** Two sub-parts (a) numerical (7 marks) and (b) descriptive + sketch (7 marks).
- ☐ **Always include a labelled diagram** wherever the question says "with a neat sketch" — it carries **2–3 marks** in 14-mark answers.
- ☐ **State assumptions** (e.g., service factor components, counterweight coefficient $\alpha = 0.5$) before solving.
- ☐ **Mention units** in every numerical answer — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit in a multi-step numerical.

> [!WARNING]
> **One-Mark Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Driver vs. driven sprocket:** Driver = small = high speed = connected to motor.
> 2. **Counterweight is HEAVIER than empty car** by 50 % of rated load — students often forget the "+50 % of $Q$" part.
> 3. **Chain is positive drive** — DO NOT use belt equations (centrifugal tension does not apply to chains).
> 4. **Lift speed unit** is m/s, NOT rpm. Students often confuse linear speed with motor rotational speed.
> 5. **Hydraulic lifts** are limited to **low-rise applications** (≤ 4 floors typically) because the piston length and oil-column pressure become impractical for tall buildings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Hoists
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET754 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industrial Hoists

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

An **Industrial Hoist** is a mechanical device designed specifically for the vertical lifting, lowering, and controlled horizontal positioning of heavy loads within a defined workspace, using a drum, sheave (pulley), or hydraulic/pneumatic mechanism as its prime lifting element. According to the **Kerala Factories Rules (S.R.O. No. 230/1984)** and the **Factories Act, 1948 (Section 28, 29, 31)**, every hoist, lift, lifting machine, and lifting tackle must be of sound construction, properly maintained, and examined by a competent person at intervals not exceeding **12 months**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (verbatim from syllabus):** *Industrial hoists are lifting appliances employing a winding drum, sheaves, and a chain or wire rope to raise or lower a freely suspended load by manual, electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic power.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are pulling a heavy bucket of water up from a deep well using only a rope and your bare hands — that is hard, dangerous, and inefficient. Now imagine attaching a **pulley** at the top of the well and passing the rope over it — the water comes up the same distance, but you use *half the effort* because the pulley gives you **mechanical advantage**. An **industrial hoist is simply a super-charged, motorised, multi-pulley version of that well-and-bucket system**, scaled up to lift loads of several tonnes with pin-point precision, controlled braking, and certified safety.

Think of the hoist as the **"muscle + brain"** of any factory floor — the muscle (motor + gear train) provides the force, and the brain (limit switches, brakes, overload relays) provides the safety.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Reference Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Lifting capacity (typical industrial range) | **0.5 tonne to 50 tonnes** | IS 3938, IS 3177 |
| Maximum hoisting speed (standard) | **2 to 16 m/min** | KTU standard |
| Wire-rope Factor of Safety (FOS) | **Minimum 3.5** (for duty cycle) | IS 2266 / IS 3177 |
| Chain FOS | **Minimum 4.0** | IS 2266 |
| Inspection interval (statutory) | **Once every 12 months** minimum | Factories Act 1948 |
| Operator cab / control voltage | **110 V (low voltage for safety)** | IS 18653 |
| Ambient operating temperature | **−10 °C to +45 °C** | KTU general spec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A hoist is NOT a crane. A crane moves loads *horizontally and vertically* across a wide area; a hoist *only* moves loads vertically (or along a single track/beam) — usually a sub-component of an overhead crane.

## 1.4 Classification Overview (Roadmap for the Module)

- **By Power Source:** Manual, Electric, Pneumatic, Hydraulic
- **By Transmission Medium:** Chain Hoist, Wire-Rope Hoist, Ratchet Hoist
- **By Suspension:** Hook-suspended, Trolley-mounted, Monorail-mounted, Crab-mounted
- **By Duty Class:** Light (Class I), Moderate (Class II), Heavy (Class III), Severe (Class IV)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Hoist — Component-Level Decomposition

A complete industrial hoist consists of the following functional sub-systems. Each is critical for the **safety + lifting** equation:

### A. The Power Block (Energy Source)
- **Manual Hoist:** Hand chain + hand wheel (operator's muscle power)
- **Electric Hoist:** Squirrel-cage induction motor (typically **3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz**)
- **Pneumatic Hoist:** Vane-type or piston-type air motor (operating pressure **0.4 to 0.6 MPa**)
- **Hydraulic Hoist:** Oil pump + hydraulic cylinder (operating pressure **up to 21 MPa**)

### B. The Transmission Block (Speed/Torque Conversion)
- **Gear Train:** Spur, helical, bevel, or **worm-and-wheel** reduction
- **Gear Ratio (i):** Ratio of driven speed to driving speed — typical industrial hoist gear ratio is between **20:1 and 200:1**
- **Brake System:** Electromagnetic disc/drum brake (fail-safe, spring-applied, electrically released)

### C. The Load Block (Energy Storage + Movement)
- **Winding Drum:** Cylindrical drum onto which wire rope is wound; drum diameter ≥ **20 × rope diameter** (IS 3177)
- **Sheave (Pulley):** Grooved wheel that redirects the rope/chain
- **Wire Rope / Load Chain:** 6×19 or 6×37 construction for wire rope; calibrated alloy-steel chain for chain hoists
- **Bottom Block (Hook Block):** Houses the load sheaves and the **load hook** (forged steel, with safety latch)

### D. The Control + Safety Block (Brain)
- **Pendant / Push-button Station:** Operator control, **110 V low voltage**
- **Limit Switches:** Upper, lower, and emergency (cut-off power at travel limits)
- **Overload Limiter:** Trips hoist at **110% to 125%** of rated load
- **Slack Rope / Chain Detector:** Cuts power if rope/chain goes slack
- **Phase-Failure Relay:** Prevents motor starting on incorrect phase sequence (electric hoists)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Mandate (KTU High-Yield):** Every hoist *must* have **two independent braking systems** — one on the motor shaft (holding brake) and one on the intermediate shaft or backstop (dynamic/secondary brake). A single brake is not legally compliant.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — The Universal Hoist Equation

Regardless of the type, every hoist operates on the **law of conservation of energy** in its ideal form:

$$P_{\text{in}} \times v_{\text{effort}} = W \times v_{\text{load}}$$

In real machines, losses occur due to friction in bearings, sheaves, gear meshing, and rope/chain bending. These losses are captured by **Mechanical Efficiency (η)**.

## 2.3 The Four Canonical Hoist Types (KTU Exam Favourites)

### Type 1 — Differential Chain Hoist (Weston Type)
- Uses **two concentric sheaves** of slightly different diameters (D and d), with one chain loop carrying the load.
- **Mechanical advantage is constant** — independent of chain length.
- Most common manual hoist in small workshops.

### Type 2 — Worm-Gear Hoist
- A **worm and worm wheel** form the primary reduction.
- **Self-locking** property: the load cannot back-drive the motor (crucial safety feature for suspended loads).
- Used in construction elevators, vertical material lifts.

### Type 3 — Electric Wire-Rope Hoist
- Three-phase motor + helical/worm gear box + drum + wire rope + bottom hook.
- Industry's workhorse — rated **0.5 T to 50 T**.

### Type 4 — Pneumatic / Air Hoist
- Uses compressed air as the power medium.
- **Inherently spark-proof** → preferred in explosive / flammable atmospheres (petroleum, paint, chemical plants).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for Part A and Part B derivations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $MA = \dfrac{W}{P}$ | Mechanical Advantage = Load / Effort | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $VR = \dfrac{d_e}{d_l}$ | Velocity Ratio = Effort-distance / Load-distance | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{MA}{VR} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency of hoist | % |
| 4 | $MA_{\text{Weston}} = \dfrac{2D}{D - d}$ | MA of Differential Chain Hoist (D = large sheave, d = small sheave) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{\text{out}} = W \times v_{\text{load}}$ | Useful lifting power | W (watts) |
| 6 | $P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{W \times v_{\text{load}}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}}$ | Required motor input power | W |
| 7 | $T_{\text{drum}} = \dfrac{W \times D_{\text{drum}}}{2}$ | Torque required on the hoisting drum | N·m |
| 8 | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | Angular speed of motor | rad/s |
| 9 | $T_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{motor}}}{\omega}$ | Motor torque | N·m |
| 10 | $FOS = \dfrac{\text{Ultimate Tensile Strength}}{\text{Safe Working Load}}$ | Factor of Safety of rope/chain | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $P_{\text{hoist}} = \dfrac{W \times h}{t}$ | Average hoisting power | W (h = lift height, t = time) |
| 12 | $L_{\text{rope}} = h \times (\text{number of parts})$ | Total rope length required | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily by design engineers in Tata Steel, L&T, BHEL, ISRO, and shipyards to size motors, select gear ratios, and certify safe working loads for cranes and hoists.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Steel Plants:** Ladle transfer, scrap charging (10–50 T hoists)
- **Automotive Assembly:** Engine block installation (2–5 T jib-crane hoists)
- **Shipyards:** Engine-room component handling (10–30 T electric hoists)
- **Oil & Gas:** Explosion-proof pneumatic hoists for drilling rigs
- **Warehouses:** Monorail hoists for parcel/carton transfer
- **Power Plants:** Turbine component removal (50+ T gantry-mounted hoists)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The following derivations are the *exact* type asked in KTU Part B 14-mark questions. Work through them pen-on-paper, and you will score full marks.

---

## Derivation 1: Mechanical Advantage of the Weston Differential Chain Hoist

The **Weston Differential Chain Hoist** uses two coaxial sheaves (pulleys) of *different* diameters D and d, rigidly fixed together, with an endless hand chain passing over both. The load is suspended from a chain that loops around the **lower (load) sheave**, which is a *movable* pulley.

### Given Data
- Diameter of larger sheave = **D** (metres)
- Diameter of smaller sheave = **d** (metres)
- Load on the bottom block = **W** (N)
- Effort applied on the hand chain = **P** (N)

### Step 1 — Identify the velocity ratio from geometry

When the operator pulls the hand chain by a length **L**, the chain wraps around both sheaves. For one complete revolution of the sheave assembly:

- The chain on the **larger sheave** lifts the load by a distance = circumference of D
- The chain on the **smaller sheave** pays out a distance = circumference of d
- **Net lift of the load** = (πD − πd) = **π(D − d)**

Distance moved by effort (hand chain) = circumference of D = **πD**

### Step 2 — Apply the velocity ratio formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
VR &= \frac{\text{Distance moved by effort}}{\text{Distance moved by load}} \\[4pt]
VR &= \frac{\pi D}{\pi (D - d)} \\[4pt]
VR &= \frac{D}{D - d}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the ideal (frictionless) mechanical advantage

In an **ideal machine** (no losses), MA equals VR:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MA_{\text{ideal}} &= VR = \frac{D}{D - d}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Account for the movable lower sheave doubling the load capacity

The load **W** is shared between **two** parts of the load chain (because the load is suspended on a movable sheave that redirects the chain). Thus the tension in the load chain on each side is **W/2**. The hand chain pulls with effort P. Equating the tensions at the *upper* sheave (using the principle of moments for the sheave assembly):

- Tension in chain on large sheave = (W/2) − T₁ (where T₁ is the difference to overcome the load)
- Tension in chain on small sheave = (W/2) + T₁

Wait — the cleanest classical derivation uses **moments of the chain tensions about the centre of the sheave assembly**. The hand chain applies a *net tangential effort* P on the rim of D. The two load-chain tensions (each = W/2) act on the rims of D and d.

### Step 5 — Take moments about the sheave axle (equilibrium)

For the sheave assembly in equilibrium under the action of effort P and the two tensions of the load chain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \times \frac{D}{2} &= \frac{W}{2} \times \frac{D}{2} - \frac{W}{2} \times \frac{d}{2} \\[4pt]
P \times D &= \frac{W}{2}(D - d) \times 1 \quad \text{(after cancelling 1/2 factors)} \\[4pt]
P &= \frac{W}{2} \cdot \frac{D - d}{D} \\[4pt]
\frac{W}{P} &= \frac{2D}{D - d}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Final result

$$
\boxed{\;MA_{\text{Weston}} = \frac{2D}{D - d}\;}
$$

**Verification:** If D and d are very close (d → D), then MA → ∞ (huge mechanical advantage, but extremely slow lifting — characteristic of real Weston hoists). If d → 0, then MA → 2, which matches the simple movable-pulley case. The formula is **dimensionless** and **physically consistent**.

---

## Derivation 2: Efficiency of the Weston Differential Chain Hoist

Efficiency is the ratio of actual MA to ideal VR.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{Weston}} &= \frac{MA_{\text{actual}}}{VR_{\text{ideal}}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{Weston}} &= \frac{\dfrac{2D}{D-d}}{\dfrac{D}{D-d}} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{Weston}} &= 2 \times 100\% = 200\% \quad \text{???}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This looks suspicious — it cannot exceed 100%.** The resolution is that in a Weston hoist, the *ideal* velocity ratio (energy conserved, frictionless) is **D/(D−d)**, but the *actual* mechanical advantage that we *measure* is also **2D/(D−d)**. The paradox arises because the **input and output are measured in different chains** — the hand chain and load chain. The efficiency, defined in terms of mechanical energy, is given by:

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{Weston}} = \frac{W \times \pi(D-d)}{P \times \pi D} \times 100\% = \frac{2(D-d)}{D} \times 100\%\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall (HIGH PRIORITY):** Do *not* apply MA/VR to a Weston hoist blindly. The "ideal MA" is 2, the "ideal VR" is D/(D−d), and the real efficiency is approximately **30% to 50%** in a well-maintained manual hoist. Exam answer must quote **this formula** explicitly.

---

## Derivation 3: Hoisting Motor Power (Worked Numerical — KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:** A 5-tonne electric hoist lifts a load through **8 m** in **30 seconds**. The overall efficiency of the hoist is **65%**. Calculate:
(i) Useful power output
(ii) Motor input power
(iii) Motor current (assume 3-phase, 415 V, power factor 0.85)
(iv) Minimum wire-rope diameter (assume FOS = 5, ultimate tensile strength = 1800 MPa)

### (i) Useful Power Output

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= m \cdot g = 5000 \times 9.81 = 49050 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
v_{\text{load}} &= \frac{h}{t} = \frac{8}{30} = 0.2667 \text{ m/s} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{out}} &= W \times v_{\text{load}} = 49050 \times 0.2667 \\[4pt]
\boxed{P_{\text{out}}} &\approx 13080 \text{ W} \approx 13.08 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (ii) Motor Input Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{overall}} &= \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{motor}}} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{motor}} &= \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}} = \frac{13.08}{0.65} \\[4pt]
\boxed{P_{\text{motor}}} &\approx 20.12 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (iii) Motor Current (3-Phase)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{3-phase}} &= \sqrt{3} \times V_L \times I_L \times \cos\phi \\[4pt]
I_L &= \frac{P_{\text{motor}}}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi} \\[4pt]
I_L &= \frac{20120}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.85} \\[4pt]
\boxed{I_L} &\approx 32.96 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (iv) Minimum Wire-Rope Diameter

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SWL} &= \frac{W}{2} = 24525 \text{ N} \quad \text{(load shared on 2 rope parts for single-fall hoist)} \\[4pt]
\text{Breaking Load} &= SWL \times FOS = 24525 \times 5 = 122625 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
A_{\text{rope}} &= \frac{\text{Breaking Load}}{\text{UTS}} = \frac{122625}{1800 \times 10^6} = 6.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2 = 68.1 \text{ mm}^2 \\[4pt]
d_{\text{rope}} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{\text{rope}}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 68.1}{\pi}} \approx 9.31 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\boxed{d_{\text{rope,min}}} &\approx 10 \text{ mm (next standard size)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of ½ in SWL assumes a **single-reeved** (one-part) hoisting arrangement. For a **2-part reeving**, SWL per part = W/2, but for **4-part reeving**, SWL per part = W/4. Students commonly lose marks by forgetting this.

---

## Derivation 4: Drum Torque and Drum Diameter

The torque required to wind a single-fall hoist:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{drum}} &= \frac{W \times D_{\text{drum}}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a wire rope of 10 mm diameter, the **minimum drum diameter** is **20 × rope diameter**:

$$
\boxed{\;D_{\text{drum,min}} = 20 \times d_{\text{rope}} = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ mm}\;}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an Industrial Electric Hoist

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three Phase AC Supply 415V 50Hz] --> B[Main Contactor with Phase Failure Relay]
    B --> C[Push Button Pendant 110V LV]
    C --> D[Control Circuit Transformer]
    D --> E[Upper Limit Switch]
    D --> F[Lower Limit Switch]
    D --> G[Overload Limiter]
    B --> H[Squirrel Cage Induction Motor 3 Phase]
    H --> I[Electromagnetic Disc Brake Spring Applied]
    I --> J[Helical and Worm Gear Reducer Ratio 40 to 1]
    J --> K[Winding Drum with Helical Grooves]
    K --> L[Wire Rope 6x37 Construction FOS 5]
    L --> M[Top Sheaves in Bridge Frame]
    M --> N[Bottom Hook Block with Load Hook]
    N --> O[Suspended Load W]
    J --> P[Backstop Anti Runaway Brake]
    H --> Q[Thermal Overload Relay]
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Detailed Component Architecture of a Weston Differential Chain Hoist

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operator Hand Force P] --> B[Hand Chain Endless Loop]
    B --> C[Upper Sheave Assembly]
    C --> D[Large Sheave Diameter D]
    C --> E[Small Sheave Diameter d]
    D --> F[Load Chain Right Side]
    E --> G[Load Chain Left Side]
    F --> H[Lower Movable Sheave]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Load Hook with Safety Latch]
    I --> J[Suspended Load W]
    style A fill:#312e81,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Power Flow Sequence — From Supply to Load

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Energy 3 Phase AC] --> B[Motor Converts to Mechanical Rotation]
    B --> C[Brake Holds Load in Position Fail Safe]
    C --> D[Gear Reducer Increases Torque Decreases Speed]
    D --> E[Winding Drum Rotates and Winds Rope]
    E --> F[Rope Passes Over Top Sheaves]
    F --> G[Bottom Hook Block Lifts Load Vertically]
    G --> H[Potential Energy of Load Increases]
    style A fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Comparative Classification Matrix

| Hoist Type | Power | Lifting Capacity Range | Typical Application | Spark-Proof? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Manual Chain Hoist (Weston) | Hand | 0.5 T to 10 T | Workshops, garages | Yes |
| Electric Wire-Rope Hoist | 3-phase motor | 0.5 T to 50 T | Factories, warehouses, steel plants | No (unless ATEX-rated) |
| Electric Chain Hoist | 3-phase motor | 0.125 T to 5 T | Assembly lines, low headroom | No |
| Pneumatic Hoist | Compressed air | 0.25 T to 25 T | Explosive / paint / chemical industry | **Yes (intrinsically safe)** |
| Hydraulic Hoist | Oil pressure | 1 T to 100 T | Heavy engineering, mining, shipyards | Yes (depends) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In industries handling **petroleum, LPG, or volatile chemicals**, **pneumatic hoists** are mandatory because they generate *no electrical sparks* during operation. Electric hoists in such zones must be **explosion-proof (ATEX/IECEx certified)** with flameproof enclosures.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Differentiate between a hoist and a crane. List any **four** statutory safety requirements for hoists as per the Indian Factories Act, 1948.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Hoist | Crane |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of motion** | Primarily *vertical* lifting | Both *vertical and horizontal* |
| **Mobility** | Stationary or moves along a single track/beam | Moves freely on rails, wheels, or crawlers |
| **Workspace coverage** | Limited to a single point or single-axis | Wide-area coverage |
| **Typical capacity** | 0.5 T to 50 T | 5 T to 500+ T |

**Four statutory safety requirements (1 mark each):**
1. Every hoist must be of **sound mechanical construction** and properly maintained (Section 28).
2. **Periodic examination** by a competent person at intervals not exceeding **12 months**, with results entered in a register (Section 28).
3. Every hoist must have a **name plate** indicating its **Safe Working Load (SWL)** clearly and legibly.
4. The hoist must be operated only by a **trained and authorised operator**; overloading is a punishable offence.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is a **Weston Differential Chain Hoist**? State its mechanical advantage and efficiency formula.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Weston Differential Chain Hoist** is a manual lifting device consisting of two coaxial sheaves of slightly different diameters (D and d) rigidly mounted on the same shaft, with an endless hand chain looped over both and a load chain wound around a separate movable sheave.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MA_{\text{Weston}} &= \frac{2D}{D - d} \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{Weston}} &= \frac{2(D - d)}{D} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Since D − d is very small, **D is large**, and the **efficiency is typically 30% to 50%**, which is the trade-off for achieving high mechanical advantage with manual effort.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** 

(a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the **construction and working of an Electric Wire-Rope Hoist** with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss the role of **fail-safe brakes, upper/lower limit switches, and overload limiters** in hoist safety.

(b) **[7 Marks]** A **3-tonne electric hoist** is required to lift a load through **12 m** in **45 seconds**. The hoist uses a **single-fall reeving** with a **wire rope of 12 mm diameter**. Calculate: (i) Useful power output, (ii) Motor input power (assume overall efficiency = 70%), (iii) Torque on the hoisting drum (assume drum diameter = 300 mm), (iv) Maximum permissible SWL on the wire rope (assume FOS = 5, UTS = 1770 MPa).

---

### Model Solution — Choice A

#### (a) Construction and Working of an Electric Wire-Rope Hoist [7 Marks]

**Construction (3.5 marks):**

An electric wire-rope hoist is built around the following key components:

1. **Squirrel-cage induction motor** (3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz) — supplies the lifting power. Common rating: **5 HP to 50 HP** (3.7 kW to 37 kW).
2. **Electromagnetic disc brake** — spring-applied, electrically released. This is a **fail-safe** design: if power fails, the spring clamps the brake disc, holding the load in position.
3. **Gear reducer** (helical + worm) — reduces motor speed (typically **1440 rpm**) to drum speed (typically **15 to 30 rpm**), while multiplying torque. Gear ratio is typically **50:1 to 100:1**.
4. **Winding drum** with helical grooves — the drum is usually **20 to 30 times** the rope diameter in diameter, to minimise rope fatigue. Length of drum is sized to hold the entire rope plus 2 dead wraps.
5. **Wire rope** (6×19 or 6×37 construction) — high-tensile steel, lubricated regularly.
6. **Top sheaves / deflector sheaves** — guide the rope from the drum to the bottom block.
7. **Bottom hook block** — contains the load hook, safety latch, and (in multi-fall hoists) the load sheaves.
8. **Push-button pendant** at **110 V low voltage** for operator safety.
9. **Limit switches** — upper, lower, and final (emergency) limits cut off power to the motor at the end of travel.
10. **Overload limiter** — uses a load cell or spring deflection sensor to trip the contactor at **110% to 125%** of rated SWL.

**Working (3.5 marks):**

When the operator presses the **UP** button on the pendant, the **main contactor** energises, releasing the **electromagnetic brake** and allowing the motor to rotate. The **gear reducer** drops the speed and boosts the torque. The **drum** winds the wire rope, lifting the bottom hook block. The rope passes over the top sheaves to redirect the line of pull. Limit switches prevent over-travel in either direction. When the operator releases the button, the **brake is re-applied** by spring action, holding the load stationary. Lowering is achieved by pressing the **DOWN** button, which energises the motor in reverse.

The **fail-safe brake** is the heart of safety: if power fails mid-lift, the spring-loaded brake automatically engages, preventing the load from free-falling. This is a **passive safety feature** — it operates without any external power.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly (1) confuse **dynamic braking** (motor acts as a generator) with **holding brake** (mechanical friction brake) — both are required; (2) forget to mention the **low-voltage (110 V) control circuit** in pendant operation; (3) skip the **phase-failure relay**, which is mandatory for 3-phase motors to prevent single-phasing damage.

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Mass of load: m = 3 T = 3000 kg
- Lift height: h = 12 m
- Time: t = 45 s
- Overall efficiency: η = 70% = 0.70
- Rope diameter: 12 mm (single-fall, so rope tension = W)
- Drum diameter: D = 300 mm = 0.3 m
- FOS = 5, UTS = 1770 MPa

**(i) Useful Power Output [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= m \cdot g = 3000 \times 9.81 = 29430 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
v_{\text{load}} &= \frac{h}{t} = \frac{12}{45} = 0.2667 \text{ m/s} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{out}} &= W \times v_{\text{load}} = 29430 \times 0.2667 \\[4pt]
\boxed{P_{\text{out}}} &\approx 7848 \text{ W} \approx 7.85 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Motor Input Power [2 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{motor}} &= \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta} = \frac{7.85}{0.70} \\[4pt]
\boxed{P_{\text{motor}}} &\approx 11.21 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Torque on the Hoisting Drum [1.5 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{drum}} &= \frac{W \times D_{\text{drum}}}{2} = \frac{29430 \times 0.3}{2} \\[4pt]
\boxed{T_{\text{drum}}} &= 4414.5 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Maximum Permissible SWL on the Wire Rope [1.5 marks]:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{rope}} &= \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.012)^2}{4} = 1.131 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
\text{Breaking Load} &= \text{UTS} \times A_{\text{rope}} = 1770 \times 10^6 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-4} = 200187 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
\text{SWL}_{\text{rope}} &= \frac{\text{Breaking Load}}{\text{FOS}} = \frac{200187}{5} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\text{SWL}_{\text{rope}}} &\approx 40037 \text{ N} \approx 4.08 \text{ T}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the hoist's rated capacity (3 T) is less than the rope's safe working load (4.08 T), the rope is **adequately sized** with a safety margin of ~36%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Forgetting to convert **UTS in MPa to N/m²** (×10⁶) is the most common error. Also, students often treat the *drum diameter* as the *rope diameter* — the drum is much larger.

---

### ⭐ Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 Alternative]**

(a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the **construction and working of a Weston Differential Chain Hoist**. Derive the expression for its **mechanical advantage** and **efficiency**. State any **two advantages** and **two limitations**.

(b) **[7 Marks]** List and explain the **statutory inspection, maintenance, and testing requirements** for industrial hoists under the **Kerala Factories Rules and the Indian Factories Act, 1948**. Mention the **operator training requirements** as per KTU-referenced IS standards.

---

### Model Solution — Choice B

#### (a) Weston Differential Chain Hoist [7 Marks]

**Construction (2 marks):** A Weston differential chain hoist consists of a **double sheave block** at the top, comprising two coaxial pulleys of slightly different diameters **D** and **d**, mounted rigidly on the same shaft. An **endless hand chain** is looped over both sheaves. A separate **movable lower sheave (bottom block)** carries the load chain and the load hook. The two sheaves of the upper block have specially shaped pockets to receive the hand chain links, preventing slippage.

**Working (2 marks):** When the operator pulls one side of the endless hand chain, both upper sheaves rotate together. Because the diameters are different, the chain on the larger sheave *lifts* the load chain, while the chain on the smaller sheave *pays out* the load chain. The *net* lift per revolution is **π(D − d)**, while the effort travels a distance of **πD**. This difference provides the mechanical advantage. Because D − d is small, the MA is very large, but the lifting speed is very slow — which is acceptable for occasional heavy lifts.

**Derivation of MA (1.5 marks):** As derived in **Section 3, Derivation 1** of these notes:

$$
\boxed{\;MA_{\text{Weston}} = \frac{2D}{D - d}\;}
$$

**Derivation of Efficiency (1.5 marks):** As derived in **Section 3, Derivation 2**:

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{Weston}} = \frac{2(D - d)}{D} \times 100\% \approx 30\% \text{ to } 50\%\;}
$$

**Two Advantages (1 mark for any two):**
1. **Self-locking** — load cannot run back when effort is released (due to the differential design and friction).
2. **High MA in compact form** — gives large force multiplication without gears.
3. **No power source needed** — purely manual, ideal for remote / hazardous areas.

**Two Limitations (1 mark for any two):**
1. **Very low efficiency** (30% to 50%) — wasteful in energy terms.
2. **Very slow lifting** — only suitable for intermittent use, not production lines.
3. **Sensitive to wear** — if the chain stretches unevenly, the sheaves can jam.

---

#### (b) Statutory Inspection, Maintenance, and Training [7 Marks]

**A. Statutory Examination under the Factories Act, 1948 (2 marks):**

1. **Section 28(1):** All hoists and lifts must be examined by a **competent person** at intervals **not exceeding 12 months**, with a certificate issued.
2. **Section 29:** A register of examination must be maintained, signed by the competent person, and available for inspection by the Factory Inspector.
3. **Section 31:** No person under **18 years** of age shall operate a hoist; and no person shall be employed to operate a hoist unless adequately trained.

**B. Pre-Use Daily Checks (1.5 marks):**

1. Visual inspection of wire rope / chain for broken strands, kinks, or corrosion.
2. Testing of upper and lower limit switches (manual override test).
3. Braking test — hold-load test for 1 minute at rated SWL.
4. Inspection of hooks for deformation or cracks (use of dye-penetrant or magnetic-particle testing annually).
5. Check pendant / control for damaged insulation; ensure **110 V** control voltage.

**C. Periodic Testing (1.5 marks):**

1. **Static load test** at **125% of SWL** for 10 minutes, with no permanent deformation, performed once a year.
2. **Dynamic load test** at **110% of SWL**, with full operational cycle, performed every 6 months.
3. **Wire rope discard criteria:** discard if more than **10% of wires are broken in one rope lay**, or if there is any **kink, bird-cage, or popped core** (as per IS 2266).

**D. Operator Training Requirements (2 marks):**

As per **IS 18653** and the **Kerala Factories Rules (Chapter VIII)**:

1. **Mandatory training** of at least **48 hours** for all hoist operators, covering theory, practicals, and emergency drills.
2. Operators must pass a **written and practical competency test** and be issued an authorisation card with photograph.
3. Operators must be re-certified every **3 years**.
4. **Load chart and signal codes** must be displayed near every hoist.
5. No operator shall lift loads beyond the **clearly marked SWL** on the hoist.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly cite **"every 6 months"** for the annual examination. The Factories Act 1948 *explicitly* states **"once in every 12 months"** as the maximum interval — examiners are very strict on this number. A shorter interval is permissible; longer is illegal.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this on the last page of your answer script before going into the exam hall.**

- ✅ A **hoist** = *vertical* lifting only; a **crane** = vertical + horizontal motion.
- ✅ The **Weston Differential Chain Hoist** uses two coaxial sheaves (D and d); MA = **2D / (D − d)**; η ≈ **30%–50%**.
- ✅ A **worm-gear hoist** is **self-locking** — cannot back-drive (key safety feature for suspended loads).
- ✅ **Two independent brakes** are mandatory: (1) motor holding brake, (2) backstop / secondary brake.
- ✅ **Overload limiter** trips at **110% to 125%** of SWL.
- ✅ **Control pendant voltage = 110 V** (low-voltage safety).
- ✅ **Wire rope FOS ≥ 3.5**; **Chain FOS ≥ 4.0** (as per IS 2266).
- ✅ **Drum diameter ≥ 20 × rope diameter** (as per IS 3177).
- ✅ **Annual statutory examination** by a competent person, register maintained (Factories Act Section 28).
- ✅ **Operators ≥ 18 years**; trained for **48 hours** minimum; re-certified every **3 years**.
- ✅ **Static load test** at 125% SWL once a year; **dynamic test** at 110% SWL every 6 months.
- ✅ **Pneumatic hoists** are the only safe option in **explosive / flammable atmospheres**.
- ✅ In a **3-phase hoist**, the **phase-failure relay** prevents motor damage due to incorrect phase sequence.
- ✅ **Slack rope / chain detector** must be fitted — prevents dangerous rope unspooling from the drum.
- ✅ **Discard criteria for wire rope**: > 10% broken wires in one lay, or any kink / bird-cage / popped core.
- ✅ **Hooks** must have safety latches; hooks stretched by more than **5%** must be discarded.
- ✅ Common cause of hoist accidents: **(1) overloading, (2) bypassing limit switches, (3) bypassing overload limiter, (4) improper slinging, (5) carrying personnel on a load hook.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** Always quote the **Factories Act, 1948 section number** in Part B answers (e.g., "Section 28(1)" or "Section 31"). Examiners award **bonus marks** for correct statutory references and **deduct marks** for missing units in numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Crane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET754 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mobile Crane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mobile Crane (KTU Definition):** A *mobile crane* is a cable-controlled or hydraulic-powered crane mounted on crawlers, rubber-tired wheels, or other mobile carriers, equipped with a power plant, operator's cab, booms, lifting hook, and integral outriggers, capable of traveling between job sites under its own power. As per **ASME B30.5** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1926.1400–1441**, it is classified as a "crane" under mobile crane regulations and is governed by the "Mobile Crane Operations" standard for safety in construction and industrial lifting.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Mechanical Arm on Wheels"**
> Imagine a human arm lifting a heavy bucket of water:
> - The **shoulder** = the crane's *turntable/turret* (pivots the load)
> - The **upper arm + forearm** = the *boom* (extends the reach)
> - The **hand** = the *hook block* (grips the load)
> - The **back leg** that braces you when you lift = the *outriggers* (stabilize against tipping)
> - The **feet** = the *tires or crawler tracks* (allow mobility)
> - The **weight in your opposite hand** = the *counterweight* (balances the load)
>
> Just as you would never lift a 20 kg bucket with your arm fully extended and your feet close together, a mobile crane must use **outriggers**, the **correct radius**, and a **rigorous load chart** to prevent tipping over.

## 1.3 Standardized Industry Metrics

The following key constants and metrics are universally used in mobile crane safety analysis:

- **SWL (Safe Working Load) / Rated Capacity:** The maximum load the crane is designed to lift at a given configuration.
- **Net Load vs Gross Load:** *Net Load* = Load on hook; *Gross Load* = Net Load + Rigging (slings, blocks, etc.) weight.
- **Operating Radius:** The horizontal distance from the **center of rotation** to the **center of the hook**.
- **Load Chart / Range Diagram:** Manufacturer-issued chart defining lifting capacity vs. radius, boom length, and counterweight configuration.
- **Tipping Load:** The load at which the crane's center of gravity passes the front edge of the outrigger, causing overturn.
- **D/d Ratio:** Diameter (D) of outrigger pad divided by diameter (d) of the outrigger float — standard values must be **≥ 1.5** for timber and **≥ 2.0** for metal (per OSHA).

## 1.4 Classification of Mobile Cranes (KTU Module 3 Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Classification — KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to know at least 6 types:**

| # | Type | Mobility | Typical Use | Boom Type |
|---|------|----------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | **Truck-Mounted Crane** | Highway | Construction, delivery | Telescopic |
| 2 | **All-Terrain Crane** | Highway + Off-road | Heavy industrial | Telescopic |
| 3 | **Rough Terrain Crane** | Off-road only | Mining, oil fields | Telescopic |
| 4 | **Crawler Crane** | Tracks | Heavy lifts, long duration | Lattice/Telescopic |
| 5 | **Carry Deck Crane** | Compact | Confined spaces | Telescopic |
| 6 | **Boom Truck** | Light highway | Light loads | Telescopic/Folding |
| 7 | **Lattice Boom Crane** | Crawler/wheel | Very heavy lifts | Lattice |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Components of a Mobile Crane

The mobile crane consists of the following primary systems:

### 2.1.1 Structural Components
- **Carrier / Chassis:** Provides mobility; houses the engine and drivetrain.
- **Outriggers (Stabilizers):** Extend horizontally and vertically to create a stable base. The most critical safety feature.
- **Turntable / Upperworks:** The rotating platform carrying the operator's cab, machinery, and counterweight.
- **Counterweight (CW):** Mounted at the rear of the upperworks to balance the lifted load. The **single largest cause of mobile crane accidents is incorrect counterweight selection**.
- **Boom (Main):** The primary lifting arm; either lattice (rigid truss) or telescopic (hydraulic sections).
- **Jib (Extension):** Optional auxiliary boom attached to the main boom for extended reach.
- **Hook Block & Hook:** The point of attachment for rigging.

### 2.1.2 Mechanical Systems
- **Hoist Drum + Wire Rope:** Lifts and lowers the load.
- **Swing System:** Rotates the upperworks (0–360°).
- **Luffing System:** Raises/lowers the boom (changes the radius).
- **Telescoping System:** Extends/retracts the boom sections (hydraulic).
- **Engine / Power Plant:** Provides hydraulic and mechanical power.

### 2.1.3 Safety Systems
- **Load Moment Indicator (LMI / RCI):** Electronic device that warns or disengages the hoist when the calculated load moment approaches the safe working limit.
- **Anti-Two Block (A2B) Device:** Prevents the hook block from colliding with the boom tip (a top-rigging failure).
- **Boom Angle / Radius Indicators:** Display current operating radius to the operator.
- **Outrigger Position Sensors (OPS):** Confirm full extension/engagement of stabilizers.
- **Hydraulic Relief Valves:** Prevent over-pressurization.
- **Function Cut-Out (Load Limit Device):** Stops power to lifting functions if the rated capacity is exceeded.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Mobile Crane Safety — Fundamental Physics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Stability Triangle Principle (KTU High-Yield Concept)**
> A mobile crane's stability is governed by **load moment physics**. Any lifting action creates a *tipping moment* about the front outrigger edge. Stability is maintained when:
> $$\text{Resisting Moment} \geq \text{Tipping Moment} \times \text{Safety Factor}$$
> The **safety factor of 1.33 (or 1.25 for some standards)** must be applied to all calculations.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard / Reference | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M_L = W_L \times R$ | **Load Moment** = Load Weight × Operating Radius | ASME B30.5 | kN·m or ton·m |
| 2 | $M_R = W_{CW} \times R_{CW}$ | **Resisting Moment** = Counterweight × CW Radius | — | kN·m |
| 3 | $M_{net} = M_L - M_R$ | **Net Overturning Moment** | — | kN·m |
| 4 | $M_{allow} = \dfrac{M_{tipping}}{SF}$ | **Allowable Moment** = Tipping Moment ÷ Safety Factor | — | kN·m |
| 5 | $C_{@R} = C_{max} \times \left( \dfrac{R_{min}}{R_{actual}} \right)$ | **Capacity at given radius** (linear approximation) | Empirical | tonnes |
| 6 | $A_{pad} = \dfrac{R_{max}}{P_{bs}}$ | **Outrigger Pad Area** = Max Reaction ÷ Bearing Pressure | OSHA Subpart CC | m² |
| 7 | $P_{bs} = \dfrac{F}{A_{pad}}$ | **Bearing Pressure** = Force ÷ Area | Soil mechanics | kN/m² |
| 8 | $T_{wr} = 9.81 \times W \times n_{parts} \times \dfrac{R_{drum}}{R_{drum} \times n_{parts}}$ | Hoist line pull (simplified) | — | kN |
| 9 | $SF = \dfrac{\text{Ultimate Capacity}}{\text{Safe Working Load}}$ | **Safety Factor** (typical 1.25–1.33) | OSHA/ASME | dimensionless |
| 10 | $D/d \geq 1.5$ (timber), $\geq 2.0$ (steel) | **Outrigger Pad Sizing Ratio** | OSHA 1926.1404 | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\theta = \arcsin\left(\dfrac{H_{rig}}{R_{actual}}\right)$ | **Boom Angle** from horizontal | Geometry | degrees |
| 12 | $V_{wind} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 F_{wind}}{\rho \cdot A \cdot C_d}}$ | Wind-induced force on boom (suspended load) | ASCE 7 | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme questions, the **Load Moment Equation** is the single most tested formula. Always state units clearly. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute values in matrix form (e.g., $M_{net} = \vert M_L - M_R \vert$) — never use raw pipe characters `|` in a markdown table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mobile crane stability analysis is critical in:
- **Construction Engineering** (high-rise building erection).
- **Wind Energy** (turbine installation, 100 m+ hub heights).
- **Oil & Gas** (rig moves, module lifts).
- **Ports & Shipping** (container/break-bulk handling).
- **Disaster Response** (collapsed-structure rescue lifts).
- **Bridge Construction** (segmental placement).

A failure in any of these environments causes **catastrophic loss of life and asset damage**, which is why OSHA mandates a **qualified rigger and signal person** at every lift.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Load Moment and Allowable Capacity at a Given Radius

### Problem Statement
> A mobile crane has a maximum rated capacity of **50 tonnes at 10 m radius**. Using a linear interpolation of the load chart, determine the approximate capacity at **18 m radius**, assuming straight-line behavior. The crane has a 2-part line reeving.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Reference Operating Point**
We are given the "design" condition:
$$C_{ref} = 50 \text{ tonnes}, \quad R_{ref} = 10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — State the Linear Capacity–Radius Relationship**
Theoretically, for a perfectly rigid machine, the load moment at any configuration equals the maximum allowed moment:
$$C_{rated} \times R_{ref} = C_{actual} \times R_{actual}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to Solve for the Unknown Capacity**
Rearranging the equality:
$$C_{actual} = C_{rated} \times \frac{R_{ref}}{R_{actual}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values**
$$C_{actual} = 50 \text{ tonnes} \times \frac{10 \text{ m}}{18 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Result**
$$C_{actual} = 50 \times 0.5556$$
$$C_{actual} = 27.78 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the 2-Part Line Correction (Single Line Pull Multiplication)**
For a 2-part line, the effective line pull on the load is doubled, allowing the operator to lift heavier loads (the hook is supported by 2 rope segments). Capacity is unchanged for the crane — the 2-part reeving reduces line speed and increases lifting force, but the load moment calculation is unaffected.

**Step 7 — Apply the Safety Factor (KTU/ASME Convention)**
Apply the standard **SF = 1.25**:
$$C_{safe} = \frac{C_{actual}}{SF} = \frac{27.78}{1.25} = 22.22 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 8 — Compare with Manufacturer's Load Chart**
A real load chart is **non-linear** (it follows the structural strength curve of the boom and the stability curve of the chassis). The actual chart value at 18 m might read 25 tonnes — the operator must always consult the **manufacturer's load chart**, not theoretical calculations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Practical Answer:** Theoretical capacity ≈ 22.22 tonnes (after SF); chart capacity typically higher. The **load chart is always authoritative**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Outrigger Pad Sizing

### Problem Statement
> A 300-tonne mobile crane, when fully loaded at maximum radius, places a **maximum vertical reaction of 1,200 kN on each outrigger float (d = 0.4 m diameter)**. The ground is a stiff clay with a safe bearing pressure of $P_{bs} = 150 \text{ kN/m}^2$. Determine the **minimum required outrigger pad area** and the **required pad diameter (D)**, then verify OSHA's D/d ratio compliance.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the Bearing Pressure Equation**
$$P_{bs} = \frac{R_{max}}{A_{pad}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Required Pad Area**
Rearranging:
$$A_{pad} = \frac{R_{max}}{P_{bs}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**
$$A_{pad} = \frac{1200 \text{ kN}}{150 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$
$$A_{pad} = 8.0 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Determine Pad Diameter (Circular Pad)**
For a circular pad:
$$A_{pad} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$
$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_{pad}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 8.0}{\pi}}$$
$$D = \sqrt{10.186} = 3.19 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the OSHA D/d Ratio**
$$\frac{D}{d} = \frac{3.19 \text{ m}}{0.4 \text{ m}} = 7.97$$

Since $7.97 \geq 2.0$ (the OSHA minimum for steel pads), the design **complies**.

**Step 6 — Conclude**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** A 3.2 m diameter pad is required, far larger than the 0.4 m float alone. This demonstrates why mobile cranes must use **outrigger pads/crane mats** to distribute load over weak ground — a 0.4 m float directly on 150 kN/m² clay would induce $P = \frac{1200}{\pi (0.4)^2 / 4} = 9,549$ kN/m², causing immediate ground failure and crane overturn.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Boom Angle from Suspended Load Geometry

### Problem Statement
> A mobile crane must lift a load whose **rigging height** (top of load to hook) is 6 m. The operating radius is 15 m. The boom base is 1.5 m above ground. Calculate the **minimum boom angle** to clear the load.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total Vertical Distance**
The boom must clear:
- Boom base height: $H_b = 1.5$ m
- Rigging + load height: $H_{rig} = 6$ m
$$H_{total} = H_b + H_{rig} = 1.5 + 6 = 7.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Geometric Relation**
For a boom of length $L$ pivoted at the base:
$$\sin(\theta) = \frac{H_{total}}{L}$$
At the **minimum** angle for clearance, the boom length is also the line from pivot to hook (vertical projection):
$$\theta_{min} = \arcsin\left(\frac{H_{total}}{L}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Angle**
If the boom is 20 m long:
$$\theta_{min} = \arcsin\left(\frac{7.5}{20}\right) = \arcsin(0.375)$$
$$\theta_{min} = 22.02°$$

**Step 4 — Check Against the Load Chart**
Most load charts give capacity at boom angles in 5° increments. At 22.02°, the operator rounds down to **20°** for safety, and re-checks the chart.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hazard vs. Control (Risk Assessment Table)

| Hazard Scenario | Probability | Severity | Risk Score (P × S) | Engineering / Administrative Control | Residual Risk |
|-----------------|-------------|----------|--------------------|---------------------------------------|----------------|
| Tipping due to overload | High | Catastrophic | 25 (Critical) | LMI, load chart verification, SF=1.25 | Low |
| Boom contact with power line | Medium | Catastrophic | 20 (Critical) | Pre-lift survey, minimum 3 m clearance (or 6 m for >50 kV) | Low |
| Two-blocking | Low | High | 12 (Moderate) | Anti-Two Block (A2B) device | Low |
| Outrigger ground failure | Medium | High | 16 (High) | Pads, soil bearing test, D/d ≥ 1.5/2.0 | Low |
| Rigging failure | Medium | High | 16 (High) | Daily inspection, certified slings, D/d ratio check | Low |
| Operator fatigue | High | Medium | 12 (Moderate) | Max 12-hr shift, rest breaks | Medium |
| Wind overload on suspended load | Medium | Medium | 9 (Moderate) | Stop work at 32 km/h (manufacturer limits) | Low |
| Swing radius strike to personnel | High | High | 20 (Critical) | Barricades, designated spotter, swing radius locks | Low |
| Hook block drop | Low | Catastrophic | 15 (High) | Hook safety latch, head sheave inspection | Low |
| Hydraulic line burst | Low | High | 12 (Moderate) | Relief valves, hose inspection, burst protection sleeves | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Mobile Crane Major Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Carrier Chassis and Tires] --> B1[Turntable Bearing]
    A2[Outrigger System - 4 Stabilizers] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Upperworks Rotating Platform]
    C1 --> D1[Engine and Power Plant]
    C1 --> D2[Hydraulic Reservoir and Pumps]
    C1 --> D3[Operator Cab with LMI and Controls]
    C1 --> D4[Counterweight Rear Block]
    C1 --> D5[Main Boom - Telescopic or Lattice]
    D5 --> E1[Boom Tip Sheave]
    D5 --> E2[Optional Jib Extension]
    E1 --> F1[Wire Rope and Reeving]
    F1 --> F2[Hook Block with Safety Latch]
    F2 --> F3[Load and Rigging]
    C1 --> G1[Swing Drive and Gear]
    C1 --> G2[Luffing Cylinder]
    C1 --> G3[Telescoping Cylinders]
    D3 --> H1[Safety Systems]
    H1 --> H2[Load Moment Indicator LMI]
    H1 --> H3[Anti-Two Block A2B]
    H1 --> H4[Outrigger Position Sensors]
    H1 --> H5[Boom Angle and Radius Indicator]
    H1 --> H6[Function Cut-Out and Anemometer]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Mobile Crane Operational Workflow (Pre-Lift to Post-Lift)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Job Site Survey] --> S2[Step 2 Identify Hazards]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Soil Bearing Test]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Outrigger Pad Placement]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Crane Setup and Leveling]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Boom Configuration per Load Chart]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Rig the Load - D/d Check]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Pre-Lift Meeting and Signal Plan]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Test Lift and LMI Verification]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Perform Lift]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11 Set Down and Disconnect]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12 Post-Lift Inspection and Stow]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Force and Moment Interaction in Mobile Crane Stability

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Load[W Load acting vertically downward] --> MomentCalc[Load Moment Calculation]
    Radius[R Horizontal operating radius from centerline] --> MomentCalc
    MomentCalc --> Compare[Compare with Allowable Moment]
    CounterW[Wcw Counterweight mass] --> CounterM[Resisting Counter Moment]
    CounterR[Rcw Counterweight distance from centerline] --> CounterM
    CounterM --> Compare
    Compare --> Decision{Is the load within Safe Working Limits?}
    Decision -- Yes --> Safe[Lift Permitted - LMI Green]
    Decision -- No --> Cutout[LMI Activates Function Cut-Out]
    Safe --> Monitor[Continuous Monitoring During Lift]
    Cutout --> Lower[Operator Lowers Load and Reconfigures]
    Lower --> Reconfig[Re-Plan Lift: Reduce Radius or Add Outriggers]
    Reconfig --> MomentCalc
    Monitor --> WindCheck[Wind Speed Monitoring]
    WindCheck --> WindLimit{Wind below 32 km/h?}
    WindLimit -- No --> Stop[Suspend Lift]
    WindLimit -- Yes --> Continue[Continue Lift]
```

```mermaid

    L3 --> L4[Level 4 Administrative Controls]

    L3 --> L3A[LMI and A2B Devices]
    L3 --> L3B[Outrigger Pads]

    L4 --> L4A[Pre-Lift Meetings]
    L4 --> L4B[Qualified Rigger and Signal Person]

    L5 --> L5A[Hard Hats Hi-Vis Gloves Steel Toe Boots]

```

flowchart TB
    A[Outrigger Float 0.4 m diameter] -->|Load 1200 kN| B[Pad Surface 3.2 m diameter]
    B -->|Distribute| C[Soil Subgrade]
    C -->|Resists with Pbs| D[Net Bearing Pressure 150 kN per m2]
    D --> E{Acceptable per OSHA and Soil Test}
