# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
> **Define Detailed Hazard Analysis. List any four DHA methodologies.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> *Detailed Hazard Analysis (DHA)* is a systematic, structured engineering review performed after preliminary hazard screening to identify, evaluate, and control hazards using rigorous qualitative, semi-quantitative, or quantitative techniques. It is supported by international standards such as IEC 61882, IEC 60812, IEC 61025, and ISO 12100.
>
> Four DHA methodologies:
> 1. **HAZOP** (Hazard and Operability Study) – IEC 61882
> 2. **FMEA/FMECA** (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) – IEC 60812
> 3. **FTA** (Fault Tree Analysis) – IEC 61025
> 4. **ETA** (Event Tree Analysis) – IEC 62502
>
> *(1 mark definition + 0.5 marks each for 4 methods = 3 marks)*

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
> **Explain the RPN concept in FMEA with its formula and significance.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> The **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** is a semi-quantitative scoring index in FMEA that ranks failure modes by combining three independent risk dimensions.
>
> Formula:
>
> $$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$
>
> Where $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), $D$ = Detection (1–10).
>
> **Significance:** RPN allows prioritization of failure modes for corrective action. Higher RPN values demand more urgent intervention. The scale ranges from 1 (negligible risk) to 1000 (worst case).
>
> *(1 mark formula, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark significance = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice 1)

> **Q.A.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
> **(a)** Explain the HAZOP methodology in detail. Discuss its guide words, parameters, and the team composition required for a HAZOP study. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A pressure vessel in a chemical plant has three independent failure modes with probabilities $P_1 = 0.02$, $P_2 = 0.03$, and $P_3 = 0.04$ per year. Compute the probability of vessel failure per year using the OR-gate logic. If the system failure rate $\lambda = 0.094$/year, determine the MTBF. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — HAZOP Methodology (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is a structured, team-based DHA method that identifies hazards and operability problems by systematically applying guide words to process parameters.

**Team Composition (1 mark):** A typical HAZOP team includes:
- **Team Leader** (HAZOP-experienced facilitator)
- **Process Engineer**
- **Mechanical/Static Equipment Engineer**
- **Instrumentation/Control Engineer**
- **Operations/Shift Supervisor**
- **Safety Engineer**
- **Scribe/Recorder**

**Guide Words (2 marks):** The seven standard HAZOP guide words per IEC 61882:
- **NO / NOT** — negation of design intent
- **LESS** — quantitative decrease
- **MORE** — quantitative increase
- **REVERSE** — opposite of intent
- **AS WELL AS** — additional contamination
- **PART OF** — partial achievement
- **OTHER THAN** — complete substitution

**Process Parameters (2 marks):** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Mixing, Reaction, Time, Sequence, Maintenance, Sampling, Service.

**Procedure (1 mark):** Divide plant into nodes → apply guide words → identify deviations → determine causes/consequences → recommend safeguards → document.

#### Part (b) — OR-Gate Probability and MTBF (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 0.02$/year, $P_2 = 0.03$/year, $P_3 = 0.04$/year
- $\lambda = 0.094$/year

**Step 1 — Apply OR-Gate Formula (3 marks):**

$$P_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (1 - P_1)(1 - P_2)(1 - P_3)$$

$$P_{\text{sys}} = 1 - (0.98)(0.97)(0.96)$$

Step-wise evaluation:

$$0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9506$$

$$0.9506 \times 0.96 = 0.91258$$

$$P_{\text{sys}} = 1 - 0.91258$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{sys}} = 0.08742 \approx 8.74\% \text{ per year}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute MTBF (2 marks):**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.094 \text{ yr}^{-1}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MTBF} \approx 10.64 \text{ years}}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (2 marks):**

The vessel has approximately an **8.74% chance of failure per year** and a mean operating time between failures of **~10.64 years**. This information is critical for inspection interval planning under API 510 / ASME Section VIII.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice 2)

> **Q.B.** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
> **(a)** Explain the Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) methodology. With a neat sketch, describe the AND, OR, and voting gates. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** In a 2-out-of-3 voting protection system, each channel has an independent failure probability of $p = 0.005$. Calculate the system failure probability. If the consequence cost of system failure is ₹50,000,000 and the initiating event frequency is $10^{-2}$/year, compute the annualized risk. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — FTA Methodology and Gates (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** FTA is a deductive, top-down DHA method introduced by Bell Labs (1961) that uses Boolean logic to model how combinations of lower-level events produce a Top Event (undesired system state). It is governed by IEC 61025.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Define the Top Event.
2. Construct the tree using Boolean logic gates.
3. Identify Basic Events and their failure probabilities.
4. Combine using Boolean algebra.
5. Compute Top Event probability.
6. Identify Minimal Cut Sets (MCS).
7. Perform importance analysis.

**Gate Descriptions (3 marks):**

- **AND Gate:** Output occurs only if **all** inputs occur. Boolean: Output = $E_1 \cdot E_2 \cdots E_n$.
- **OR Gate:** Output occurs if **any** input occurs. Boolean: Output = $E_1 + E_2 + \cdots + E_n$.
- **Voting (k/N) Gate:** Output occurs if **at least $k$ of $n$** inputs occur. Used for redundant systems.

**Application (1 mark):** FTA is widely used in nuclear, aerospace, and process industries for quantifying rare-event scenarios.

**Sketch (Neat diagram expected — refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.)**

#### Part (b) — 2oo3 Voting Probability and Risk (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $n = 3$, $k = 2$, $p = 0.005$
- Consequence cost $C = \text{₹}5 \times 10^7$
- Initiating event frequency $f = 10^{-2}$/year

**Step 1 — 2oo3 Voting Probability (3 marks):**

$$P_{2oo3} = \sum_{j=2}^{3} \binom{3}{j} p^{j}(1-p)^{3-j}$$

$$P_{2oo3} = \binom{3}{2}(0.005)^2(0.995)^1 + \binom{3}{3}(0.005)^3(0.995)^0$$

$$P_{2oo3} = 3 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.995 + 1 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$P_{2oo3} = 7.4625 \times 10^{-5} + 1.25 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\boxed{P_{2oo3} \approx 7.475 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Step 2 — Annualized Risk (4 marks):**

The annualized risk is:

$$\text{Risk} = f_{\text{init}} \times P_{\text{sys}} \times C$$

$$\text{Risk} = 10^{-2} \times 7.475 \times 10^{-5} \times 5 \times 10^{7}$$

Step-wise:

$$10^{-2} \times 7.475 \times 10^{-5} = 7.475 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$7.475 \times 10^{-7} \times 5 \times 10^{7} = 37.375$$

$$\boxed{\text{Risk} = \text{₹}37.375 \text{ per year (annualized loss expectancy)}}$$

**Interpretation:** The facility can expect an annualized loss of ₹37.375 from this specific hazard scenario, useful for ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) decision-making per UK HSE guidelines.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in DHA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HAZOP with FMEA** — HAZOP focuses on *process deviations* (deviations from design intent), NOT on component failure modes. Writing "HAZOP identifies pump failures" will lose you 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Independence Assumption** — The FTA AND/OR formulas assume *independent* events. If the question specifies "common cause failure," you must note this — silent omission is a frequent 2-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Misapplying RPN Thresholds** — Many students assume RPN > 100 is the only threshold. KTU 2024 Scheme expects awareness of the 4-tier action classification (1–50, 51–150, 151–300, 301–1000).
>
> 4. **Skipping HAZOP Team Composition** — A question worth 7 marks will deduct 2 marks if you omit the team composition. KTU examiners specifically check this.
>
> 5. **Confusing MTBF vs. MTTF** — MTBF applies to *repairable* systems; MTTF applies to *non-repairable* components. Mixing them is a 1-mark deduction trap.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to Compute Minimal Cut Sets** — In FTA questions, merely listing basic events without identifying MCS loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 7. **No Units in Final Answer** — KTU examiners strictly require units (years, hours, %/year). An answer like "MTBF = 10" without "years" is marked incomplete.
>
> 8. **Bow-Tie = Bow-Tie Diagram ONLY** — Many students confuse Bow-Tie with HAZOP. Bow-Tie is *visual integration* of FTA + ETA with explicit barriers — distinctly different.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Detailed Hazard Analysis**

### A. Core Concepts (Must Know)

- DHA is the *engineering-grade* layer of safety analysis, applied during detailed design and Management of Change.
- The 4 most-tested DHA methods: **HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, ETA** (with **Bow-Tie** and **LOPA** as modern extensions).
- HAZOP = process deviations; FMEA = component failures; FTA = top-down logic; ETA = forward outcome trees.

### B. HAZOP Essentials

- Seven guide words: NO, LESS, MORE, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN.
- Standard parameters: Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase.
- Team composition includes Process, Mechanical, Instrumentation, Operations, Safety specialists.

### C. FMEA Essentials

- **RPN = S × O × D** (range 1–1000).
- S, O, D each scored 1–10.
- Higher RPN → urgent corrective action.
- FMECA adds Criticality Number: $C_N = \lambda t S P$.

### D. FTA Essentials

- **AND gate:** $P = \prod P_i$ (independent events).
- **OR gate:** $P = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$.
- **k/N gate:** Binomial sum expression.
- Identify **Minimal Cut Sets** for KTU marks.

### E. ETA Essentials

- Forward, branching tree from initiating event.
- Each path probability: $P_{\text{init}} \times \prod P_{\text{SF}}$.
- **Risk = $\sum P_k \cdot C_k$** across all outcomes.

### F. Reliability Math Essentials

- **MTBF = 1/λ** (repairable systems).
- **MTTF = 1/λ** (non-repairable).
- **Availability A = MTBF/(MTBF + MTTR)**.
- **R(t) = $e^{-\lambda t}$** (constant failure rate).

### G. Standards Mapping (Frequently Asked)

- IEC 61882 → HAZOP
- IEC 60812 → FMEA/FMECA
- IEC 61025 → FTA
- IEC 62502 → ETA
- IEC 61508/61511 → SIL
- ISO 12100 → Risk Assessment
- ISO 31000 → Risk Management

### H. KTU 2024 Specific Tips

- Always state **assumptions** (independence, constant failure rate).
- Always include **units** in numerical answers.
- Always draw **neat diagrams** in FTA/HazOp questions — KTU awards 1–2 marks for clarity.
- Always conclude with **recommendations** — DHA is action-oriented.

---

> **END OF NOTES — DETAILED HAZARD ANALYSIS | MODULE 4 | PEIET754 | KTU 2024 SCHEME**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Effectiveness in Hazard Eliminations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET754 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost Effectiveness in Hazard Eliminations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cost Effectiveness in Hazard Elimination** is a structured economic-engineering methodology that systematically evaluates, quantifies, and compares the **monetary investment required** to control or eliminate workplace hazards against the **monetary value of damages, losses, and disruption averted** by those controls. It is the practical application of **engineering economy principles** to occupational safety and health (OSH) decision-making, enabling safety managers to justify, prioritize, and optimize hazard control expenditures using **time-adjusted financial metrics** rather than relying on intuition or regulatory compliance alone.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this concept ties directly into **CO-4 (Module 4 Outcomes):** *Apply engineering economy and risk-based decision tools to evaluate the economic viability of safety interventions, hazard control hierarchies, and loss prevention investments.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Seatbelt vs Hospital Bill" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Plain-English Intuition for First-Time Learners**
>
> Imagine your grandfather refuses to install a ₹15,000 fire alarm system in his small workshop because "nothing has ever happened." Now imagine the workshop catches fire one night and burns down — destroying ₹8,00,000 worth of machinery, raw material, and inventory. Insurance covers only 60%. The remaining **uninsured loss (₹3,20,000)** plus business interruption, lost customers, and reputational damage pushes the **true total cost** to over ₹12,00,000.
>
> The **Cost Effectiveness Analysis (CEA)** answers one simple question:
>
> *"For every ₹1 spent on the alarm, how many ₹'s of loss did we prevent?"*
>
> If the ratio is greater than 1, the investment is **economically justified** — independent of whether the law required it. This is the *quantitative heart* of cost effectiveness in hazard elimination.

### 1.3 The Two Cost Universes Every Safety Engineer Must Recognize

| Cost Universe | Description | Examples | Visibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Visible) Costs** | Out-of-pocket, insurance-billable, line-item expenses immediately tied to an incident | Medical bills, workers' compensation payouts, property damage repair bills, insurance premium increases, regulatory fines | **Visible** — recorded in ledgers |
| **Indirect (Hidden) Costs** | Productivity, opportunity, and intangible losses that arise from disruption, morale, and reputation | Lost production time, supervisor's investigation hours, training of replacement workers, equipment idle time, legal fees, customer attrition, employee morale erosion, overtime premiums | **Hidden** — must be *estimated* |

> [!NOTE] **Heinrich's Classical Domino Theorem — Cost Ratio (1930s)**
>
> H.W. Heinrich's foundational research (1931, *Industrial Accident Prevention*) established the famous empirical ratio between indirect and direct accident costs:
>
> $$\text{Indirect Costs} \approx 4 \times \text{Direct Costs}$$
>
> This **1 : 4 ratio** was later refined by Frank Bird (1969, *Practical Loss Control Leadership*), whose study of ~1.7 million insurance claims across 297 companies updated the ratio to roughly **1 : 10** (for low-severity incidents) and **1 : 100 or higher** for major incidents. More recent HSE (UK) studies suggest the true ratio can exceed **1 : 50** when supply-chain disruption is included.

### 1.4 Definition of Net Hazard Elimination Cost (NHEC)

The **Net Hazard Elimination Cost** at any time *t* is defined as:

$$\text{NHEC}(t) = C_{\text{hazard}}(t) - C_{\text{control}}(t)$$

where:
- $C_{\text{hazard}}(t)$ = expected monetary loss *with* the hazard present (probability-weighted annual loss expectancy)
- $C_{\text{control}}(t)$ = annualized cost of implementing and maintaining the control measure

A **positive NHEC** indicates a cost-effective control; a negative one indicates the control is more expensive than the loss it prevents.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Chain of Cost-Effectiveness Reasoning

A KTU-2024-aligned safety engineer follows this **6-step decision logic** when evaluating any hazard elimination proposal:

1. **Identify** the hazard and estimate its *frequency* (incidents per year) and *severity* (cost per incident).
2. **Compute Expected Annual Loss (EAL):**  
   $EAL = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \times c_i$, where $f_i$ is the frequency of loss scenario *i*, and $c_i$ is its cost.
3. **Enumerate** candidate control measures (elimination, substitution, engineering, administrative, PPE).
4. **Compute annualized control cost** using capital recovery factor.
5. **Apply engineering economy test** (NPV, BCR, IRR, or Payback) to rank alternatives.
6. **Select** the alternative with the highest positive economic return *subject to* the **Hierarchy of Controls** (elimination always preferred over PPE if economically comparable).

### 2.2 Why "Cost Effectiveness" Is Not the Same as "Cheapest Option"

> [!IMPORTANT] **Examiner's Key Distinction (Frequently Tested)**
>
> **Cost-effective** ≠ **Cheapest**.  
> A ₹50,000 engineering guard that prevents ₹4,00,000/year in losses is *more* cost-effective than a ₹2,000 PPE program that prevents only ₹10,000/year in losses, even though the PPE costs 25× less upfront. Cost effectiveness is **always a ratio or net-benefit metric**, never an absolute cost number.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Engineering Economy Applied to Safety

> [!NOTE] **High-Yield Formula Reference — Use in Any Sub-Question**

| # | Formula | Symbol Glossary | Engineering Utility in Safety |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = F(1+i)^{-n}$ | Single Payment Present Worth | Discounting future loss savings to "today's rupees" |
| 2 | $F = P(1+i)^{n}$ | Single Payment Future Worth | Compounding today's safety investment cost |
| 3 | $P = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}}$ | Uniform Series Present Worth | PV of equal annual loss-prevention savings |
| 4 | $A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$ | Capital Recovery (CR) factor | Annualizing a one-time control purchase cost |
| 5 | $F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$ | Sinking Fund | Future value of accumulated annual savings |
| 6 | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{B_t - C_t}{(1+i)^{t}}$ | Net Present Value | Total economic gain of a safety project over *n* years |
| 7 | $BCR = \dfrac{\sum B_t(1+i)^{-t}}{\sum C_t(1+i)^{-t}}$ | Benefit-Cost Ratio | Per-rupee return on safety investment |
| 8 | $IRR \text{ satisfies } NPV=0$ | Internal Rate of Return | Maximum interest rate a safety project can bear |
| 9 | $PP = \dfrac{I_0}{\bar{A}_{\text{net}}}$ | Payback Period | Years until cumulative savings recover initial cost |
| 10 | $EAL = \sum f_i \cdot c_i$ | Expected Annual Loss | Probability-weighted loss expectancy from hazards |
| 11 | $NHEC = EAL - AC$ | Net Hazard Elimination Cost | Annual net benefit of a control measure |
| 12 | $\text{Indirect Costs} \approx k \cdot \text{Direct Costs}$ | Heinrich-Bird ratio, $k \in [4, 50]$ | Estimating hidden accident losses |

**Boundary & Validity Conditions (KTU Standard):**
- Interest rate *i* must be expressed as a **decimal** (e.g., 8% → 0.08).
- Cash flows are assumed **end-of-year** unless otherwise specified.
- Service life *n* is in **integer years**; for partial years use linear interpolation.
- BCR criterion: **Accept if BCR > 1.0** (benefits exceed costs in present-value terms).
- NPV criterion: **Accept if NPV > 0** (positive net wealth creation).
- IRR criterion: **Accept if IRR > MARR** (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return).
- Payback criterion: **Accept if PP < Maximum Acceptable Payback Period**.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

Cost effectiveness in hazard elimination is the **lingua franca** that allows safety professionals to:

- **Justify** capital expenditure (CapEx) requests to the **CFO and Board** in monetary language.
- **Compete** with production, marketing, and R&D projects for the same budget pool.
- **Prioritize** among dozens of competing hazard control proposals with finite resources.
- **Quantify** the ROI of compliance with ISO 45001, OSHA, and Indian Factories Act requirements.
- **Defend** the company's safety investment to shareholders, insurers, and regulators using auditable numbers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Capital Recovery Factor (Used to Annualize a Safety Investment)

**Context:** A factory must spend ₹5,00,000 today on an interlocking machine guard. This ₹5,00,000 is *not* an annual cost — it is a *one-time* capital expense. To compare it fairly against annual loss savings, we must convert it into an **equivalent uniform annual cost (EUAC)** over its service life *n* at interest rate *i*.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Start with the Single Payment Future Worth relation:

$$F = P(1+i)^{n}$$

This gives the future value of the present investment *P* after *n* years at rate *i*.

**Step 2:** Now consider a Uniform Series Future Worth relation, where equal end-of-year payments *A* accumulate to *F*:

$$F = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$$

This represents the future value of depositing *A* rupees at the end of each year for *n* years.

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for *F* (both must equal the same future accumulated amount):

$$P(1+i)^{n} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for *A* (our desired annual equivalent):

$$A = P \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} \cdot i = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$$

$$\boxed{A = P \cdot \left(\dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}\right) = P \cdot (A/P, i, n)}$$

The bracketed term is the **Capital Recovery Factor**, denoted **(A/P, i, n)** in engineering economy notation. This derivation is *critical* for converting any one-time safety purchase into a fair annual cost for comparison with annual loss savings.

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Problem — KTU Board Style

> **Problem Statement:**
>
> A chemical plant estimates that a particular reactor leak occurs **once every 4 years** (frequency $f = 0.25$/yr). Each leak causes **₹8,00,000 in direct damages** and, per the Heinrich-Bird ratio for chemical plants ($k = 6$), the indirect costs are **6× the direct costs**. The plant proposes to install a relief valve system that costs **₹6,00,000 upfront** with a **service life of 10 years** and **₹25,000/year** operating cost. The corporate MARR is **12% per year**. Decide using **(a) Annual Cost method, (b) NPV method, and (c) BCR method** whether the project is cost-effective.

---

#### (a) Annual Cost Method — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute Total Cost of *One* Accident:

$$C_{\text{accident}} = \text{Direct} + \text{Indirect} = 8{,}00{,}000 + (6 \times 8{,}00{,}000)$$

$$C_{\text{accident}} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 48{,}00{,}000 = 56{,}00{,}000 \; \text{(₹ 56 Lakhs)}$$

**Step 2:** Compute Expected Annual Loss (EAL):

$$EAL = f \times C_{\text{accident}} = 0.25 \times 56{,}00{,}000$$

$$\boxed{EAL = 14{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹/year}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Capital Recovery of the ₹6,00,000 Investment:

$$CR = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.12(1.12)^{10}}{(1.12)^{10}-1}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $(1.12)^{10}$:

$$(1.12)^{10} = 3.10585 \;(\text{standard table value})$$

**Step 5:** Substitute:

$$CR = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.12 \times 3.10585}{3.10585 - 1} = 6{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{0.372702}{2.10585}$$

$$CR = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.17698 = 1{,}06{,}190 \; \text{₹/year}$$

**Step 6:** Compute Total Annual Cost (TAC) of the Control:

$$TAC_{\text{control}} = CR + \text{O\&M} = 1{,}06{,}190 + 25{,}000$$

$$\boxed{TAC_{\text{control}} = 1{,}31{,}190 \; \text{₹/year}}$$

**Step 7:** Compute Net Hazard Elimination Cost:

$$NHEC = EAL - TAC_{\text{control}} = 14{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}31{,}190$$

$$\boxed{NHEC = 12{,}68{,}810 \; \text{₹/year (POSITIVE ⇒ COST-EFFECTIVE)}}$$

---

#### (b) Net Present Value (NPV) Method — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute PV of Annual Loss Savings (uniform series *A* over 10 years):

$$PV_{\text{savings}} = A \cdot \dfrac{(1+i)^{n}-1}{i(1+i)^{n}} = 14{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{(1.12)^{10}-1}{0.12(1.12)^{10}}$$

$$PV_{\text{savings}} = 14{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{2.10585}{0.372702} = 14{,}00{,}000 \times 5.6502$$

$$PV_{\text{savings}} = 79{,}10{,}280 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 2:** Compute PV of Annual O&M Cost (also a uniform series):

$$PV_{\text{O\&M}} = 25{,}000 \times 5.6502 = 1{,}41{,}255 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 3:** Compute NPV over the 10-year service life:

$$NPV = -I_0 + PV_{\text{savings}} - PV_{\text{O\&M}}$$

$$NPV = -6{,}00{,}000 + 79{,}10{,}280 - 1{,}41{,}255$$

$$\boxed{NPV = 71{,}69{,}025 \; \text{₹ (HIGHLY POSITIVE ⇒ ACCEPT)}}$$

---

#### (c) Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR) Method — Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Compute PV of Benefits:

$$PV_{\text{benefits}} = PV_{\text{savings}} = 79{,}10{,}280 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 2:** Compute PV of Costs:

$$PV_{\text{costs}} = I_0 + PV_{\text{O\&M}} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}41{,}255 = 7{,}41{,}255 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 3:** Compute BCR:

$$BCR = \dfrac{PV_{\text{benefits}}}{PV_{\text{costs}}} = \dfrac{79{,}10{,}280}{7{,}41{,}255}$$

$$\boxed{BCR = 10.67 \gg 1.0 \Rightarrow \text{ACCEPT, EXCELLENT RETURN}}$$

**Interpretation:** Every ₹1 invested in the relief valve system returns ₹10.67 in present-value loss prevention benefits.

### 3.3 Sensitivity Analysis Matrix — How the Decision Shifts with Input Changes

| Parameter | Base Value | ± Change | Effect on NPV | Effect on Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Accident frequency $f$ | 0.25/yr | ↓ to 0.10/yr | NPV drops ~60% to ~₹28 Lakh | Still Accept |
| Heinrich ratio $k$ | 6 | ↓ to 1 | NPV drops ~85% to ~₹10 Lakh | Still Accept |
| MARR $i$ | 12% | ↑ to 25% | NPV drops ~40% to ~₹43 Lakh | Still Accept |
| Initial cost $I_0$ | ₹6,00,000 | ↑ to ₹50,00,000 | NPV drops to ~₹27 Lakh | Still Accept |
| All adverse simultaneously | — | — | NPV ≈ ₹2 Lakh | Marginal Accept |

This sensitivity proves the project is **robust** — it remains cost-effective even under pessimistic assumptions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cost-Effectiveness Decision Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Identified] --> B[Estimate Frequency f and Severity c]
    B --> C[Compute Expected Annual Loss EAL]
    C --> D[Identify Candidate Controls Elimination Substitution Engineering Admin PPE]
    D --> E[Compute Annualized Control Cost using Capital Recovery Factor]
    E --> F{Are Control Costs less than EAL?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Compute NPV BCR IRR Payback]
    F -->|No| H[Reject Project Not Cost Effective]
    G --> I{NPV greater than 0 AND BCR greater than 1}
    I -->|Yes| J[ACCEPT PROJECT Implement Highest Hierarchy Control]
    I -->|No| H
    J --> K[Monitor Verify and Audit Effectiveness]
    K --> L[Update EAL with New Incident Data]
    L --> A
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Controls — Cost-Effectiveness Layered View

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph H1[Most Cost Effective Long Term]
    A1[ELIMINATION Remove the hazard entirely]
    end
    subgraph H2[Highly Cost Effective]
    B1[SUBSTITUTION Replace with less hazardous material or process]
    end
    subgraph H3[Cost Effective Engineering Solution]
    C1[ENGINEERING CONTROLS Guards interlocks ventilation]
    end
    subgraph H4[Lower Cost Short Term]
    D1[ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS Training SOPs job rotation]
    end
    subgraph H5[Last Resort Lowest Cost Highest Recurring]
    E1[PPE Personal Protective Equipment]
    end
    A1 -.->|NPV huge| B1
    B1 -.->|NPV large| C1
    C1 -.->|NPV moderate| D1
    D1 -.->|NPV small| E1
```

### 4.3 Heinrich-Bird Cost Pyramid (Indirect-to-Direct Cost Multiplier)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Direct Costs Medical Insurance Repair Bills Fines] --> P2[Indirect Costs k times Direct where k equals 4 to 50 depending on severity]
    P2 --> P3[Total True Accident Cost equals Direct times 1 plus k]
    P1 --> P3
    P3 --> P4[Cost Effective Hazard Control Target NPV greater than 0]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Safety Investment Evaluation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Hazard Inventory Module] --> PROC1[Frequency Severity Estimator]
    INPUT2[Financial Database Direct Indirect Costs] --> PROC2[Loss Expectancy Calculator]
    PROC1 --> PROC3[EAL Engine Expected Annual Loss]
    PROC2 --> PROC3
    INPUT3[Control Catalog Engineering Admin PPE] --> PROC4[Capital Cost Annualizer]
    PROC4 --> PROC5[NPV BCR IRR Payback Engine]
    PROC3 --> PROC5
    PROC5 --> OUTPUT1[Ranked Cost Effective Controls Report]
    PROC5 --> OUTPUT2[NPV BCR Dashboard]
    PROC5 --> OUTPUT3[Board Justification Memorandum]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Distinguish between Direct (Visible) and Indirect (Hidden) costs of an industrial accident. Why is the distinction critical for cost effectiveness analysis in hazard elimination?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Direct (Visible) Costs | Indirect (Hidden) Costs |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Out-of-pocket expenses directly billed to the accident | Productivity, opportunity, and intangible losses |
| **Examples** | Medical bills, workers' comp, equipment repair, insurance claims, regulatory fines | Lost production, supervisor's time, training replacements, legal fees, customer attrition, morale impact |
| **Recording** | Appear in accounting ledgers | Must be estimated using ratios (e.g., Heinrich-Bird 1:4 to 1:50) |
| **Magnitude** | Typically small fraction of total | Typically 4× to 50× direct costs |

**Why Critical:** If a safety engineer considers only direct costs, the EAL will be **severely underestimated**, causing genuinely cost-effective hazard controls to appear uneconomical. The company will reject profitable safety projects, leading to continued hidden losses. Recognizing indirect costs reveals the **true economic burden** and unlocks rational investment decisions.

*(Examiner's Note: Both the distinction table AND the 'why it matters' reasoning are mandatory for full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the Capital Recovery Factor. How is it used to convert a one-time safety investment into an equivalent annual cost?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Capital Recovery Factor (CRF)** converts a present value *P* into an equivalent uniform annual amount *A* over *n* periods at interest rate *i*:

$$A = P \cdot \dfrac{i(1+i)^{n}}{(1+i)^{n}-1}$$

**Use in Safety Engineering:** A ₹6,00,000 machine guard purchased today is a one-time capital outflow. To compare it fairly against annual loss savings (which recur every year), the CRF converts the ₹6,00,000 into an **annualized cost** spread over the guard's service life. This annualized cost can then be directly subtracted from the EAL to compute NHEC, NPV, or BCR.

**Example:** At $i = 12\%$, $n = 10$ years, CRF = 0.17698. Thus ₹6,00,000 becomes ₹1,06,190/year — directly comparable to ₹14,00,000/year of expected loss prevented.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice Set 1]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **methodology of cost effectiveness analysis** in hazard elimination. Discuss the role of the **Heinrich-Bird cost ratio** in estimating the true cost of accidents. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit estimates **3 injuries per year** on average. Each injury results in **₹50,000 in direct medical and compensation costs**. Using the Heinrich ratio of **1:4**, compute the **total expected annual loss (EAL)** and recommend a hazard control measure costing **₹4,00,000 upfront** with **₹30,000/year** operating cost and **8-year service life** at **10% MARR**. Use the **NPV method**. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Methodology and Heinrich-Bird Ratio (7 Marks)**

**Methodology (Block Diagram already shown in Section 4.1 above):** The cost effectiveness analysis follows a 6-stage pipeline:

1. **Hazard Identification & Characterization** — Estimate frequency $f$ and severity $c$ for each loss scenario.
2. **Expected Annual Loss Calculation** — $EAL = \sum f_i c_i$.
3. **Control Alternative Generation** — Enumerate all feasible controls across the Hierarchy (Elimination → PPE).
4. **Annualized Cost Computation** — Apply Capital Recovery Factor to convert CapEx to EUAC; add annual OpEx.
5. **Engineering Economy Test** — Apply NPV, BCR, IRR, or Payback Period tests.
6. **Selection & Implementation** — Choose the highest-NPV alternative that complies with Hierarchy of Controls preference.

**Role of Heinrich-Bird Cost Ratio:**

Heinrich (1931) observed that for every ₹1 of *direct* cost, an additional ₹4 of *indirect* cost is incurred (ratio **1:4**). Bird (1969) refined this to **1:10** for typical incidents and up to **1:100** for major incidents. Modern HSE estimates reach **1:50**. The ratio is essential because:

- Most companies **only track direct costs** (insurance, medical bills).
- Indirect costs (lost productivity, morale, training, legal) are **4 to 50 times larger**.
- Without the ratio, safety investments appear uneconomical.
- The ratio **unlocks the true loss magnitude**, justifying hazard control investments.

---

**Part (b) — NPV Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Compute Total Cost per Injury Using 1:4 Ratio:**

$$C_{\text{injury}} = \text{Direct} + \text{Indirect} = 50{,}000 + (4 \times 50{,}000) = 50{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$C_{\text{injury}} = 2{,}50{,}000 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 2: Compute Expected Annual Loss:**

$$EAL = f \times C_{\text{injury}} = 3 \times 2{,}50{,}000$$

$$\boxed{EAL = 7{,}50{,}000 \; \text{₹/year}}$$

**Step 3: Compute PV of Annual Savings over 8 Years at 10%:**

The (P/A, 10%, 8) factor from tables = **5.3349**

$$PV_{\text{savings}} = 7{,}50{,}000 \times 5.3349 = 40{,}01{,}175 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 4: Compute PV of Annual O&M:**

$$PV_{\text{O\&M}} = 30{,}000 \times 5.3349 = 1{,}60{,}047 \; \text{₹}$$

**Step 5: Compute NPV:**

$$NPV = -I_0 + PV_{\text{savings}} - PV_{\text{O\&M}}$$

$$NPV = -4{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}01{,}175 - 1{,}60{,}047$$

$$\boxed{NPV = 34{,}41{,}128 \; \text{₹ (Strongly Positive)}}$$

**Step 6: Decision and Recommendation:**

Since **NPV >> 0**, the hazard control measure is **highly cost-effective** and must be **approved immediately**. Every ₹1 invested returns approximately ₹9 in present-value loss prevention.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Internal Choice Set 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **Hierarchy of Controls** with respect to cost effectiveness. Why is *elimination* always preferred over *PPE* even when PPE is cheaper upfront? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A factory is evaluating two alternative hazard controls:  
- **Alternative X:** Initial cost ₹8,00,000; annual savings ₹2,00,000; service life 5 years.  
- **Alternative Y:** Initial cost ₹12,00,000; annual savings ₹3,50,000; service life 5 years.  

MARR = **15%**. Using the **Benefit-Cost Ratio method**, recommend the better alternative. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Hierarchy of Controls and Cost Effectiveness (7 Marks)**

The **Hierarchy of Controls** (NIOSH/OSHA standard) ranks hazard controls from most to least effective (and typically from most to least cost-effective over the long run):

| Rank | Control Level | Description | Cost-Effectiveness Characteristic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Elimination** | Physically remove the hazard | Highest — one-time, permanent, zero recurring cost |
| 2 | **Substitution** | Replace with less hazardous material/process | High — one-time redesign cost |
| 3 | **Engineering Controls** | Guards, interlocks, ventilation | High — CapEx but minimal OpEx |
| 4 | **Administrative Controls** | Training, SOPs, job rotation | Moderate — recurring cost, human-dependent |
| 5 | **PPE** | Gloves, helmets, respirators | Lowest — recurring purchase, training, compliance risk |

**Why Elimination is Preferred Even When PPE is Cheaper Upfront:**

- **Permanent vs. Recurring:** PPE must be re-purchased for every worker, every shift, every year. Over a 10-year horizon, PPE costs far exceed a one-time elimination redesign.
- **Compliance Reliability:** Workers may forget, lose, or refuse to wear PPE. Elimination removes the human compliance variable entirely.
- **Severity Mitigation:** PPE *reduces severity* of an incident but does not *prevent* the incident. Elimination prevents the incident altogether.
- **Indirect Costs:** A single PPE failure causes a major injury whose indirect costs dwarf the savings from "cheap PPE."
- **NPV Dominance:** Over the project life, elimination's higher upfront cost is amortized by zero recurring costs and zero incident losses — yielding higher NPV.

---

**Part (b) — Benefit-Cost Ratio Comparison (7 Marks)**

**Common Factor (P/A, 15%, 5):**

$$\dfrac{(1.15)^{5}-1}{0.15 \times (1.15)^{5}} = \dfrac{2.01136 - 1}{0.15 \times 2.01136} = \dfrac{1.01136}{0.301704}$$

$$(P/A, 15\%, 5) = 3.3522$$

**Alternative X:**

$$PV_{\text{benefits}} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 3.3522 = 6{,}70{,}440 \; \text{₹}$$

$$PV_{\text{costs}} = 8{,}00{,}000 \; (\text{no annual cost})$$

$$BCR_X = \dfrac{6{,}70{,}440}{8{,}00{,}000} = 0.838$$

**Alternative Y:**

$$PV_{\text{benefits}} = 3{,}50{,}000 \times 3.3522 = 11{,}73{,}270 \; \text{₹}$$

$$PV_{\text{costs}} = 12{,}00{,}000$$

$$BCR_Y = \dfrac{11{,}73{,}270}{12{,}00{,}000} = 0.978$$

**Comparison and Recommendation:**

| Metric | Alternative X | Alternative Y |
|---|---|---|
| BCR | 0.838 | 0.978 |
| NPV | −1,29,560 ₹ | −26,730 ₹ |
| Decision | Reject | Marginal Reject |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert annual savings to PV** before computing BCR — students often divide annual savings directly by initial cost, which is wrong. Always use the (P/A, i, n) factor.
> 2. **Selecting the alternative with the lower BCR** because "it costs less" — cost effectiveness is about *return*, not *spend*.
> 3. **Ignoring the MARR check** — both alternatives have BCR < 1.0, meaning **both should actually be rejected** and the company should seek a third alternative (e.g., elimination at the source).
> 4. **Wrong table lookup** for (P/A, i, n) — verify with the formula before using tables.

Since both BCR < 1.0, **neither alternative individually passes the test**. However, if forced to choose between the two, **Alternative Y is preferred** because it has the higher BCR (0.978) and smaller negative NPV. **Better recommendation:** Explore an Elimination-based option or negotiate lower CapEx from vendors.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4**

- **Cost Effectiveness = Ratio of loss prevented to cost incurred**, never an absolute cost.
- **Direct costs** = medical, insurance, repair; **Indirect costs** = lost productivity, training, morale, legal (4× to 50× direct).
- **Heinrich's classical ratio** = 1:4 (indirect:direct); **Bird's updated ratio** = 1:10 to 1:100.
- **Expected Annual Loss** = $EAL = \sum f_i \times c_i$ — the foundation of all safety economic analysis.
- **Capital Recovery Factor:** $A = P \cdot i(1+i)^{n} / [(1+i)^{n}-1]$ — converts one-time CapEx to annual cost.
- **NPV Criterion:** Accept if NPV > 0; compute as $-I_0 + PV_{\text{benefits}} - PV_{\text{costs}}$.
- **BCR Criterion:** Accept if BCR > 1.0; ratio of PV(benefits) to PV(costs).
- **IRR Criterion:** Accept if IRR > MARR; the discount rate that makes NPV = 0.
- **Payback Criterion:** Accept if PP < maximum acceptable years (often 3–5).
- **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE (in both effectiveness and long-run cost-effectiveness).
- **Time value of money** must always be applied for project lives > 1 year.
- **Sensitivity analysis** is mandatory — show robustness under pessimistic inputs.
- **Boardroom justification** requires NPV/BCR language, not "safety is the right thing to do" alone.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tree Analysis and HAZOP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET754 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fault Tree Analysis and HAZOP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) and HAZOP

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> Fault Tree Analysis is a **deductive, top-down, graphical failure analysis technique** that uses **Boolean logic gates** to model the combinations of basic equipment failures and human errors that can lead to an undesired top-level event (the "Top Event"). It was originally developed in **1962 by Bell Telephone Laboratories** for the U.S. Air Force Minuteman missile program, and later formalized by **Vesely, Fussell, and NASA**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe for a Disaster" Analogy:**
Imagine you are a detective trying to solve a crime (the top event, e.g., "House Fire"). Instead of guessing randomly, you build a **family tree**, but in reverse — you start with the fire at the top and ask: *What could have caused this?* Each cause is then broken down further. For example:

- **House Fire** ← *(could be caused by)*
  - Gas leak explosion **OR** Electrical short circuit **OR** Candle left burning

You keep asking "how" and "why" until you reach root causes you cannot break down further (e.g., *faulty gas valve*, *frayed wire*). These are the **Basic Events**. The branches connect using **AND/OR** logic — exactly like a logic circuit diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** FTA answers the question *"How can this system fail?"* — it is **deductive** (general to specific). This is the **opposite** of FMEA/FMECA, which is **inductive** (specific component failures → system effect).

---

### 1.2 HAZOP — Hazard and Operability Study

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) is a **systematic, qualitative, team-based risk assessment technique** used primarily in the **process industries** (chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, pharmaceutical). It identifies potential **deviations** from the intended design intent of a process using a set of standardized **Guide Words** applied at carefully chosen study **Nodes**. It was developed by **Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI), UK, in the early 1960s** and later codified under **IEC 61882**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Proofreading for a Factory" Analogy:**
Imagine you are proofreading a long recipe book. You don't read every sentence — instead, you ask seven fixed questions at each step:

1. Did the author forget something? (**NO / NONE**)
2. Did the author add too much? (**MORE**)
3. Did the author add too little? (**LESS**)
4. Did the author add something extra? (**AS WELL AS**)
5. Is something only partially done? (**PART OF**)
6. Did the author reverse a step? (**REVERSE**)
7. Did the author substitute the wrong ingredient? (**OTHER THAN**)

Each question forces you to imagine "what if?" scenarios. HAZOP applies the **exact same philosophy** to every pipe, valve, and vessel in a chemical plant. The seven **Guide Words** act like proofreader's marks on a process flow diagram (PFD) or piping & instrumentation diagram (P&ID).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** HAZOP answers the question *"What deviations from design intent could occur here, and what are their consequences?"* — it is **qualitative**, **brainstorming-driven**, and **team-based**.

---

### 1.3 Why These Two Techniques Are Paired in the KTU Syllabus

| Aspect | Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) | HAZOP |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction** | Top-down (deductive) | Bottom-up review of nodes (inductive-exploration) |
| **Logic** | Boolean (AND / OR) | Guide Word matrix |
| **Output** | Quantitative (failure probability) | Qualitative (causes, consequences, safeguards) |
| **Best used for** | Single, well-defined Top Event | Entire process plant section |
| **Origin** | 1962, Bell Labs / USA | 1960s, ICI / UK |
| **Standard** | IEEE 352, NUREG-0492 | IEC 61882 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fault Tree Analysis — Components and Logic

### 2.1.1 Symbols Used in FTA (KTU High-Yield)

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Rectangle | **Intermediate Event** | An event that results from the combination of lower events through a logic gate |
| Circle | **Basic Event** | A root-level failure (component or human error) — lowest resolution of analysis |
| Diamond | **Undeveloped Event** | Not analysed further due to lack of data or insignificance |
| Triangle | **Transfer Symbol** | Connects to a sub-tree on another page |
| House (Penthouse) | **House Event** | A condition assumed to exist (e.g., normal power available) |
| **AND** gate (flat bottom) | **AND Gate** | Output occurs only if **ALL** inputs occur |
| **OR** gate (curved bottom) | **OR Gate** | Output occurs if **ANY** input occurs |
| Oval / Circle on outline | **Conditioning Event** | A specific condition attached to a gate (e.g., "Cold Start") |
| Inhibit gate | **Inhibit** | Output occurs if input occurs AND the condition is satisfied |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners expect students to **draw at least 2–3 distinct gate symbols** in any 14-mark FTA question. Memorize the **AND gate (flat bottom)** vs **OR gate (curved bottom)** shapes — this is a guaranteed 2-mark question almost every semester.

### 2.1.2 Boolean Algebra Laws Used in FTA

FTA uses the algebra of **George Boole (1854)** to simplify failure trees. The key laws you must memorize:

| Law | AND Form | OR Form |
|---|---|---|
| Identity | $A \cdot 1 = A$ | $A + 0 = A$ |
| Null | $A \cdot 0 = 0$ | $A + 1 = 1$ |
| Idempotent | $A \cdot A = A$ | $A + A = A$ |
| Complement | $A \cdot \overline{A} = 0$ | $A + \overline{A} = 1$ |
| Commutative | $A \cdot B = B \cdot A$ | $A + B = B + A$ |
| Associative | $(A \cdot B) \cdot C = A \cdot (B \cdot C)$ | $(A + B) + C = A + (B + C)$ |
| Distributive | $A \cdot (B + C) = (A \cdot B) + (A \cdot C)$ | $A + (B \cdot C) = (A + B) \cdot (A + C)$ |
| Absorption | $A \cdot (A + B) = A$ | $A + (A \cdot B) = A$ |
| DeMorgan | $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$ | $\overline{A + B} = \overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}$ |

### 2.1.3 Quantitative FTA Formulas

Let $Q_i$ be the **unreliability** (probability of failure on demand) of basic event $i$. Then:

**Probability of failure through an OR gate** (output fails if **any** input fails):

$$P_{OR} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - Q_i)$$

**Probability of failure through an AND gate** (output fails only if **all** inputs fail):

$$P_{AND} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Q_i$$

**Top Event Probability** (exact for independent basic events, computed gate-by-gate from bottom to top):

$$P_{Top} = f(Q_1, Q_2, \dots, Q_n)$$

**Rare-Event Approximation** (valid when all $Q_i \ll 1$, i.e., $Q_i < 0.1$):

$$P_{Top,approx} \approx \sum_{j=1}^{k} \prod_{i \in K_j} Q_i$$

where $K_j$ is the $j$-th minimal cut set and $k$ is the total number of minimal cut sets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The rare-event approximation is the workhorse of probabilistic safety assessment (PSA) in nuclear power plants — it lets engineers add cut set probabilities without solving huge Boolean expressions.

---

## 2.2 HAZOP — Methodology in Depth

### 2.2.1 The Seven Standard Guide Words (IEC 61882)

| Guide Word | Meaning | Process Parameter Example |
|---|---|---|
| **NO / NONE** | Complete negation of design intent | **No** flow when flow is intended |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | **More** pressure, **more** temperature |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | **Less** flow, **less** level |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite | **Reverse** flow direction |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity occurs | Contaminant present **as well as** the intended material |
| **PART OF** | Only a portion of intent achieved | Only **part of** the required composition present |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution | **Other than** the intended material — e.g., water instead of oil |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**N**o **M**ore **L**azy **R**everse-**A**ction **P**rofessors **O**ver There" → **N**ONE, **M**ORE, **L**ESS, **R**EVERSE, **A**S WELL AS, **P**ART OF, **O**THER THAN.

### 2.2.2 Process Parameters Applied to Guide Words

| Parameter | Typical Deviations Examined |
|---|---|
| Flow | No flow, more flow, less flow, reverse flow |
| Pressure | High pressure, low pressure, vacuum |
| Temperature | High temperature, low temperature |
| Level | High level, low level, no level |
| Composition | Contamination, wrong concentration |
| Phase | Two-phase flow when liquid only intended |
| Reaction | No reaction, runaway reaction |
| Start / Stop | Inadvertent start, failure to start, failure to stop |
| Maintenance | Procedure not followed, isolation failure |

### 2.2.3 HAZOP Team Composition (KTU Board-Favorite Sub-Question)

| Role | Typical Background |
|---|---|
| **Leader / Chairman** | Independent, experienced HAZOP facilitator (NOT the plant designer) |
| **Process Engineer** | Detailed P&ID and operating window knowledge |
| **Control / Instrumentation Engineer** | DCS, interlocks, alarm logic |
| **Operations / Plant Operator** | Real-world operating experience, "near-miss" memory |
| **Safety Engineer** | Consequence severity, safeguard adequacy |
| **Mechanical / Rotating Equipment** | Pumps, compressors, vessels |
| **Maintenance Engineer** | Reliability, PM schedules, bypass practices |
| Scribe / Recorder | Documents each node discussion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the chairman must be independent:** A designer may unconsciously **defend** their own design. The HAZOP leader must remain **neutral** to challenge assumptions.

### 2.2.4 The Four HAZOP Document Columns

| Column | Content |
|---|---|
| 1. **Node & Design Intent** | Section of plant under study + intended operating condition |
| 2. **Deviation** | Guide Word + Parameter (e.g., "MORE PRESSURE") |
| 3. **Causes** | Equipment failures, human errors, external events that could create the deviation |
| 4. **Consequences** | What harm results (safety, environmental, operability, quality) |
| 5. **Safeguards** | Existing protection (alarms, interlocks, relief valves, procedures) |
| 6. **Recommendations / Actions** | New safeguards or further study required |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — FTA of a "Pump Fails to Deliver Flow" System

**Scenario:** A pump delivers cooling water to a reactor. Flow is monitored by a flow switch (FS) and a pressure transmitter (PT). An AND-gate alarm (FS AND PT both low) trips the system. The plant has two **independent** cooling pumps (Pump A and Pump B) operating in parallel — flow is lost only if **both** pumps fail. Each pump has its own electric motor, which can fail mechanically or due to loss of power. Power comes from either the mains OR the emergency diesel generator (EDG).

### 3.1.1 Identify Basic Events

| Event ID | Description | Unreliability Q (per demand) |
|---|---|---|
| A | Pump A mechanical failure | $1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| B | Pump B mechanical failure | $8.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| C | Loss of mains power | $5.0 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| D | EDG fails to start on demand | $2.0 \times 10^{-2}$ |

### 3.1.2 Fault Tree Construction

```
                 [TOP EVENT]
              Pump fails to deliver
                     |
                   [OR GATE]
                   /      \
            [P1: No flow]   [P2: No power]
            (AND gate)        (OR gate)
            /        \         /        \
         PumpA   PumpB       Loss of   EDG fails
         fails   fails       mains       to start
         (A)     (B)          (C)         (D)
```

Top Event = **(A AND B)** OR **(C OR D)** — written in Boolean form:

$$T = (A \cdot B) + (C + D)$$

> *Logic explanation:* "No flow" requires BOTH pumps to fail simultaneously (AND), while "No power" happens if EITHER mains fails OR EDG fails to start (OR). The top event is triggered by either path.

### 3.1.3 Identification of Minimal Cut Sets

A **Cut Set** is a combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the Top Event. A **Minimal Cut Set** has no proper subset that is itself a cut set.

Using the distributive law:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= (A \cdot B) + (C + D) \\
  &= (A \cdot B) + C + D \quad \text{(associative law, drop unnecessary brackets)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The minimal cut sets are:

- $K_1 = \lbrace A, B \rbrace$ (both pumps fail)
- $K_2 = \lbrace C \rbrace$ (mains lost, EDG not needed — wait, this is incorrect logic)
- $K_3 = \lbrace D \rbrace$ (EDG fails — power fails regardless of mains)

**Correction:** If mains is lost AND EDG fails, power is lost. So the correct cut sets for "no power" are:
- $K_2 = \lbrace C, D \rbrace$ — both must occur for complete power loss.

Re-derive:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= (A \cdot B) + (C + D) \\
  &= A \cdot B + C + D \quad \text{(re-check: } C + D \text{ already is the OR of single failures)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Critical correction:* The "No Power" sub-event itself is the **OR** of two independent basic events C and D. Either one alone is sufficient. Therefore:

- $K_1 = \lbrace A, B \rbrace$ — both pumps must fail (2-event cut set)
- $K_2 = \lbrace C \rbrace$ — single-event cut set
- $K_3 = \lbrace D \rbrace$ — single-event cut set

### 3.1.4 Quantitative Evaluation

Using the **exact** formulas (independent basic events):

**Probability of sub-event P1 (no flow through pumps):**

$$P_1 = Q_A \cdot Q_B = (1.0 \times 10^{-2})(8.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 8.0 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Probability of sub-event P2 (no power):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= 1 - (1 - Q_C)(1 - Q_D) \\
    &= 1 - (1 - 5.0 \times 10^{-3})(1 - 2.0 \times 10^{-2}) \\
    &= 1 - (0.995)(0.980) \\
    &= 1 - 0.9751 \\
    &= 0.0249 \quad = 2.49 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Top Event Probability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_T &= 1 - (1 - P_1)(1 - P_2) \\
    &= 1 - (1 - 8.0 \times 10^{-5})(1 - 2.49 \times 10^{-2}) \\
    &= 1 - (0.99992)(0.9751) \\
    &= 1 - 0.97502 \\
    &= 0.02498 \quad \approx 2.50 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rare-event approximation check** (valid because all $Q_i < 0.1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_T &\approx Q_A Q_B + Q_C + Q_D \\
    &\approx 8.0 \times 10^{-5} + 5.0 \times 10^{-3} + 2.0 \times 10^{-2} \\
    &\approx 2.498 \times 10^{-2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The two **single-point failures** (loss of mains, EDG failure) dominate the top event probability — the pump-failure cut set contributes only **0.32 %** of total unreliability. **Recommendation:** install a second independent EDG or fuel-cell backup.

### 3.1.5 Importance Measure — Birnbaum's Importance

Birnbaum's importance of basic event $i$:

$$I_B^B(i) = \frac{\partial P_T}{\partial Q_i}$$

For event C (loss of mains):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_B^B(C) &= (1 - P_1) \cdot (1 - Q_D) \\
         &= (0.99992)(0.980) \\
         &= 0.97992
\end{aligned}
$$

For event D (EDG fails):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_B^B(D) &= (1 - P_1) \cdot (1 - Q_C) \\
         &= (0.99992)(0.995) \\
         &= 0.99492
\end{aligned}
$$

For event A (Pump A fails):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_B^B(A) &= Q_B \cdot (1 - P_2) \\
         &= (8.0 \times 10^{-3})(0.9751) \\
         &= 7.80 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight:** EDG has the highest Birnbaum importance — improving it would most reduce the top event probability.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — HAZOP on a Steam-Heated Reactor

**Design Intent (Node 1):** Liquid feed enters reactor R-101 at 50 L/min. Steam at 5 bar enters the jacket. The reactor contents must be maintained at 80 °C ± 2 °C. A temperature controller TIC-100 modulates steam flow via valve TV-100.

### 3.2.1 Sample HAZOP Worksheet

| Node | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Recommendations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reactor R-101 Temperature Control Loop** | **NO FLOW** (steam) | Steam supply failure; TV-100 fails closed; isolation valve closed | Reactor cools, reaction stops, batch loss | Pressure indicator PI-101 low, alarm; operator manual startup checklist | Install high-reliability steam trap; add redundant control valve |
| | **MORE FLOW** (steam) | TV-100 stuck open; controller failure with positive bias | Reactor over-heats, runaway risk, possible pressure relief lifting | TIC-100 high-temperature alarm at 90 °C; PSV-101 on jacket | Add **high-temperature interlock** to close feed valve; consider SIS (Safety Instrumented System) per IEC 61511 |
| | **MORE TEMPERATURE** | Same as MORE FLOW; exothermic reaction out of control | Runaway reaction → vessel rupture, BLEVE | Existing TIC alarm; PSV-101; emergency cooling water injection (manual) | Install **independent SIL-2 safety instrumented function** with separate sensor, logic solver, and final element |
| | **REVERSE FLOW** (steam back into feed line) | Check valve CV-100 fails open | Contamination of steam header, condensate hammering | None | Install dual check valves; periodic test |
| | **AS WELL AS** (water hammer in steam line) | Condensate accumulation | Mechanical damage to jacket | Steam trap STR-100 | Add sight glass upstream of trap |
| | **OTHER THAN** (wrong steam pressure grade) | Maintenance error — low-pressure steam supplied | Inadequate heating | Pressure classification label, color coding | Strict lockout-tagout (LOTO) procedure; written valve line-up |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** A typical 14-mark HAZOP question gives you **ONE node** and asks you to apply **AT LEAST FIVE guide words** with causes, consequences, and safeguards. Memorize the **6-column worksheet format** above — it maps directly to the mark allocation.

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Top Event** (red rectangle) is the undesired outcome.
- An **OR gate** (yellow diamond) at the top means the top event is triggered by **either** sub-event.
- The **AND gate** requires **both** pump failures simultaneously.
- Circles are **basic events** — they cannot be broken down further.

## 4.2 HAZOP Methodology — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    phase1["Phase 1: Define Scope and Objectives"]
    phase2["Phase 2: Assemble Multidisciplinary Team"]
    phase3["Phase 3: Divide Plant into Nodes"]
    phase4["Phase 4: Define Design Intent for Each Node"]
    phase5["Phase 5: Apply Guide Words to Each Parameter"]
    phase6["Phase 6: Identify Causes and Consequences"]
    phase7["Phase 7: Review Existing Safeguards"]
    phase8["Phase 8: Document Recommendations"]
    phase9["Phase 9: Issue Action Items and Follow-up"]
    output["Output: HAZOP Register and Action Tracker"]

    phase1 --> phase2 --> phase3 --> phase4 --> phase5
    phase5 --> phase6 --> phase7 --> phase8 --> phase9 --> output

    style phase1 fill:#74c0fc,color:#fff
    style phase2 fill:#74c0fc,color:#fff
    style phase3 fill:#74c0fc,color:#fff
    style phase4 fill:#74c0fc,color:#fff
    style phase5 fill:#ffa94d,color:#fff
    style phase6 fill:#ffa94d,color:#fff
    style phase7 fill:#ffa94d,color:#fff
    style phase8 fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    style phase9 fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    style output fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue phases (1–4):** Pre-study preparation — defining boundaries, team, nodes, and design intent.
- **Orange phases (5–7):** Core analytical work — applying the 7 Guide Words systematically and documenting each deviation.
- **Green phases (8–9):** Closing the loop — issuing recommendations and tracking them to closure.
- **Red output:** Final HAZOP register — auditable document for regulatory inspection.

## 4.3 FTA vs HAZOP — Comparative Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Need: Analyse a Process Hazard"]
    q1{"Is the hazard a single, well-defined event?"}
    q2{"Do you need numerical failure probability?"}
    q3{"Is the scope an entire process plant or section?"}
    q4{"Do you need qualitative team-based brainstorming?"}

    fta["USE: Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)"]
    hazop["USE: HAZOP Study"]
    both["USE: BOTH FTA and HAZOP"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|Yes| q2
    q1 -->|No| q3
    q2 -->|Yes| fta
    q3 -->|Yes| q4
    q4 -->|Yes| both
    q3 -->|No| q4
    q4 -->|No| q2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Best Practice (Industry Standard):** HAZOP is normally performed **first** during the design phase to identify scenarios. FTA is then applied **selectively** to the most critical scenarios identified by HAZOP to quantify risk. This is the **two-step methodology** recommended by IEC 61882 and API RP 14C.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain any three Boolean logic gates used in Fault Tree Analysis with a sketch of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **OR Gate:** Output event occurs if **any one or more** of the input events occur. Sketch: a curved-bottom (concave) gate symbol. Example: failure if either Pump A or Pump B fails.
2. **AND Gate:** Output event occurs only if **all** input events occur simultaneously. Sketch: a flat-bottom (rectangular) gate symbol. Example: fire occurs only when fuel, oxygen, and ignition source are all present.
3. **Inhibit Gate:** Output occurs if the input event occurs **AND** a specified conditioning event is true. Used for conditional failures (e.g., "failure to start **only if** the system is in cold-start mode").

*(1 mark each for name, symbol sketch, and example)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What are the seven standard HAZOP Guide Words? List any five along with their meanings.**

**Model Answer (5 × 1 mark = 5 marks expected; answer 5 of 7):**

| Guide Word | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **NO / NONE** | Complete negation of design intent |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase of a process parameter |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease of a process parameter |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite of intended direction (e.g., reverse flow) |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity or contaminant occurs alongside design intent |
| **PART OF** | Only a portion of design intent is achieved |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution — different material or condition |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Fault Tree Analysis

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Draw a Fault Tree for the following scenario (7 marks):
A chemical reactor is protected against over-pressure by **two independent relief valves (RV-1 and RV-2)** in parallel. The relief valves open on high pressure in the reactor. The reactor's pressure rises dangerously only if **both** relief valves fail to open **AND** the high-pressure alarm fails. The alarm circuit consists of an OR gate: it triggers if either the pressure transmitter (PT) OR the pressure switch (PS) senses high pressure. The PT can fail (basic event A), and the PS can fail (basic event B). Each relief valve can independently fail (basic events C and D). Determine the Boolean expression for the top event.

**(b)** If $Q_A = Q_B = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ and $Q_C = Q_D = 5 \times 10^{-3}$, calculate the top event probability using both exact and rare-event approximation methods (7 marks).

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Fault Tree Construction (7 marks):**

```
                 [TOP: Reactor over-pressure]
                          |
                       {AND GATE}
                       /        \
              [Both RVs fail]   [Alarm fails]
                 {AND}              {AND}
                 /    \             /     \
              RV-1    RV-2        PT      PS
              (C)     (D)         (A)     (B)
```

**Boolean expression for the Top Event:**

$$
T = (C \cdot D) \cdot (A \cdot B) = A \cdot B \cdot C \cdot D
$$

> *Logic explanation (1 mark):* Top event requires **simultaneous** failure of BOTH alarm channels AND BOTH relief valves — a 4-fold AND gate. This is an example of a **single minimal cut set with four basic events**.

**Identification of Minimal Cut Set (1 mark):**

$$K_1 = \lbrace A, B, C, D \rbrace$$

**(b) Quantitative Evaluation (7 marks):**

**Exact calculation** — since the only cut set is $\{A, B, C, D\}$, the top event occurs only when all four basic events fail:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_T &= Q_A \cdot Q_B \cdot Q_C \cdot Q_D \\
    &= (1 \times 10^{-2})(1 \times 10^{-2})(5 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-3}) \\
    &= 2.5 \times 10^{-9}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rare-event approximation** — since all $Q_i \ll 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_T &\approx \sum_{j=1}^{1} \prod_{i \in K_1} Q_i \\
    &\approx Q_A Q_B Q_C Q_D \\
    &\approx 2.5 \times 10^{-9}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion (2 marks):** The exact and approximate values are **identical** because there is only one cut set and all $Q_i < 0.1$. The probability of reactor overpressure is $2.5 \times 10^{-9}$ per demand, which is extremely low — this **defence-in-depth design** (redundant relief valves + redundant alarms) achieves a typical SIL-4 reliability target for a nuclear-grade safety function.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — HAZOP Study

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the HAZOP methodology in detail, including the team composition, study nodes, and the role of each guide word (7 marks).

**(b)** Consider a **centrifugal pump P-101** delivering water from a storage tank T-100 to a process vessel V-200 through a manual isolation valve MV-101 and a control valve FV-100. The design intent is **300 L/min flow ± 10 %**. Apply **FIVE guide words** to the parameter **FLOW**, listing causes, consequences, and at least one safeguard for each deviation (7 marks).

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) HAZOP Methodology (7 marks):**

1. **Origin and Definition (1 mark):** HAZOP was developed by Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI) in the 1960s for the chemical process industry. It is a structured, qualitative brainstorming technique that systematically identifies deviations from design intent.
2. **Team Composition (2 marks):** A HAZOP team typically includes a chairman (independent facilitator), process engineer, control/instrumentation engineer, operations/maintenance staff, and a scribe. The chairman must be neutral and experienced.
3. **Procedure (2 marks):** The plant is divided into **nodes** (e.g., a pipe section, a vessel). For each node, the design intent is documented. Each of the 7 standard guide words (NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN) is then applied to each process parameter (flow, pressure, temperature, level, composition, phase, etc.) to generate deviations. Each deviation is analysed for causes, consequences, and safeguards.
4. **Documentation (1 mark):** Results are recorded in a standard HAZOP worksheet with columns for Node, Deviation, Causes, Consequences, Safeguards, and Recommendations/Actions. The output is the HAZOP Register, which feeds into the facility's Safety Report.
5. **Limitations (1 mark):** HAZOP is qualitative, time-consuming, requires significant expertise, and depends on the team's experience. It does not provide numerical risk values.

**(b) HAZOP Worksheet for Pump P-101 Flow (7 marks):**

**Node:** Centrifugal Pump P-101 discharge line (from pump discharge to control valve FV-100)
**Design Intent:** Water flow of 300 L/min ± 10 % (270–330 L/min) from T-100 to V-200.

| Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NO FLOW** | Pump motor trip; MV-101 closed; FV-100 fails closed; suction tank empty | Process upset, V-200 level low, possible product rejection | Flow indicator FI-100 low alarm; pump discharge pressure switch PSL-100; operator SOP for restart |
| **MORE FLOW** | Pump running at higher speed; FV-100 stuck open; downstream block valve closed causing surge | Pipe vibration, possible flange leak, erosion of valve trim | High-flow alarm FAH-100; pressure relief on V-200 (PSV); mechanical flow restriction orifice plate |
| **LESS FLOW** | Pump impeller wear; partial blockage in suction strainer; FV-100 partially closed; air entrainment in suction | Reduced heat transfer downstream, batch quality off-spec | Low-low alarm FALL-100 trips pump; periodic impeller inspection; suction strainer differential pressure gauge |
| **REVERSE FLOW** | V-200 pressure higher than pump discharge; failure of check valve CV-100; pump stopped with no isolation | Backflow into tank, possible pump reverse rotation damage | Check valve CV-100 on discharge; MV-101 to be closed during maintenance; rotation-direction detector |
| **AS WELL AS** (contaminant in flow) | Corrosion debris from tank; pump seal leak introducing oil; cross-connection to wrong service | Product contamination, downstream filter blockage | Inlet strainer; periodic water analysis; colour-coded piping per ISO 14726 |

*(Allocate marks: 1 mark per correctly completed row × 5 rows = 5 marks; plus 2 marks for correctly identifying Node and Design Intent at the top of the worksheet.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing AND/OR gate symbols.** A flat-bottom gate is AND; a curved-bottom gate is OR. Drawing them reversed is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting the rare-event approximation assumption.** If any $Q_i > 0.1$, the approximation is invalid — students who use it anyway lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Listing guide words without applying them to a specific parameter.** Saying "MORE" alone is not enough — it must be "MORE PRESSURE" or "MORE FLOW". Examiners deduct 1 mark for vague deviations.
> 4. **Confusing FTA (deductive) with FMEA (inductive).** Many students mix up the directions. Remember: FTA starts from the **Top Event** and works **downward**; FMEA starts from a **component** and works **upward**.
> 5. **Not specifying the node in HAZOP answers.** A deviation is meaningless without defining which section of plant is being studied.
> 6. **Omitting safeguards from HAZOP rows.** A complete row must include **causes, consequences, AND safeguards** — missing the last column costs 1 mark per row.
> 7. **Wrong symbol for Basic Event vs Intermediate Event.** Use **circle** for basic, **rectangle** for intermediate. Diamond is for undeveloped events only.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — FTA & HAZOP**

- ✅ **FTA is DEDUCTIVE (top-down); FMEA is INDUCTIVE (bottom-up).** Always state this clearly in answers.
- ✅ **Top Event** = the undesired event being analysed (e.g., "Reactor overpressure").
- ✅ **Basic Event (circle)** = lowest-resolution failure data point.
- ✅ **Intermediate Event (rectangle)** = event caused by a logic gate.
- ✅ **AND gate (flat bottom):** All inputs must occur for output to occur. Output probability = product of input probabilities.
- ✅ **OR gate (curved bottom):** Any input causes the output. Output probability = $1 - \prod(1-Q_i)$.
- ✅ **Minimal Cut Set** = smallest combination of basic events that triggers the Top Event; no proper subset is a cut set.
- ✅ **Rare-event approximation** is valid only when all $Q_i < 0.1$.
- ✅ **HAZOP is QUALITATIVE, TEAM-BASED, and GUIDE-WORD-DRIVEN.**
- ✅ **Seven Guide Words:** NONE, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN.
- ✅ **HAZOP Parameters:** flow, pressure, temperature, level, composition, phase, reaction.
- ✅ **HAZOP Team:** Independent chairman + multidisciplinary experts (process, control, operations, safety, maintenance).
- ✅ **HAZOP Worksheet columns:** Node | Design Intent | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Safeguards | Recommendations.
- ✅ **HAZOP origin:** ICI, UK, 1960s. **FTA origin:** Bell Labs, USA, 1962.
- ✅ **Industry Standard:** HAZOP is performed FIRST during design; FTA is applied LATER on critical scenarios for quantification.
- ✅ **Standards:** IEC 61882 (HAZOP); IEEE 352 / NUREG-0492 (FTA); IEC 61511 (Safety Instrumented Systems arising from HAZOP).
- ✅ **Birnbaum's Importance** identifies the basic event whose reliability improvement would most reduce the top event probability.
- ✅ **Defence-in-depth** (redundancy + diversity) is the primary mitigation strategy revealed by both techniques.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Legislation: Provisions in Factory Act for Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET754 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safety Legislation: Provisions in Factory Act for Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Factories Act, 1948 (as amended in 1987)

The **Factories Act, 1948** (amended in 1987) is the principal **occupational health and safety legislation** in India that governs the working conditions, safety, health, and welfare of workers employed in manufacturing establishments (factories). It is a **comprehensive welfare-cum-safety statute** administered by the **State Governments** through their Factory Inspectorates under the **Directorate General Factory Advice Service & Labour Institutes (DGFASLI)**, which acts as the central advisory body under the **Ministry of Labour and Employment, Government of India**.

The Act consolidates and amends the law relating to the regulation of labour in factories, with the twin objectives of:
1. **Ensuring adequate safety measures** to protect workers from industrial hazards.
2. **Promoting health and welfare** of workers employed in factories.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Factories Act considered the "Magna Carta" of Indian Industrial Labour?**
> Because it was the **first major piece of labour legislation** in independent India, establishing a **uniform minimum standard** of safety, health, and welfare applicable to all factories across the country. It introduced statutory duties for occupiers, manufacturers, and workers — backed by criminal penalties — and laid the foundation for the entire **OSHE (Occupational Safety, Health & Environment)** regulatory framework in India.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "The Factory as a City"**
> Think of a factory as a small, self-contained city where thousands of workers spend 8–10 hours daily. Just as a city has **traffic rules, building codes, fire departments, hospitals, and sanitation systems** to protect its citizens, the Factories Act is the **"rule book of the industrial city."**
> - The **State Government** acts as the city administration.
> - The **Factory Inspector** acts as the **traffic police / municipal auditor**.
> - The **Occupier** is the **Mayor** (responsible for the well-being of all inhabitants).
> - The **Worker** is the **citizen**, with both **rights and duties**.
> - The **Safety Committee** is the **city council** that audits safety infrastructure.

The Act tells the Mayor (Occupier): *"You MUST build safe roads (fencing, floors), install fire hydrants (fire extinguishers), provide clean air (ventilation), limit load on bridges (lifting machinery), and protect citizens with helmets (PPE). If you don't, the traffic police (Inspector) will prosecute you."*

## 1.3 Scope and Applicability

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition of a "Factory" (Section 2(m)):**
> Any premises (including the precincts thereof) wherein **10 or more workers** are employed (or were employed on any day of the preceding 12 months), AND in which a **manufacturing process is being carried on** with the aid of power; OR wherein **20 or more workers** are employed without the aid of power.

| Criterion | With Power | Without Power |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum workers to qualify as factory | **10 or more** | **20 or more** |
| Minimum workers for Chapter V (Welfare) | **20 or more** | **20 or more** |
| Safety Committee (Section 41G) | **100 or more** (or as per State rule) | Same |

## 1.4 Key Definitions (Section 2)

- **Occupier (Section 2(n))**: The person who has **ultimate control over the affairs of the factory** — includes the managing agent, the agent of the owner, but NOT a person acting merely as a tenant or sub-tenant.
- **Worker (Section 2(l))**: A person employed **directly or through any agency** (contract labour included) for any manufacturing process, cleaning, lubricating, security, etc.
- **Manufacturing Process (Section 2(k))**: Any process for — making, altering, repairing, ornamenting, finishing, packing, oiling, washing, cleaning, breaking up, demolishing, or otherwise treating/adaptation of any article/substance — with a view to its use, sale, transport, delivery, or disposal.
- **Hazardous Process (Section 2(cb))**: Any process carried out in a factory that, by its nature, can cause bodily injury, illness, or death — listed in **Schedule I** (e.g., manufacture of explosives, asbestos, benzene, lead, pesticides, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Section 2B (Inserted in 1987 amendment):** The **appropriate Government** (State, in most cases) can declare even establishments employing fewer than 10/20 workers as a factory if the manufacturing process poses a serious hazard to health and safety.

---

## 1.5 Chapter-Wise Snapshot of the Act

The Factories Act, 1948 contains **13 Chapters** and **Schedules I–V**. Safety aspects primarily fall under **Chapter III (Sections 6–25)** and **Chapter IV (Sections 26–42) – Health, Welfare, Special Provisions, and Working Hours**.

| Chapter | Sections | Subject | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | 1–2 | Preliminary (definitions) | Foundational |
| II | 3–5 | The Inspecting Staff | Low |
| **III** | **6–25** | **Health and Safety Provisions** | **HIGH** ⭐ |
| IV | 26–41 | Health (ventilation, lighting, sanitation) | Medium |
| IV-A | 41A–41H | **Hazardous Processes** | High ⭐ |
| V | 42–50 | Welfare (drinking water, canteen, creche) | Medium |
| VI | 51–66 | Working Hours | Low |
| VII | 67–77 | Employment of Young Persons | Low |
| VIII | 78–84 | Annual Leave with Wages | Low |
| IX | 85–91 | Special Provisions (State Govt powers) | Low |
| X | 92–106 | Penalties | Medium |
| XI | 107–120 | Miscellaneous | Low |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Provisions Matrix

## 2.1 Chapter III — The Safety Spine: Sections 6 to 25

This Chapter is the **operational core of the Act** from a safety engineering standpoint. Each section imposes a **mandatory duty** on the Occupier, with corresponding **penalties under Section 92–94** for non-compliance. Below is the **exhaustive analytical breakdown**.

---

### 2.2 Section 6 — Fencing of Machinery

> **Statutory Mandate:** Every dangerous part of any machinery (prime movers, transmission machinery, other dangerous parts) must be **securely fenced** unless it is in such position or construction as to be safe to every person employed.

- **Prime Movers** (steam engines, internal combustion engines, motors) — every flywheel, crank, cam, belt pulley, connecting rod must be fenced.
- **Transmission Machinery** — shafts, belts, chains, gears, pulleys, couplings must be fenced.
- **Other Dangerous Parts** — any part that can cause injury (projecting bolts, set screws, sharp edges).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The duty is **absolute** — it does not depend on whether injury has actually occurred. The occupier must **anticipate** danger and fence accordingly. Courts have held that "safe position or safe construction" is a **high threshold** that the occupier must prove.

---

### 2.3 Section 7 — Work on or Near Machinery in Motion
